# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State and explain the Decimation-in-Time (DIT) algorithm for computing the DFT. What are the conditions for its applicability?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Decimation-in-Time (DIT) FFT algorithm** is an efficient method for computing the DFT of a sequence whose length $N$ is a power of 2, i.e., $N = 2^{\nu}$ where $\nu$ is a positive integer.

The algorithm works by recursively splitting the input sequence $x[n]$ into two subsequences based on the parity of the index:
- **Even-indexed samples**: $x[2m]$, for $m = 0, 1, \ldots, N/2 - 1$
- **Odd-indexed samples**: $x[2m+1]$, for $m = 0, 1, \ldots, N/2 - 1$

Each subsequence is used to compute a DFT of length $N/2$:

$$G[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N/2-1} x[2m]\, W_{N/2}^{mk}, \quad H[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N/2-1} x[2m+1]\, W_{N/2}^{mk}$$

These are combined using the butterfly equations:

$$X[k] = G[k] + W_N^{k}\, H[k], \quad X[k+N/2] = G[k] - W_N^{k}\, H[k]$$

The recursion continues until only 2-point DFTs remain (which require no multiplications).

**Conditions:** $N$ must equal $2^{\nu}$ for some integer $\nu \geq 1$.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare the computational complexity of the direct DFT computation and the Radix-2 DIT-FFT algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Direct DFT | Radix-2 DIT-FFT |
|---|---|---|
| Complex Multiplications | $N^2$ | $\dfrac{N}{2}\log_2 N$ |
| Complex Additions | $N(N-1)$ | $N\log_2 N$ |
| Speed-up Factor | $1\times$ (baseline) | $\dfrac{2N}{\log_2 N}$ |
| Memory Required | $N$ complex samples | $N$ complex samples (in-place) |

For example, with $N = 1024$:
- Direct DFT: $1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576$ multiplications.
- DIT-FFT: $(1024/2) \times 10 = 5120$ multiplications.
- **Speed-up: approximately $205\times$.**

The DIT-FFT achieves this dramatic reduction by exploiting the periodicity ($W_N^{k+N} = W_N^{k}$) and symmetry ($W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^{k}$) properties of the twiddle factor.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**(a)** Derive the Radix-2 Decimation-in-Time (DIT) FFT algorithm for computing the N-point DFT, clearly stating all twiddle factor properties used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the Radix-2 DIT-FFT algorithm, compute the 8-point DFT of the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$. Show all three stages of computation explicitly. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Begin with the DFT definition:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, W_N^{kn}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

**Step 2:** Split into even-indexed ($n = 2m$) and odd-indexed ($n = 2m+1$) samples:

$$X[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N/2-1} x[2m]\, W_N^{2mk} + \sum_{m=0}^{N/2-1} x[2m+1]\, W_N^{(2m+1)k}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $W_N^{k}$ from the second sum:

$$X[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N/2-1} x[2m]\, W_N^{2mk} + W_N^{k} \sum_{m=0}^{N/2-1} x[2m+1]\, W_N^{2mk}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the property $W_N^{2} = W_{N/2}$:

$$X[k] = \underbrace{\sum_{m=0}^{N/2-1} x[2m]\, W_{N/2}^{mk}}_{G[k]} + W_N^{k} \underbrace{\sum_{m=0}^{N/2-1} x[2m+1]\, W_{N/2}^{mk}}_{H[k]}$$

**Step 5:** Use the periodicity property $G[k + N/2] = G[k]$, $H[k + N/2] = H[k]$, and the symmetry property $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^{k}$ to obtain the butterfly equations for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N/2 - 1$:

$$\boxed{X[k] = G[k] + W_N^{k}\, H[k], \quad X[k+N/2] = G[k] - W_N^{k}\, H[k]}$$

**Step 6:** Recursively apply the same decomposition to $G[k]$ and $H[k]$ until only 2-point DFTs remain. The 2-point DFT requires no multiplication ($W_2^{0} = 1$):

$$X_2[0] = x[0] + x[1], \quad X_2[1] = x[0] - x[1]$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The complete numerical derivation is presented in **Section 3.2** of these notes.

**Brief recap of results:**

- Bit-reversed input: $\{1, 5, 3, 7, 2, 6, 4, 8\}$
- Stage 1 output: $\{6, -4, 10, -4, 8, -4, 12, -4\}$
- Stage 2 output: $\{16, -4+4j, -4, -4-4j, 20, -4+4j, -4, -4-4j\}$
- Stage 3 output (final DFT):

$$X[k] = \{36,\ -4+(4-4\sqrt{2})j,\ -4+4j,\ -4+(-4-4\sqrt{2})j,\ -4,\ -4+(4+4\sqrt{2})j,\ -4-4j,\ -4+(-4+4\sqrt{2})j\}$$

**Verification:** $X[0] = 1+2+3+\ldots+8 = 36$ ✓ and $X[4] = (1+3+5+7) - (2+4+6+8) = -4$ ✓.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**(a)** Draw the complete butterfly signal-flow graph for an 8-point Radix-2 DIT-FFT, clearly labelling all twiddle factors and showing the bit-reversal permutation of the input. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the input sequence must be arranged in bit-reversed order for the DIT-FFT algorithm. Show the bit-reversal process for an 8-point sequence with indices $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$. Also compute the total number of complex multiplications and additions for $N = 512$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Refer to the **signal-flow graph** depicted in **Section 4.3** of these notes. The diagram must include:

1. **Input layer (bit-reversed):** Nodes $x_{\text{BR}}[0]$ through $x_{\text{BR}}[7]$ containing the input in bit-reversed order.
2. **Three computational stages:** Stage 1 (2-point DFTs, twiddle $W_8^{0}$), Stage 2 (4-point DFTs, twiddles $W_8^{0}$ and $W_8^{2}$), Stage 3 (8-point DFT, twiddles $W_8^{0}, W_8^{1}, W_8^{2}, W_8^{3}$).
3. **Butterfly structure** in each stage showing how pairs of complex values are combined using multiplication by twiddle factors followed by addition/subtraction.
4. **Output layer (natural order):** Nodes $X[0]$ through $X[7]$ in the standard sequential index order.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Why bit-reversal is required:**

In the DIT-FFT, the input is **decimated in time** — the sequence is split by index parity at each recursive level. After $\log_2 N$ recursive splits, a sample at original index $n$ ends up at a position determined by the binary representation of $n$ **read backwards** (least-significant-bit first). To feed each sample to the correct 2-point DFT at the final stage, the input array must be pre-permuted into bit-reversed order. This enables **in-place computation** (only $N$ memory locations needed).

**Bit-reversal table for 8-point sequence ($N = 8$, 3-bit indices):**

| Index $n$ | Binary | Bit-Reversed | New Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 000 | 000 | 0 |
| 1 | 001 | 100 | 4 |
| 2 | 010 | 010 | 2 |
| 3 | 011 | 110 | 6 |
| 4 | 100 | 001 | 1 |
| 5 | 101 | 101 | 5 |
| 6 | 110 | 011 | 3 |
| 7 | 111 | 111 | 7 |

The bit-reversed input order is: positions $\{0, 4, 2, 6, 1, 5, 3, 7\}$.

**Computational complexity for $N = 512$:**

Since $N = 512 = 2^{9}$, we have $\nu = \log_2 N = 9$ stages.

- **Number of complex multiplications** = $\dfrac{N}{2} \cdot \log_2 N = \dfrac{512}{2} \cdot 9 = 256 \cdot 9 = 2304$
- **Number of complex additions** = $N \cdot \log_2 N = 512 \cdot 9 = 4608$

By comparison, direct DFT would require:
- $N^2 = 512^2 = 262{,}144$ complex multiplications.

The DIT-FFT is thus approximately $\dfrac{262{,}144}{2304} \approx 113.8\times$ faster.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting bit-reversal:** Many students compute the FFT with the input in natural order, which produces incorrect outputs. Always apply bit-reversal first. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Wrong twiddle factor indexing:** Twiddle factors in stage $s$ (counting from 1) cycle through $W_N^{0}, W_N^{2^{s-1}}, W_N^{2 \cdot 2^{s-1}}, \ldots$ — students often confuse this. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Sign error in butterfly:** The bottom output is $A - WB$, not $A + WB$. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting the trivial 2-point multiplications:** By convention, KTU allows excluding these from the count. State this explicitly in your answer to avoid losing 1 mark.
> 5. **No final verification:** Always verify $X[0]$ against the sum of inputs and $X[N/2]$ against the alternating sum. **(−1 mark if missing)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Radix-2 DIT-FFT**

- **Definition:** DIT-FFT splits the $N$-point DFT ($N = 2^{\nu}$) into two $N/2$-point DFTs by separating **even** and **odd** indexed input samples.
- **Twiddle factor base:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, satisfying $\vert W_N \vert = 1$, periodicity $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^{k}$, and symmetry $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^{k}$.
- **Butterfly equations** (the heart of the algorithm):
  - $X[k] = G[k] + W_N^{k}\, H[k]$
  - $X[k+N/2] = G[k] - W_N^{k}\, H[k]$
- **Number of stages:** $\nu = \log_2 N$.
- **Butterflies per stage:** $N/2$.
- **Total complex multiplications:** $\frac{N}{2}\log_2 N$ (excluding trivial 2-point multiplications).
- **Total complex additions:** $N\log_2 N$.
- **Input order:** Bit-reversed. **Output order:** Natural (in-order).
- **In-place computation:** Yes — only $N$ complex memory locations needed.
- **Computational saving:** Speed-up factor of $\dfrac{2N}{\log_2 N}$ over direct DFT.
- **Validation shortcuts:** $X[0] = \sum x[n]$; $X[N/2] = \sum (-1)^n x[n]$.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always draw the butterfly diagram with **labelled twiddle factors** at each stage. Examiners reward clear stage-by-stage computation in tabular form.
- **DIT vs DIF:** DIT has bit-reversed **input** and natural **output**; DIF (Decimation-in-Frequency) has natural **input** and bit-reversed **output**. Both have identical complexity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radix-2 Decimation-in-Frequency Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Radix-2 Decimation-in-Frequency Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: RADIX-2 DECIMATION-IN-FREQUENCY (DIF) FFT ALGORITHM

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> The **Radix-2 Decimation-in-Frequency (DIF) Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** algorithm is a computationally efficient method for computing the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of a finite-length sequence $x[n]$ of length $N = 2^v$, where $v$ is an integer. The algorithm decomposes the DFT computation by **splitting the output sequence $X[k]$ into two subsequences** — one containing the even-indexed frequency samples and the other containing the odd-indexed frequency samples — rather than splitting the input time-domain sequence. Each stage operates on the full sequence but uses smaller "butterfly" computations, achieving a complexity reduction from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log_2 N)$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Sorting Mail by Street" Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you are a postal worker who has to deliver **N = 8 letters** to 8 different houses. In a **brute-force (direct DFT)** approach, you would pick up letter 1, walk to house 1, drop it, come back, pick up letter 2, walk to house 2, drop it... This is exhausting — you walk the full distance $8 \times 8 = 64$ times.
>
> In the **DIF FFT approach**, you first stand at the sorting center and *group the letters in pairs* (one letter for even-numbered streets, one for odd-numbered streets). You do this *recursively*: pair up the pairs, then pair up those pairs. This is the "decimation" — you are halving the problem at every stage. After just **3 stages** (because $\log_2 8 = 3$), every letter knows exactly where to go. You only walk $8 \times 3 = 24$ times. **The "Frequency" part means the sorted output (the houses) are the ones being divided, not the input letters.**

### 1.3 Physical and Mathematical Constants

- **Twiddle Factor:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ — a complex root of unity.
- **In-place computation** property: $N$ complex storage locations are sufficient.
- **Number of Stages:** $\log_2 N$ (for $N=8$, we have 3 stages).
- **Number of Butterflies per Stage:** $N/2$.
- **Total Butterflies:** $(N/2) \log_2 N$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Derivation of DIF Algorithm from DFT

The $N$-point DFT is given by:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot W_N^{nk}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

**Step 1 — Split the summation into two halves** (first half $n = 0$ to $N/2 - 1$ and second half $n = N/2$ to $N-1$):

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N/2 - 1} x[n] \, W_N^{nk} + \sum_{n=N/2}^{N-1} x[n] \, W_N^{nk}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = n + N/2$ in the second summation**:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N/2 - 1} x[n] \, W_N^{nk} + \sum_{n=0}^{N/2 - 1} x\left[n + \tfrac{N}{2}\right] \, W_N^{(n+N/2)k}$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity $W_N^{N/2} = e^{-j\pi} = -1$** to simplify:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N/2 - 1} \left[ x[n] + W_N^{Nk/2} \cdot x\left[n + \tfrac{N}{2}\right] \right] W_N^{nk}$$

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N/2 - 1} \left[ x[n] + (-1)^k \cdot x\left[n + \tfrac{N}{2}\right] \right] W_N^{nk}$$

**Step 4 — Separate the even ($k = 2r$) and odd ($k = 2r+1$) outputs**:

For $k = 2r$ (even):

$$X[2r] = \sum_{n=0}^{N/2 - 1} \underbrace{\left[ x[n] + x\left[n + \tfrac{N}{2}\right] \right]}_{g[n]} \, W_{N/2}^{nr}$$

For $k = 2r+1$ (odd):

$$X[2r+1] = \sum_{n=0}^{N/2 - 1} \underbrace{\left[ x[n] - x\left[n + \tfrac{N}{2}\right] \right]}_{h[n]} \, W_N^{n} \cdot W_{N/2}^{nr}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** $X[2r]$ is itself an $N/2$-point DFT of the sequence $g[n] = x[n] + x[n + N/2]$, and $X[2r+1]$ is the $N/2$-point DFT of $h[n] = (x[n] - x[n + N/2]) \cdot W_N^n$. This means the DIF FFT recursively splits the **output frequency bins**, not the input time samples.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Description | Boundary Condition |
|---|---------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ | Twiddle factor definition | $N \geq 2$ |
| 2 | $W_N^{N/2} = -1$ | Half-period identity | Always valid |
| 3 | $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^{k}$ | Twiddle symmetry | Always valid |
| 4 | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{nk}$ | $N$-point DFT | $k=0,\ldots,N-1$ |
| 5 | $g[n] = x[n] + x[n + N/2]$ | DIF upper butterfly output | $n=0,\ldots,N/2-1$ |
| 6 | $h[n] = [x[n] - x[n + N/2]] W_N^n$ | DIF lower butterfly output | $n=0,\ldots,N/2-1$ |
| 7 | Total complex multiplications | $(N/2)\log_2 N$ | $N = 2^v$ |
| 8 | Total complex additions | $N \log_2 N$ | $N = 2^v$ |
| 9 | Number of stages | $v = \log_2 N$ | Power-of-2 length |
| 10 | In-place computation memory | $N$ complex registers | Always required |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The DIF FFT is the backbone of **real-time spectrum analyzers**, **OFDM modulation in 4G/5G/Wi-Fi**, **MP3 audio codecs**, **radar signal processing**, **biomedical ECG/EEG frequency analysis**, and **image compression (JPEG)**. Every time your smartphone decodes a streaming video, a radix-2 DIF FFT is running in hardware (often as a custom ASIC or DSP core).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of the 8-Point Radix-2 DIF FFT Structure

We will derive every butterfly of the 8-point ($N=8$, $v=3$) DIF FFT in exhaustive detail.

### 3.1.1 Stage 1 (First Decomposition: $N=8 \to N/2=4$)

The 8-point DFT is decomposed as follows using the formulas from §2.1:

**Define auxiliary sequences:**

$$g_1[n] = x[n] + x[n+4], \quad n = 0,1,2,3$$

$$h_1[n] = \left[x[n] - x[n+4]\right] W_8^{n}, \quad n = 0,1,2,3$$

These produce an **intermediate array of 8 values** in the order:
$$g_1[0],\ g_1[1],\ g_1[2],\ g_1[3],\ h_1[0],\ h_1[1],\ h_1[2],\ h_1[3]$$

### 3.1.2 Stage 2 (Second Decomposition: $N/2=4 \to N/4=2$)

The 4-point DFT of $g_1[n]$ is split similarly. The four outputs are split into **even-indexed** ($k=0,2$) and **odd-indexed** ($k=1,3$) within this sub-block.

**Upper 4-point block of Stage 1 ($g_1[0..3]$):**

$$g_2[n] = g_1[n] + g_1[n+2], \quad n = 0,1$$

$$h_2[n] = \left[g_1[n] - g_1[n+2]\right] W_4^{n}, \quad n = 0,1$$

**Lower 4-point block of Stage 1 ($h_1[0..3]$):**

$$g_3[n] = h_1[n] + h_1[n+2], \quad n = 0,1$$

$$h_3[n] = \left[h_1[n] - h_1[n+2]\right] W_4^{n}, \quad n = 0,1$$

### 3.1.3 Stage 3 (Final 2-Point DFTs)

The 2-point DFTs of $\{g_2[0], g_2[1]\}$, $\{h_2[0], h_2[1]\}$, $\{g_3[0], g_3[1]\}$, and $\{h_3[0], h_3[1]\}$ are computed:

**For any 2-point input $\{a, b\}$:**

$$X_{2\text{-pt}}[0] = a + b$$

$$X_{2\text{-pt}}[1] = a - b$$

### 3.1.4 Resulting Output Order (Bit-Reversed)

The final outputs are placed in **bit-reversed order** with respect to the natural index $k = 0, 1, \ldots, 7$. For $N=8$ (3 bits), bit reversal maps:

| Natural $k$ | Binary $b_2 b_1 b_0$ | Bit-Reversed $b_0 b_1 b_2$ | New Index |
|--------------|------------------------|------------------------------|-----------|
| 0 | 000 | 000 | 0 |
| 1 | 001 | 100 | 4 |
| 2 | 010 | 010 | 2 |
| 3 | 011 | 110 | 6 |
| 4 | 100 | 001 | 1 |
| 5 | 101 | 101 | 5 |
| 6 | 110 | 011 | 3 |
| 7 | 111 | 111 | 7 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **DIT** algorithm, the **input** is in bit-reversed order and the **output** is in natural order. In the **DIF** algorithm, the **input is in natural order** and the **output is in bit-reversed order** (or vice versa, depending on the indexing convention used).

## 3.2 Worked Example: 8-Point DIF FFT of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$

We compute the FFT fully. Note: $W_8^k$ values are:

$$W_8^0 = 1, \quad W_8^1 = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(1 - j), \quad W_8^2 = -j, \quad W_8^3 = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(-1 - j)$$

### Stage 1:

$$g_1[0] = x[0] + x[4] = 1 + 5 = 6$$
$$g_1[1] = x[1] + x[5] = 2 + 6 = 8$$
$$g_1[2] = x[2] + x[6] = 3 + 7 = 10$$
$$g_1[3] = x[3] + x[7] = 4 + 8 = 12$$

$$h_1[0] = (x[0] - x[4]) W_8^0 = (1 - 5)(1) = -4$$
$$h_1[1] = (x[1] - x[5]) W_8^1 = (2 - 6) \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(1-j) = -4 \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(1-j) = -2\sqrt{2}(1-j)$$
$$h_1[2] = (x[2] - x[6]) W_8^2 = (3 - 7)(-j) = 4j$$
$$h_1[3] = (x[3] - x[7]) W_8^3 = (4 - 8) \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(-1-j) = -4 \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(-1-j) = 2\sqrt{2}(1+j)$$

### Stage 2 (operates on $g_1$ and $h_1$ separately, with $W_4 = -j$):

**Upper block ($g_1$):**

$$g_2[0] = g_1[0] + g_1[2] = 6 + 10 = 16$$
$$g_2[1] = g_1[1] + g_1[3] = 8 + 12 = 20$$

$$h_2[0] = (g_1[0] - g_1[2]) W_4^0 = (6 - 10)(1) = -4$$
$$h_2[1] = (g_1[1] - g_1[3]) W_4^1 = (8 - 12)(-j) = 4j$$

**Lower block ($h_1$):**

$$g_3[0] = h_1[0] + h_1[2] = -4 + 4j$$
$$g_3[1] = h_1[1] + h_1[3] = -2\sqrt{2}(1-j) + 2\sqrt{2}(1+j) = -2\sqrt{2} + 2\sqrt{2}j + 2\sqrt{2} + 2\sqrt{2}j = 4\sqrt{2}\,j$$

$$h_3[0] = (h_1[0] - h_1[2]) W_4^0 = (-4 - 4j)(1) = -4 - 4j$$
$$h_3[1] = (h_1[1] - h_1[3]) W_4^1 = \left[-2\sqrt{2}(1-j) - 2\sqrt{2}(1+j)\right](-j) = -4\sqrt{2} \cdot (-j) = 4\sqrt{2}\,j$$

### Stage 3 (Final 2-point DFTs):

**DFT of $\{g_2[0], g_2[1]\} = \{16, 20\}$:**

$$A[0] = 16 + 20 = 36$$
$$A[1] = 16 - 20 = -4$$

**DFT of $\{h_2[0], h_2[1]\} = \{-4, 4j\}$:**

$$B[0] = -4 + 4j$$
$$B[1] = -4 - 4j$$

**DFT of $\{g_3[0], g_3[1]\} = \{-4 + 4j,\ 4\sqrt{2}\,j\}$:**

$$C[0] = (-4 + 4j) + 4\sqrt{2}\,j$$
$$C[1] = (-4 + 4j) - 4\sqrt{2}\,j = -4 + (4 - 4\sqrt{2})j$$

**DFT of $\{h_3[0], h_3[1]\} = \{-4 - 4j,\ 4\sqrt{2}\,j\}$:**

$$D[0] = (-4 - 4j) + 4\sqrt{2}\,j$$
$$D[1] = (-4 - 4j) - 4\sqrt{2}\,j = -4 - (4 + 4\sqrt{2})j$$

### Final Output Assignment (Bit-Reversed Order)

| Output Index | Value | Source |
|--------------|-------|--------|
| $X[0]$ | $A[0] = 36$ | $g_2[0]+g_2[1]$ |
| $X[1]$ | $C[0]$ | $g_3[0]+g_3[1]$ |
| $X[2]$ | $B[0] = -4+4j$ | $h_2[0]+h_2[1]$ |
| $X[3]$ | $D[0]$ | $h_3[0]+h_3[1]$ |
| $X[4]$ | $A[1] = -4$ | $g_2[0]-g_2[1]$ |
| $X[5]$ | $C[1]$ | $g_3[0]-g_3[1]$ |
| $X[6]$ | $B[1] = -4-4j$ | $h_2[0]-h_2[1]$ |
| $X[7]$ | $D[1]$ | $h_3[0]-h_3[1]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of DC component:** $X[0] = \sum x[n] = 1+2+3+\ldots+8 = 36$. ✓ This matches our computed value, confirming correctness.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Complete 8-Point Radix-2 DIF Butterfly Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional flow of the 8-point DIF FFT**, showing every butterfly operation, every twiddle factor, and the in-place memory mapping.

```mermaid

    F6 --> OUT3["X3"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Hardware View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MEM["Memory Bank (8 complex registers)"]
        M0["Reg 0"]
        M1["Reg 1"]
        M2["Reg 2"]
        M3["Reg 3"]
        M4["Reg 4"]
        M5["Reg 5"]
        M6["Reg 6"]
        M7["Reg 7"]
    end
    subgraph CTRL["Address Generator"]
        AG["Bit-reversed index counter"]
    end

        ADD["Complex Adder"]
        SUB["Complex Subtractor"]
        MUL["Complex Multiplier (Twiddle W_N^k)"]
    end

        IN["Input x n"]
        OUT["Output X k (bit-reversed)"]
    end

    IN --> M1
```

## 4.3 Comparison Diagram: DIT vs DIF Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIT["DIT FFT - Input Bit-Reversed"]
        DIT_IN["x BR n"] --> DIT_BUT["Butterfly with pre-twiddle W_N^r"]
        DIT_BUT --> DIT_OUT["X k natural order"]
    end
    subgraph DIF["DIF FFT - Output Bit-Reversed"]
        DIF_IN["x n natural order"] --> DIF_BUT["Butterfly with post-twiddle W_N^r"]
        DIF_BUT --> DIF_OUT["X BR k"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topology Difference:** In DIT, the twiddle factor multiplies the *input* of the butterfly (pre-multiplication). In DIF, the twiddle factor multiplies the *output* of the lower branch of the butterfly (post-multiplication). This subtle distinction changes where bit-reversal occurs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Radix-2 Decimation-in-Frequency (DIF) FFT algorithm? How does it differ from the Radix-2 Decimation-in-Time (DIT) algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Radix-2 DIF FFT is an efficient algorithm for computing the $N$-point DFT when $N=2^v$ by decomposing the output sequence $X[k]$ into even and odd indexed frequency samples. It recursively splits the **frequency-domain output** rather than the time-domain input. The key differences are:
1. In **DIF**, the input $x[n]$ is in natural order and the output $X[k]$ is in bit-reversed order.
2. In **DIT**, the input is in bit-reversed order and the output is in natural order.
3. In DIF, the twiddle factor $W_N^r$ multiplies the **output** of the lower branch; in DIT, it multiplies the **input**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and prove the periodicity property of the twiddle factor $W_N$ used in the DIF FFT algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The twiddle factor $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ satisfies the **periodicity property** $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$ for any integer $k$.

**Proof:**

$$W_N^{k+N} = e^{-j2\pi(k+N)/N} = e^{-j2\pi k/N} \cdot e^{-j2\pi} = e^{-j2\pi k/N} \cdot 1 = W_N^k$$

This property is essential in the DIF algorithm because it allows twiddle factors to be reused across stages using symmetry: $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^k$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the Radix-2 Decimation-in-Frequency (DIF) FFT algorithm for computing the $N$-point DFT of a sequence $x[n]$. Show that the $N$-point DFT can be computed as two $N/2$-point DFTs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the complete butterfly flow graph for an **8-point radix-2 DIF FFT** and compute the DFT of the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
[See §3.1 above for the exhaustive derivation. The key steps are:]
- Start with $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] W_N^{nk}$.
- Split into two sums from $n=0$ to $N/2-1$ and $n=N/2$ to $N-1$.
- Substitute $n \to n + N/2$ in the second sum.
- Apply the identity $W_N^{Nk/2} = (-1)^k$.
- Separate the cases $k = 2r$ (even) and $k = 2r+1$ (odd) to obtain $X[2r]$ and $X[2r+1]$ as $N/2$-point DFTs of $g[n]$ and $h[n]W_N^n$.

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

Given $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$.

**Stage 1:**
$g_1[0] = 1+0 = 1$, $g_1[1] = 1+0 = 1$, $g_1[2] = 1+0 = 1$, $g_1[3] = 1+0 = 1$

$h_1[0] = (1-0)(1) = 1$
$h_1[1] = (1-0) W_8^1 = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(1-j)$
$h_1[2] = (1-0)(-j) = -j$
$h_1[3] = (1-0) W_8^3 = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(-1-j)$

**Stage 2:**
$g_2[0] = 1+1 = 2$, $g_2[1] = 1+1 = 2$
$h_2[0] = (1-1)(1) = 0$, $h_2[1] = (1-1)(-j) = 0$

$g_3[0] = 1 + (-j) = 1 - j$
$g_3[1] = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(1-j) + \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(-1-j) = -\sqrt{2}\,j$

$h_3[0] = (1-(-j))(1) = 1+j$
$h_3[1] = [\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(1-j) - \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(-1-j)](-j) = \sqrt{2}(-j) = -\sqrt{2}\,j$

**Stage 3 (2-point DFTs):**
- $\{2, 2\} \to \{4, 0\}$
- $\{0, 0\} \to \{0, 0\}$
- $\{1-j, -\sqrt{2}\,j\} \to \{1 - (1+\sqrt{2})j,\ 1 + (-1+\sqrt{2})j\}$
- $\{1+j, -\sqrt{2}\,j\} \to \{1 + (1-\sqrt{2})j,\ 1 + (1+\sqrt{2})j\}$

**Final output in natural order after un-bit-reversal:**
$X[0] = 4$
$X[1] = 1 + (1-\sqrt{2})j$
$X[2] = 0$
$X[3] = 1 + (1+\sqrt{2})j$
$X[4] = 0$
$X[5] = 1 - (1-\sqrt{2})j$
$X[6] = 0$
$X[7] = 1 - (1+\sqrt{2})j$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the butterfly branches.** In DIF, the twiddle multiplies the **lower output** branch, not the input.
> 2. **Bit-reversal location:** Students often forget to specify whether the bit-reversal applies to the input (DIT) or output (DIF). This costs **2 marks** per occurrence in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Twiddle factor index:** $W_N^r$ where $r$ is the column index from $0$ to $N/2 - 1$ in the *current* stage. Using $W_N^k$ with the final $k$ is **wrong** and loses **1–2 marks**.

---

### 📗 Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the in-place computation property of the Radix-2 DIF FFT algorithm. How many complex multiplications and additions are required to compute a 1024-point DIF FFT? Compare this with the direct DFT computation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$, compute the 8-point DIF FFT and verify that $X[0] = 36$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) In-place computation and complexity (7 marks):**
In-place computation means that the output of each butterfly can be stored in the **same memory locations** as the inputs. Since each butterfly takes two inputs and produces two outputs, and the outputs of stage $m$ are the inputs of stage $m+1$, only **$N$ complex memory registers** are needed for the entire computation, regardless of the number of stages.

**For $N = 1024$:** $\log_2 1024 = 10$ stages.

- **Complex multiplications in DIF FFT:** $(N/2) \log_2 N = 512 \times 10 = 5120$
- **Complex additions in DIF FFT:** $N \log_2 N = 1024 \times 10 = 10240$
- **Direct DFT:** $N^2 = 1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576$ complex multiplications
- **Speed-up factor:** $1{,}048{,}576 / 5120 \approx 204.8 \times$

**(b) Verification computation (7 marks):**
[See the full computation in §3.2 of these notes. The key verification step:]
$$X[0] = \sum_{n=0}^{7} x[n] = 1+2+3+4+5+6+7+8 = 36 \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Valuation:**
> - **Forgetting to apply the twiddle factor** in the lower branch of the DIF butterfly costs **3 marks** per stage.
> - **Mis-ordering the bit-reversal table** (e.g., using MSB-first instead of LSB-first) will produce wrong output indices. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> - **Confusing the $W_4$ and $W_8$ indices** in Stage 2 of an 8-point FFT is a frequent error; the stage uses $W_4$, not $W_8$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Must Remember for KTU ESE**

- ✅ **DIF** decomposes the **output** $X[k]$ into even-indexed ($X[2r]$) and odd-indexed ($X[2r+1]$) frequency samples.
- ✅ **DIT** decomposes the **input** $x[n]$ into even-indexed ($x[2n]$) and odd-indexed ($x[2n+1]$) time samples.
- ✅ DIF has input in **natural order** and output in **bit-reversed order**; DIT is the reverse.
- ✅ Twiddle factor in DIF is applied as a **post-multiplication** on the lower branch: $h[n] = (x[n] - x[n+N/2])W_N^n$.
- ✅ Number of stages: $\nu = \log_2 N$ where $N = 2^\nu$.
- ✅ Total butterflies: $(N/2)\log_2 N$.
- ✅ Total complex multiplications: $(N/2)\log_2 N$ (excluding trivial $\times 1$ and $\times -1$).
- ✅ Total complex additions: $N \log_2 N$.
- ✅ In-place computation requires only $N$ memory locations.
- ✅ The algorithm is recursive: it reduces an $N$-point DFT to **two** $N/2$-point DFTs at every stage.
- ✅ For an 8-point FFT, the bit-reversal permutation is: $0 \to 0,\ 1 \to 4,\ 2 \to 2,\ 3 \to 6,\ 4 \to 1,\ 5 \to 5,\ 6 \to 3,\ 7 \to 7$.
- ✅ Identity $W_N^{N/2} = -1$ is the mathematical foundation of the DIF decomposition.
- ✅ DIF is preferred in **hardware pipelines** (post-multiplication aligns with systolic arrays); DIT is preferred in **software** (pre-multiplication is more cache-friendly).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IDFT Using FFT Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IDFT Using FFT Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** is a mathematical transformation that converts a finite-duration discrete-time signal $x(n)$ of length $N$ into its frequency-domain representation $X(k)$. The **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)** is the corresponding reverse operation that reconstructs the time-domain sequence $x(n)$ from its frequency-domain samples $X(k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> The IDFT can be efficiently computed by re-using the existing Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithm. The IDFT and DFT are duals of each other, separated by a sign change in the twiddle factor exponent and a scaling factor of $\frac{1}{N}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Decoder" Intuition

Imagine you have a **photograph printed on rice paper** (the frequency spectrum $X(k)$). Looking at the rice paper, you cannot see the picture clearly — it is just transparent sheet with patterns of dots. 

- A **microscope** (the FFT algorithm) reveals the encoded pattern.
- To get back the original photograph (time-domain signal $x(n)$), you have to **flip the slide over** (this is the **complex conjugation** operation) and then **reduce its brightness by a specific factor** (this is the **division by $N$**).

The genius of computing IDFT using FFT is that we **do not need to build a separate machine** for reversing. We just cleverly feed the FFT engine the mirror-image of the input and post-process its output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The same FFT hardware/algorithm can compute IDFT. This saves memory, reduces code size, and halves the design effort in real-time DSP systems (e.g., OFDM receivers in 4G/5G, audio codecs, radar processors).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Twiddle Factor:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ — the fundamental complex exponential used in both DFT and FFT.
- **Computational Complexity:** DFT requires $N^2$ complex multiplications, whereas radix-2 FFT requires only $\frac{N}{2}\log_2 N$ complex multiplications. The factor of reduction is approximately $\frac{2N}{\log_2 N}$.
- **Scaling Factor:** $\frac{1}{N}$ is the normalization constant that must be applied at the end of the IDFT computation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — DFT and IDFT Definitions

The **DFT pair** is defined as:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) W_N^{nk}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

The **IDFT pair** is defined as:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-nk}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

where the **twiddle factor** is:

$$W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N} = \cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{N}\right) - j\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{N}\right)$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Why IDFT Can Be Computed Using FFT

The IDFT and DFT share the **same mathematical structure** — only the sign of the twiddle factor exponent differs. This duality is what enables FFT to compute IDFT.

- **Step 1 — Recognize the Duality:** The IDFT equation has $W_N^{-nk}$ while the DFT has $W_N^{+nk}$. These are complex conjugates because $\left(W_N^{nk}\right)^* = W_N^{-nk}$ (since $W_N$ lies on the unit circle).
- **Step 2 — Apply the Conjugate Trick:** A complex conjugate of an FFT result is equivalent to replacing $W_N^{+nk}$ with $W_N^{-nk}$. Therefore, conjugation effectively "reverses" the FFT direction.
- **Step 3 — Account for the Scaling:** The factor $\frac{1}{N}$ is missing in the DFT formulation. This scaling must be applied explicitly at the end of the procedure.
- **Step 4 — Combine the Operations:** The complete procedure to compute IDFT using FFT is: conjugate the input, run FFT, conjugate the output, divide by $N$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of IDFT-via-FFT:**
> - **OFDM Systems (4G LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi):** Transmitters use IDFT-via-IFFT to generate orthogonal sub-carriers; receivers use FFT for demodulation.
> - **MP3 / AAC Audio Decoders:** Spectral coefficients are transformed back to time-domain audio samples using IDFT.
> - **Speech Processing (Vocoders):** Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) vocoders reconstruct speech using IDFT-based synthesis filters.
> - **Medical Imaging (MRI):** k-space data is converted to spatial image using 2D-FFT-based reconstruction.
> - **Vibration Analysis in Mechanical Systems:** Modal analysis uses IDFT to convert frequency response functions back to impulse response functions (FRF → IRF).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** |
|:------------|:-------------------------|:----------------|
| Twiddle Factor | $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ | Fundamental complex exponential kernel |
| DFT | $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) W_N^{nk}$ | Forward transform — time to frequency |
| IDFT | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-nk}$ | Inverse transform — frequency to time |
| IDFT via FFT (compact form) | $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{FFT}\left\{ X^*(k) \right\} \right]^*$ | The 4-step procedure in one equation |
| Complex Conjugate of Twiddle | $\left( W_N^{nk} \right)^* = W_N^{-nk}$ | The duality property used in the algorithm |
| DFT Complexity | $N^2$ complex mults + $N(N-1)$ complex adds | Direct computation |
| Radix-2 FFT Complexity | $\frac{N}{2}\log_2 N$ complex mults + $N \log_2 N$ complex adds | Efficient computation |
| Speed-up Factor | $\frac{2N}{\log_2 N}$ | Ratio of DFT to FFT multiplications |
| Parseval's Check | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X(k) \vert^2$ | Energy conservation property (verification) |
| Magnitude of Output | $\vert x(n) \vert$ | Used in verification of computed sequence |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Rigorous Derivation: Why IDFT Equals $\frac{1}{N}\left[\text{FFT}\{X^*(k)\}\right]^*$

**Starting Point:** The formal definition of IDFT.

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-nk}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

**Step 1 — Take the Complex Conjugate of Both Sides:**

$$x^*(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^*(k) \left( W_N^{-nk} \right)^*$$

*Logic:* Conjugation distributes over the sum; the conjugate of $\frac{1}{N}$ is still $\frac{1}{N}$ since it is a real positive scalar.

**Step 2 — Apply the Conjugate Property of the Twiddle Factor:**

$$\left( W_N^{-nk} \right)^* = \left( e^{-j2\pi nk/N} \right)^* = e^{+j2\pi nk/N} = W_N^{+nk}$$

*Logic:* Conjugating a complex exponential on the unit circle negates its angle, flipping the sign of the exponent.

**Step 3 — Substitute Back:**

$$x^*(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^*(k) W_N^{+nk}$$

*Logic:* This is now the standard DFT summation form, applied to the sequence $X^*(k)$.

**Step 4 — Recognize the DFT Structure:**

The summation $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^*(k) W_N^{+nk}$ is exactly the **N-point DFT of the conjugated sequence $X^*(k)$**.

$$x^*(n) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \text{DFT}\left\{ X^*(k) \right\}$$

*Logic:* DFT definition is $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y(k) W_N^{nk}$ for any sequence $y(k)$. Here $y(k) = X^*(k)$.

**Step 5 — Conjugate Both Sides to Recover $x(n)$:**

$$x(n) = \left[ x^*(n) \right]^* = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{DFT}\left\{ X^*(k) \right\} \right]^*$$

*Logic:* $x(n)$ is the conjugate of $x^*(n)$. Since $\frac{1}{N}$ is real, it remains unchanged.

**Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\,x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{FFT}\left\{ X^*(k) \right\} \right]^*\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm Block Summary:**
> 1. **Input:** $X(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.
> 2. **Pre-process:** Compute $Y(k) = X^*(k)$ (complex conjugate of each sample).
> 3. **Core Process:** Compute $Z(n) = \text{N-point FFT}[Y(k)]$ using DIT or DIF butterfly.
> 4. **Post-process:** Compute $Z^*(n)$ (conjugate each output).
> 5. **Scale:** Divide every sample by $N$: $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} Z^*(n)$.
> 6. **Output:** $x(n)$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (N = 4)

**Problem:** Compute the IDFT of the sequence $X(k) = \lbrace 4, 1+j, 0, 1-j \rbrace$ using the FFT algorithm.

**Step 1 — Conjugate the Input:**

$$X^*(k) = \lbrace 4, 1-j, 0, 1+j \rbrace$$

**Step 2 — Apply 4-Point DIT-Radix-2 FFT on $X^*(k)$:**

Using the 4-point FFT butterfly structure with $W_4 = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_4^0 &= 1 \\
W_4^1 &= -j \\
W_4^2 &= -1 \\
W_4^3 &= +j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bit-reversed input order:** $X^*(0), X^*(2), X^*(1), X^*(3) = \lbrace 4, 0, 1-j, 1+j \rbrace$

**Stage 1 (Butterflies with $W_2^0 = 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0^{(1)} &= 4 + 0 = 4 \\
A_1^{(1)} &= 4 - 0 = 4 \\
A_2^{(1)} &= (1-j) + (1+j) = 2 \\
A_3^{(1)} &= (1-j) - (1+j) = -2j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 2 (Butterflies with $W_4^0 = 1$ and $W_4^1 = -j$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z(0) &= 4 + 2 \cdot (1) = 4 + 2 = 6 \\
Z(2) &= 4 - 2 \cdot (1) = 4 - 2 = 2 \\
Z(1) &= 4 + (-2j) \cdot (-j) = 4 + 2j^2 = 4 - 2 = 2 \\
Z(3) &= 4 - (-2j) \cdot (-j) = 4 - 2j^2 = 4 + 2 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* $(-2j) \cdot (-j) = 2j^2 = -2$.

**Re-ordering after Stage 2** (natural order):

$$Z(n) = \lbrace Z(0), Z(1), Z(2), Z(3) \rbrace = \lbrace 6, 2, 2, 6 \rbrace$$

**Step 3 — Conjugate the FFT Output:**

Since $Z(n)$ is purely real, $Z^*(n) = Z(n) = \lbrace 6, 2, 2, 6 \rbrace$.

**Step 4 — Divide by $N = 4$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(0) &= \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \\
x(1) &= \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \\
x(2) &= \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \\
x(3) &= \frac{6}{4} = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$x(n) = \lbrace 1.5,\ 0.5,\ 0.5,\ 1.5 \rbrace$$

**Verification (Direct IDFT Formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(0) &= \frac{1}{4} [4 + (1+j) + 0 + (1-j)] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + 1 + j + 0 + 1 - j] = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \ \checkmark \\
x(1) &= \frac{1}{4} [4 + (1+j)(+j) + 0 + (1-j)(-j)] = \frac{1}{4}[4 + j + j^2 + (-j + j^2)] \\
     &= \frac{1}{4}[4 + j - 1 - j - 1] = \frac{1}{4}[2] = 0.5 \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification using Parseval's Theorem:** $\sum \vert x(n) \vert^2 = 1.5^2 + 0.5^2 + 0.5^2 + 1.5^2 = 5$. And $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vert X(k) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{4}[16 + 2 + 0 + 2] = \frac{20}{4} = 5$. Both sides match — energy is conserved! ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Procedure Checklist

| **Step** | **Operation** | **Mathematical Form** | **Reason** |
|:--------:|:--------------|:----------------------|:-----------|
| 1 | Conjugate input | $Y(k) = X^*(k)$ | Replaces $W_N^{-nk}$ with $W_N^{+nk}$ |
| 2 | Run FFT | $Z(n) = \text{FFT}[Y(k)]$ | Reuses the same FFT engine |
| 3 | Conjugate output | $W(n) = Z^*(n)$ | Removes the conjugation introduced in Step 1 |
| 4 | Scale by $1/N$ | $x(n) = W(n)/N$ | Restores the IDFT normalization constant |
| 5 | Verify | Parseval's check | Confirms energy conservation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IDFT-via-FFT Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Frequency Domain Xk"] --> B["Step 1 Compute Conjugate Yk equals X star k"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Apply N Point FFT Algorithm DIT or DIF"]
    C --> D["Step 3 Compute Conjugate of FFT Output Wn equals Z star n"]
    D --> E["Step 4 Divide Each Sample by N"]
    E --> F["Output Time Domain Sequence xn"]
    F --> G["Validation via Parseval Theorem"]
    G --> H{"Energy Matched"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Result Accepted"]
    H -- No --> J["Recheck Butterfly Coefficients"]
```

## 4.2 4-Point DIT-Radix-2 Butterfly (Computing FFT Stage)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["X0"] --> S0["Sum"]
    A1["X2"] --> S0
    A0 --> D0["Difference"]
    A1 --> D0
    A2["X1"] --> S1["Sum"]
    A3["X3"] --> S1
    A2 --> M1["Multiply by W4 exp 1 equals minus j"]
    M1 --> D1["Difference"]
    A3 --> D1
    S0 --> O0["Z0"]
    S1 --> O1["Z1"]
    D0 --> O2["Z2"]
    D1 --> O3["Z3"]
```

## 4.3 DFT vs FFT Computational Complexity Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["N Point Transform Required"] --> Q{"Computational Budget"}
    Q -- "Hardware limited Low latency" --> R["Use Radix 2 FFT Complexity N log N"]
    Q -- "Offline processing Small N less than 32" --> S["Direct DFT Complexity N squared acceptable"]
    Q -- "Need IDFT capability" --> T["Apply IDFT using FFT Trick Conjugate input Conjugate output Scale by 1 over N"]
    T --> U["Single FFT Engine Handles Both Directions"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Real-Time DSP System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ADC["Analog Input via ADC"] --> BUF["Input Buffer N samples"]
    BUF --> SEL{"Mode Selector"}
    SEL -- "Forward DFT" --> FFT_CORE["Radix 2 FFT Engine"]
    SEL -- "Inverse IDFT" --> CONJ1["Conjugate Input Buffer"]
    CONJ1 --> FFT_CORE
    FFT_CORE --> POST["Post Processor"]
    POST --> CONJ2["Conjugate Output"]
    CONJ2 --> DIV["Divide by N"]
    DIV --> OUT_IDFT["Time Domain Output"]
    FFT_CORE --> OUT_DFT["Frequency Domain Output"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** In modern System-on-Chip (SoC) designs (e.g., TI C6000 series, ADI SHARC, Xilinx FPGA IP cores), a **single FFT hardware accelerator** is shared between forward and inverse transforms. The mode-selector simply toggles a flag that enables/disables the conjugate-and-scale post-processing unit. This is exactly the IDFT-via-FFT technique implemented in hardware.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**State the IDFT formula. Explain how the IDFT can be computed using the FFT algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

The IDFT is given by:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-nk}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

The IDFT can be computed using FFT in four steps:
1. **Conjugate** the input spectrum: $Y(k) = X^*(k)$.
2. Compute the **N-point FFT** of $Y(k)$: $Z(n) = \text{FFT}[Y(k)]$.
3. **Conjugate** the FFT output: $W(n) = Z^*(n)$.
4. **Scale** by $\frac{1}{N}$: $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} W(n)$.

The compact form is $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{FFT}\{X^*(k)\} \right]^*$. The duality of $W_N^{nk}$ and $W_N^{-nk}$ as complex conjugates is the key mathematical property that enables this technique.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]`
**Compare the computational complexity of direct IDFT computation versus IDFT using FFT algorithm for N = 1024 points.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Direct IDFT:** Requires $N^2 = 1024^2 = 1,048,576$ complex multiplications and $N(N-1) \approx 1,047,552$ complex additions.
- **IDFT using FFT (Radix-2):** Requires $\frac{N}{2} \log_2 N = 512 \times 10 = 5,120$ complex multiplications and $N \log_2 N = 10,240$ complex additions.
- **Speed-up factor:** $\frac{N^2}{(N/2)\log_2 N} = \frac{2N}{\log_2 N} = \frac{2048}{10} \approx 204.8$.

The IDFT-via-FFT technique is therefore approximately **205 times faster** for $N = 1024$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between DFT and IDFT, and show that the IDFT can be computed using the FFT algorithm with appropriate pre- and post-processing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the IDFT of the sequence $X(k) = \lbrace 8, 2+2j, 0, 2-2j \rbrace$ using the FFT algorithm. Show all butterfly computations. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

The IDFT is defined as:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) W_N^{-nk}$$

**Step 1 — Conjugation:** Taking the complex conjugate of both sides:

$$x^*(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^*(k) \left( W_N^{-nk} \right)^*$$

**Step 2 — Twiddle Conjugate Property:** Using $\left( W_N^{-nk} \right)^* = e^{j2\pi nk/N} = W_N^{+nk}$:

$$x^*(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^*(k) W_N^{nk}$$

**Step 3 — DFT Identification:** The summation is precisely the N-point DFT of $X^*(k)$:

$$x^*(n) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \text{DFT}\{X^*(k)\}$$

**Step 4 — Final Conjugation:** Taking the conjugate again:

$$\boxed{\,x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{FFT}\{X^*(k)\} \right]^*\,}$$

The pre-processing is conjugation of input, and post-processing is conjugation of output followed by division by $N$.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Conjugate the Input:**

$$X^*(k) = \lbrace 8,\ 2-2j,\ 0,\ 2+2j \rbrace$$

**Step 2 — Apply 4-Point DIT-FFT with $W_4^0 = 1$, $W_4^1 = -j$:**

Bit-reversed order: $\lbrace X^*(0), X^*(2), X^*(1), X^*(3) \rbrace = \lbrace 8, 0, 2-2j, 2+2j \rbrace$

**Stage 1 (Twiddle = 1):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0^{(1)} &= 8 + 0 = 8 \\
A_1^{(1)} &= 8 - 0 = 8 \\
A_2^{(1)} &= (2-2j) + (2+2j) = 4 \\
A_3^{(1)} &= (2-2j) - (2+2j) = -4j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 2 (Twiddles: $W_4^0 = 1$ for $m=0$, $W_4^1 = -j$ for $m=1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z(0) &= 8 + 4(1) = 12 \\
Z(2) &= 8 - 4(1) = 4 \\
Z(1) &= 8 + (-4j)(-j) = 8 + 4j^2 = 8 - 4 = 4 \\
Z(3) &= 8 - (-4j)(-j) = 8 - 4j^2 = 8 + 4 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

In natural order: $Z(n) = \lbrace 12, 4, 4, 12 \rbrace$

**Step 3 — Conjugate Output (real-valued, so unchanged):**

$$Z^*(n) = \lbrace 12, 4, 4, 12 \rbrace$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $N = 4$:**

$$x(n) = \lbrace 3,\ 1,\ 1,\ 3 \rbrace$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the procedure to compute IDFT using FFT. List the advantages of this approach. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the 8-point IDFT of $X(k) = \lbrace 8, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0 \rbrace$ using FFT and verify using direct IDFT formula. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Block Diagram Description:**

The IDFT-via-FFT system consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Input Block:** Receives frequency-domain samples $X(k)$.
2. **Complex Conjugator:** Computes $Y(k) = X^*(k)$ (negates the imaginary part of each sample).
3. **FFT Engine:** Computes $Z(n) = \text{FFT}[Y(k)]$ using DIT or DIF butterfly structure.
4. **Complex Conjugator (Output):** Computes $W(n) = Z^*(n)$.
5. **Divider / Scaler:** Multiplies each sample by $\frac{1}{N}$.
6. **Output Block:** Delivers the reconstructed time-domain signal $x(n)$.

**Advantages of IDFT-via-FFT:**

- **Memory Efficiency:** Only one FFT routine is stored in program memory, halving code size.
- **Computational Speed:** Reduces operations from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log_2 N)$.
- **Hardware Reuse:** A single FFT accelerator (in FPGA/ASIC) handles both DFT and IDFT.
- **Design Simplicity:** Easier verification, testing, and maintenance of the DSP pipeline.
- **Real-time Feasibility:** Enables high-speed applications like OFDM, audio coding, and radar signal processing.

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:** $X(k) = \lbrace 8, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0 \rbrace$ (impulse in frequency domain).

**Step 1 — Conjugate Input:** Since $X(k)$ is real, $X^*(k) = X(k)$.

**Step 2 — Apply 8-Point FFT:** 

By the duality property, the 8-point FFT of a constant $X^*(k) = 8$ yields:

$$\text{FFT}\{8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8, 8\} = \lbrace 64, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0 \rbrace$$

This is because the DFT of a constant sequence $x(n) = c$ gives $X(0) = Nc$ and $X(k) = 0$ for $k \neq 0$.

**Step 3 — Conjugate Output:** $Z^*(n) = \lbrace 64, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0 \rbrace$ (unchanged, all real).

**Step 4 — Divide by $N = 8$:**

$$x(n) = \lbrace 8, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0 \rbrace$$

**Verification via Direct IDFT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(0) &= \frac{1}{8} \sum_{k=0}^{7} X(k) W_8^{0} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot 8 = 1 \quad \text{(recheck needed below)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Correction during verification:* The given $X(k) = 8$ at $k=0$ only.

$$
x(n) = \frac{1}{8} \cdot 8 \cdot W_8^{0} = 1 \quad \text{for all } n
$$

So $x(n) = \lbrace 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1 \rbrace$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Notice:** The example in the question yields a constant unit-amplitude time-domain signal because a single non-zero DC bin corresponds to a constant sequence in the time domain. The final correct answer is $x(n) = \lbrace 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1 \rbrace$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Conjugate Placement Error:** Students often forget that **two** conjugations are needed — one at the input AND one at the output. Skipping either one will produce a sign-flipped or completely wrong result.
> - **Division by $N$ Omission:** Forgetting the $\frac{1}{N}$ scaling factor is the **#1 reason** students lose 2-3 marks. The FFT engine alone does NOT produce the IDFT; you must explicitly scale.
> - **Twiddle Factor Sign Confusion:** Some students write $W_N^{-nk}$ inside the FFT call. The FFT routine only accepts $W_N^{+nk}$; the conjugation trick handles the sign change.
> - **Bit-Reversal Order in Numerical Problems:** After applying FFT, the output must be re-ordered to natural index $n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$. KTU examiners specifically check for this re-ordering.
> - **Complex Arithmetic in Butterflies:** Forgetting that $j \times j = -1$ and $(-j) \times (-j) = -1$ leads to butterfly computation errors. Practice basic complex multiplications.
> - **No Mix-up with DIT vs DIF:** Either DIT or DIF algorithm can be used, but the twiddle factor arrangement in each stage differs. Stick to one method consistently during the exam.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ The **IDFT** is the inverse of the **DFT**, related by a sign change in the twiddle factor and a factor of $\frac{1}{N}$.
- ✅ The **twiddle factor** is $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$; its conjugate property $\left(W_N^{nk}\right)^* = W_N^{-nk}$ is the **mathematical backbone** of the IDFT-via-FFT technique.
- ✅ The **compact IDFT-via-FFT formula** is $x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \text{FFT}\{X^*(k)\} \right]^*$.
- ✅ The procedure has **4 steps**: (1) Conjugate input, (2) Apply N-point FFT, (3) Conjugate output, (4) Divide by $N$.
- ✅ **Direct IDFT** requires $N^2$ complex multiplications; **IDFT-via-FFT** requires only $\frac{N}{2} \log_2 N$ multiplications.
- ✅ The **speed-up factor** is $\frac{2N}{\log_2 N}$ — for $N = 1024$, the speed-up is approximately **205×**.
- ✅ **Hardware realization:** A single FFT engine in an FPGA/ASIC can be reconfigured (via a mode flag) to perform both DFT and IDFT — the IDFT-via-FFT technique is industry standard.
- ✅ **Real-time applications:** OFDM (Wi-Fi, 4G/5G), audio codecs (MP3, AAC), speech synthesis, MRI image reconstruction, and vibration analysis all rely on this technique.
- ✅ **Verification method:** Use **Parseval's theorem** — $\sum \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum \vert X(k) \vert^2$ — to confirm your computed IDFT result.
- ✅ **Remember the scaling:** Never forget the $\frac{1}{N}$ factor — it is the normalization that distinguishes IDFT from DFT.
- ✅ **Bit-reversal:** When using radix-2 DIT/DIF FFT, the input must be in bit-reversed order and the output is in natural order (or vice versa for DIF).
- ✅ **Twiddle symmetry properties** (useful in butterfly simplifications): $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^{k}$ and $W_N^{N-k} = W_N^{-k} = \left(W_N^k\right)^*$.
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** Conjugate placement, missing $\frac{1}{N}$ factor, incorrect bit-reversal, and confusion between DIT/DIF butterfly structure.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme alignment:** This topic maps to **CO1** (Apply DFT/FFT for signal analysis) and **CO2** (Design efficient DSP algorithms), and is typically tested in Module 1 for 7-14 marks in the End Semester Examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to IIR and FIR Systems, Structures for IIR Systems: Direct-Form Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to IIR and FIR Systems, Structures for IIR Systems: Direct-Form Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Digital Systems: An Introduction

A **discrete-time system** processes an input sequence $x(n)$ to produce an output sequence $y(n)$ according to a well-defined mathematical rule. When this rule is governed by a **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)**, the system is classified as **Linear Shift-Invariant (LSI)** — the most important class studied in KTU DSP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **digital filter** is an LSI discrete-time system that shapes the frequency content of an input signal according to a prescribed specification (e.g., low-pass, high-pass, band-pass). It can be realized using two fundamentally different mathematical structures: **IIR** or **FIR**.

### 1.2 IIR Systems — The Infinite Memory System

**IIR (Infinite Impulse Response)** systems are recursive digital filters whose impulse response $h(n)$ extends **infinitely in time** because the output $y(n)$ depends on past output samples in addition to current and past input samples.

**General Difference Equation (N-th order IIR):**

$$y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The presence of the terms $a_k \cdot y(n-k)$ (feedback terms) creates the *recursive* structure. This is why the impulse response never dies out completely — every past output "echoes" into the future, hence **infinite**.

### 1.3 FIR Systems — The Finite Memory System

**FIR (Finite Impulse Response)** systems are non-recursive digital filters whose impulse response $h(n)$ has a **finite duration of M+1 samples**. The output depends *only* on current and past input samples.

**General Difference Equation (M-th order FIR):**

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice the *absence* of feedback terms $a_k \cdot y(n-k)$. Since there is no recursion, the impulse response is mathematically guaranteed to be of finite length $M+1$. This makes FIR filters inherently **stable**.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy — "The Echo Hall vs. The Anechoic Chamber"

| System Type | Real-World Analogy | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **IIR** | A microphone inside a large concert hall | The sound keeps bouncing off walls — every reflection produces new reflections. An initial clap is heard **forever** (with diminishing echoes). |
| **FIR** | A microphone inside an anechoic chamber | Foam-lined walls absorb all sound. An initial clap is heard for a **finite duration** (typically 30–100 ms) and then silence returns completely. |

This analogy captures the essence: **feedback creates infinite memory**, while **pure feedforward creates only finite memory**.

### 1.5 Why We Need to Study Realization Structures

For the *same* transfer function, there exist **multiple block diagram realizations** — Direct Form I, Direct Form II (canonical), Transposed, Cascade, Parallel, Lattice, etc. Each realization differs in:
- Number of delay elements (memory)
- Sensitivity to coefficient quantization
- Susceptibility to overflow
- Ease of implementation on DSP hardware

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Module 2 emphasizes **Direct-Form I** and **Direct-Form II (Canonical)** for IIR systems. Students must master drawing these structures from a given difference equation — this is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in University Exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General IIR Difference Equation — Logic Breakdown

Starting from the LCCDE:

$$y(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y(n-k) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k)$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Left-hand side (LHS):** Contains $y(n)$ and $y(n-1), y(n-2), \ldots, y(n-N)$ — these are **delayed versions of the output** (feedback components).
- **Right-hand side (RHS):** Contains $x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M)$ — these are **delayed versions of the input** (feedforward components).
- The coefficients $\{a_k\}$ are called **feedback (or pole) coefficients**, while $\{b_k\}$ are called **feedforward (or zero) coefficients**.
- A **causal** system uses only $n, n-1, n-2, \ldots$ (no future samples).

### 2.2 Transfer Function of IIR Systems (The Central Equation)

Taking the Z-transform of the LCCDE under zero initial conditions:

$$Y(z) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}\, Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}\, X(z)$$

$$Y(z) \left[ 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k} \right] = X(z) \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k} \right]$$

Therefore, the **Transfer Function** is:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is the central equation:** Every IIR filter design technique (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic, Bilinear Transform) ends with this rational polynomial in $z^{-1}$. From here, the *realization* is simply a matter of choosing how to draw the block diagram.

### 2.3 Decomposition into Two Cascaded Sections

The transfer function can be factored as a **product of two simpler transfer functions**:

$$H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z)$$

where:

$$H_1(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k} \quad \text{(All-zero / FIR / Feedforward part)}$$

$$H_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}} \quad \text{(All-pole / Feedback part)}$$

- $H_1(z)$ contains only **zeros** (numerator roots of $H(z)$).
- $H_2(z)$ contains only **poles** (denominator roots of $H(z)$).
- **Direct Form I** implements $H_1(z)$ first, then $H_2(z)$.
- **Direct Form II** implements $H_2(z)$ first, then $H_1(z)$ — and crucially **merges the two delay lines into one**.

### 2.4 IIR vs FIR — Comparative Analysis

| Property | IIR System | FIR System |
|---|---|---|
| Impulse Response Length | Infinite | Finite (M+1 samples) |
| Recursion | Yes (has feedback) | No (purely non-recursive) |
| Difference Equation | Has $a_k y(n-k)$ terms | No feedback terms |
| Transfer Function | Rational (poles + zeros) | Polynomial (zeros only) |
| Stability | Conditional (poles inside unit circle) | Always stable |
| Phase Response | Generally non-linear phase | Can achieve **linear phase** (symmetric coefficients) |
| Computational Cost | Low order → low cost | High order for sharp transitions |
| Memory Required | Lower | Higher |
| Coefficient Sensitivity | High | Low |
| KTU 2024 Status | Studied in **Module 2** | Studied in **Module 3** |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table collects **every formula** you need for Direct-Form I and Direct-Form II derivations. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every KTU University Exam.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| IIR Difference Equation | $y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k)$ | Recursive definition |
| FIR Difference Equation | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k)$ | Non-recursive definition |
| IIR Transfer Function | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}}$ | Rational in $z^{-1}$ |
| All-zero section | $H_1(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}$ | Numerator of $H(z)$ |
| All-pole section | $H_2(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}}$ | Reciprocal of denominator |
| Direct Form I decomposition | $H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z)$ | Zeros first, then poles |
| Direct Form II decomposition | $H(z) = H_2(z) \cdot H_1(z)$ | Poles first, then zeros (commutative) |
| Direct Form I delays | $M + N$ | Total memory elements |
| Direct Form II delays | $\max(M, N)$ | **Canonical** — minimum memory |
| Stability Condition | All poles $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ | Inside unit circle in z-plane |
| Magnitude Response | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert H(z) \vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$ | On unit circle |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IIR Direct-Form structures** are used in real-time audio equalizers (graphic EQ in mixing consoles), digital crossover networks in loudspeakers, biomedical signal processors (ECG/EEG filtering), and adaptive noise cancellation in hearing aids.
- The **canonical (Direct Form II)** is preferred in hardware implementation (FPGA, ASIC) because it minimizes flip-flop usage — critical when designing cost-sensitive embedded DSP chips.
- **Direct Form I** is preferred when numerical overflow is a concern, because the dynamic range of intermediate signals is bounded.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Setting Up a Reference Second-Order IIR System

To keep the derivation concrete and exam-friendly, consider a **second-order IIR filter** (the most frequently tested case in KTU):

$$y(n) = -a_1\, y(n-1) - a_2\, y(n-2) + b_0\, x(n) + b_1\, x(n-1) + b_2\, x(n-2)$$

This is a **2nd-order** system because the maximum delay in both input and output is **2 samples** ($N = M = 2$). It has **5 coefficients**: two feedback $\{a_1, a_2\}$ and three feedforward $\{b_0, b_1, b_2\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always write the LCCDE with feedback terms on the LHS and feedforward terms on the RHS. The negative sign of $a_k$ is part of the standard form.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Transfer Function (Z-Domain)

**Step 1:** Apply the Z-transform to both sides, using the time-shift property: $Z\{x(n-k)\} = z^{-k} X(z)$.

$$Y(z) = -a_1\, z^{-1} Y(z) - a_2\, z^{-2} Y(z) + b_0\, X(z) + b_1\, z^{-1} X(z) + b_2\, z^{-2} X(z)$$

**Step 2:** Group all $Y(z)$ terms on the LHS and all $X(z)$ terms on the RHS.

$$Y(z) + a_1\, z^{-1} Y(z) + a_2\, z^{-2} Y(z) = b_0\, X(z) + b_1\, z^{-1} X(z) + b_2\, z^{-2} X(z)$$

$$Y(z)\, \bigl[ 1 + a_1\, z^{-1} + a_2\, z^{-2} \bigr] = X(z)\, \bigl[ b_0 + b_1\, z^{-1} + b_2\, z^{-2} \bigr]$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$.

$$H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1\, z^{-1} + b_2\, z^{-2}}{1 + a_1\, z^{-1} + a_2\, z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4:** Factor as $H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z)$ where:

$$H_1(z) = b_0 + b_1\, z^{-1} + b_2\, z^{-2} \quad \text{(All-zero section)}$$

$$H_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 + a_1\, z^{-1} + a_2\, z^{-2}} \quad \text{(All-pole section)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Direct-Form I Structure

**Conceptual Idea:** Direct Form I is a **direct transcription** of the LCCDE. We implement the all-zero section $H_1(z)$ first, then feed its output into the all-pole section $H_2(z)$.

**Step 1:** Define an intermediate signal $w(n)$ as the output of the all-zero (FIR) section.

$$w(n) = b_0\, x(n) + b_1\, x(n-1) + b_2\, x(n-2)$$

**Step 2:** Define $y(n)$ as the output of the all-pole section driven by $w(n)$.

$$y(n) = w(n) - a_1\, y(n-1) - a_2\, y(n-2)$$

**Step 3:** Identify the required delay elements:
- For $H_1(z)$: we need $x(n-1)$ and $x(n-2)$ → **2 delays** on the input side.
- For $H_2(z)$: we need $y(n-1)$ and $y(n-2)$ → **2 delays** on the output side.
- **Total delays = M + N = 4** for this second-order case.

**Step 4:** Build the block diagram (visualized in Section 4).

### 3.4 Derivation of Direct-Form II (Canonical) Structure

**Conceptual Idea:** Since LTI systems commute, $H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z) = H_2(z) \cdot H_1(z)$. We swap the order and **share the delay line** between both sections.

**Step 1:** Implement $H_2(z)$ first. Define an intermediate signal $v(n)$ representing the internal state.

$$v(n) = w(n) - a_1\, v(n-1) - a_2\, v(n-2)$$

where $w(n)$ is the input to this section (which is $x(n)$ itself in the swapped order).

Actually, when we swap the order, $w(n) = x(n)$ becomes the input. So:

$$v(n) = x(n) - a_1\, v(n-1) - a_2\, v(n-2)$$

**Step 2:** The output $y(n)$ is then produced by applying $H_1(z)$ to $v(n)$.

$$y(n) = b_0\, v(n) + b_1\, v(n-1) + b_2\, v(n-2)$$

**Step 3:** Identify delay elements:
- Only one shared delay line is needed for $v(n-1)$ and $v(n-2)$.
- **Total delays = max(M, N) = 2** for this second-order case.

**Step 4:** Compare:
- Direct Form I needs **M + N = 4** delays.
- Direct Form II needs **max(M, N) = 2** delays.
- This 50% memory saving is why Direct Form II is called **canonical**.

### 3.5 General N-th Order Derivation

For a general N-th order IIR system with $M = N$:

**Direct Form I:**

$$w(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} b_k\, x(n-k)$$

$$y(n) = w(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y(n-k)$$

- Delays needed: **2N** (N for input, N for output).

**Direct Form II (Canonical):**

$$v(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, v(n-k)$$

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} b_k\, v(n-k)$$

- Delays needed: **N** (single shared line).

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Realize the following IIR system in Direct Form I and Direct Form II:

$$y(n) = 0.5\, y(n-1) - 0.3\, y(n-2) + x(n) + 2x(n-1) + x(n-2)$$

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients by comparing with standard form.

$$a_1 = -0.5, \quad a_2 = 0.3, \quad b_0 = 1, \quad b_1 = 2, \quad b_2 = 1$$

(Note: the standard form has $-a_k$ on the RHS, so $y(n) - 0.5y(n-1) + 0.3y(n-2) = \ldots$ gives $a_1 = -0.5$, $a_2 = 0.3$.)

**Step 2:** Write the transfer function.

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5z^{-1} + 0.3z^{-2}}$$

**Step 3:** Direct Form I equations.

$$w(n) = x(n) + 2x(n-1) + x(n-2)$$

$$y(n) = w(n) + 0.5\, y(n-1) - 0.3\, y(n-2)$$

(Here we substitute $-a_1 = 0.5$ and $-a_2 = -0.3$.)

**Step 4:** Direct Form II equations.

$$v(n) = x(n) + 0.5\, v(n-1) - 0.3\, v(n-2)$$

$$y(n) = v(n) + 2v(n-1) + v(n-2)$$

**Step 5:** Count delays.
- Direct Form I: 4 delays (2 for $x$, 2 for $y$).
- Direct Form II: 2 delays (only $v$).

This example is **directly applicable** for the KTU 14-mark question on Direct-Form realizations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Direct-Form I Block Diagram (2nd Order)

The following Mermaid flowchart represents the canonical Direct-Form I realization. Notice two separate delay lines — one for input $x(n)$ producing $w(n)$, and one for output $y(n)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xn(["x(n)"]) --> mulB0["x b0"]
    mulB0 --> add1((Sum+))
    xn --> dly1["D z-1"]
    dly1 --> dly2["D z-1"]
    dly2 --> mulB2["x b2"]
    dly2 --> mulB1["x b1"]
    mulB1 --> add1
    mulB2 --> add1
    add1 --> wn["w(n)"]
    wn --> add2((Sum+))
    wn --> mulA1["x -a1"]
    mulA1 --> add2
    yn(["y(n-1) feedback"]) --> mulA1
    add2 --> dlyY1["D z-1"]
    dlyY1 --> dlyY2["D z-1"]
    dlyY2 --> mulA2["x -a2"]
    mulA2 --> add2
    dlyY1 --> yn
    add2 --> ynout(["y(n)"])
    ynout --> dlyY1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The upper horizontal branch computes the all-zero (FIR) part: $w(n) = b_0 x(n) + b_1 x(n-1) + b_2 x(n-2)$.
- The lower branch computes the all-pole part: $y(n) = w(n) - a_1 y(n-1) - a_2 y(n-2)$.
- **Total delay elements: 4** (two for input, two for output).

### 4.2 Direct-Form II (Canonical) Block Diagram (2nd Order)

This is the **canonical form** — only one shared delay line in the middle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xn(["x(n)"]) --> add1((Sum+))
    add1 --> vn["v(n)"]
    vn --> add2((Sum+))
    vn --> mulA1["x -a1"]
    mulA1 --> add1
    vn --> dlyV1["D z-1"]
    dlyV1 --> dlyV2["D z-1"]
    dlyV2 --> mulA2["x -a2"]
    mulA2 --> add1
    dlyV1 --> mulA1
    add2 --> mulB0["x b0"]
    add2 --> mulB1["x b1"]
    mulB1 --> add3((Sum+))
    add2 --> dlyV1
    add2 --> mulB2["x b2"]
    dlyV2 --> mulB1
    dlyV2 --> mulB2
    mulB2 --> add3
    mulB0 --> add3
    add3 --> yn(["y(n)"])
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The middle branch (single delay line) stores $v(n-1)$ and $v(n-2)$ — this is shared.
- The left section computes $v(n) = x(n) - a_1 v(n-1) - a_2 v(n-2)$ (all-pole).
- The right section computes $y(n) = b_0 v(n) + b_1 v(n-1) + b_2 v(n-2)$ (all-zero).
- **Total delay elements: 2** (canonical, minimum memory).

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow (System-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["LCCDE y(n)"] --> B["Apply Z-Transform"]
    B --> C["Derive H of z"]
    C --> D{"Choose Realization"}
    D --> E["Direct Form I: M+N delays"]
    D --> F["Direct Form II: max M N delays"]
    E --> G["Higher memory but stable numerics"]
    F --> H["Canonical minimum memory"]
    G --> I["Implement on DSP"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Real-time digital filter output"]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Summary Table (For Exam Answer Writing)

| Feature | Direct Form I | Direct Form II (Canonical) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Delay Elements | $M + N$ | $\max(M, N)$ |
| Number of Multipliers | $M + N + 1$ | $M + N + 1$ |
| Number of Adders | $M + N$ | $M + N$ |
| Order of Implementation | Feedforward first, then Feedback | Feedback first, then Feedforward |
| Memory Requirement | Higher | Lower (canonical) |
| Coefficient Sensitivity | Moderate | Higher (poles and zeros share state) |
| Numerical Overflow | Less prone | More prone |
| Preferred for | Software floating-point | Hardware (FPGA/ASIC) with limited memory |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define IIR and FIR systems. State one key difference between them.**

**Model Answer:**

- **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) System:** A discrete-time system whose impulse response $h(n)$ extends to infinity. Its output depends on past outputs (feedback/recursive).
- **FIR (Finite Impulse Response) System:** A discrete-time system whose impulse response $h(n)$ has a finite duration. Its output depends only on present and past inputs (non-recursive).
- **Key Difference:** IIR systems contain feedback terms $\{a_k\}$ in their difference equation and poles in their transfer function; FIR systems are purely feedforward and contain only zeros (no poles).

---

**Q2. What is meant by the "canonical" form of an IIR filter realization? Why is Direct Form II called canonical?**

**Model Answer:**

- A **canonical** realization uses the **minimum possible number of delay elements** required to implement a given transfer function.
- **Direct Form II is called canonical** because it requires only $\max(M, N)$ delay elements, compared to $M + N$ elements in Direct Form I. This represents a 50% memory reduction when $M = N$, achieved by sharing a single delay line between the feedforward and feedback sections.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Pattern]**

> **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the Direct-Form I structure for a second-order IIR system described by:

$$y(n) = -a_1 y(n-1) - a_2 y(n-2) + b_0 x(n) + b_1 x(n-1) + b_2 x(n-2)$$

Clearly show the difference equation, Z-domain transfer function, decomposition into all-zero and all-pole sections, and the block diagram with all delay elements labeled.

**(b)** Convert the same system into Direct Form II (canonical) form. Show that the number of delay elements is reduced from 4 to 2 and explain the principle behind this reduction.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Apply Z-transform to the LCCDE:

$$Y(z)\,[1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}] = X(z)\,[b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}]$$

**Step 2:** Transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 3:** Factor as $H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z)$:

$$H_1(z) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}, \quad H_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4:** Define intermediate signal $w(n) = b_0 x(n) + b_1 x(n-1) + b_2 x(n-2)$. Then $y(n) = w(n) - a_1 y(n-1) - a_2 y(n-2)$. The block diagram (see Section 4.1) uses **4 delay elements**.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Since LTI systems commute, write $H(z) = H_2(z) \cdot H_1(z)$.

**Step 2:** Define internal state $v(n)$: $v(n) = x(n) - a_1 v(n-1) - a_2 v(n-2)$.

**Step 3:** Then $y(n) = b_0 v(n) + b_1 v(n-1) + b_2 v(n-2)$.

**Step 4:** Only **2 delay elements** are needed (for $v(n-1)$ and $v(n-2)$). The principle: the two delay lines in Direct Form I (one for $x$, one for $y$) store **identical sequences** when the system is at steady state. By recognizing this redundancy and sharing a single delay line, we save memory without changing the input-output behaviour. (Block diagram: Section 4.2.)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Explain the difference equation representation of an N-th order IIR system. Distinguish clearly between feedback and feedforward coefficients.

**(b)** For the transfer function $H(z) = \dfrac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.64 z^{-2}}$, determine the Direct-Form II realization. Write the time-domain difference equations and draw the block diagram.

**Model Solution (a):**

The general N-th order IIR difference equation is:

$$y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x(n-k)$$

- **Feedback coefficients** $\{a_k\}$ multiply past outputs $y(n-k)$. They introduce poles into $H(z)$ and create the recursive (infinite-memory) character of the system.
- **Feedforward coefficients** $\{b_k\}$ multiply current and past inputs $x(n-k)$. They introduce zeros into $H(z)$ and shape the FIR portion of the filter.
- The order $N$ is the maximum of input and output delays; stability requires all poles $\vert p_k \vert < 1$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients: $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 0.5$, $b_2 = 0.25$; $a_1 = -0.8$, $a_2 = 0.64$.

**Step 2:** Direct Form II equations (intermediate variable $v(n)$):

$$v(n) = x(n) + 0.8\, v(n-1) - 0.64\, v(n-2)$$

$$y(n) = v(n) + 0.5\, v(n-1) + 0.25\, v(n-2)$$

**Step 3:** The block diagram follows the structure of Section 4.2 with the specified numerical coefficients. **Total delays = 2** (canonical).

**Stability check:** Denominator $1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.64z^{-2}$ has poles at $z = 0.4 \pm j0.5571$, with magnitude $\sqrt{0.16 + 0.31} \approx 0.688 < 1$. **System is stable.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** The LCCDE in standard form has $-a_k y(n-k)$ on the RHS. Students often forget the negative sign when drawing blocks, leading to wrong feedback paths.
> 2. **Delay Counting Mistakes:** Confusing $M$ (input order) with $N$ (output order). Always state "Direct Form I uses $M+N$ delays, Direct Form II uses $\max(M,N)$ delays."
> 3. **Order of Cascade Confusion:** Direct Form I is zeros-first-then-poles; Direct Form II is poles-first-then-zeros. Drawing them in the wrong order **costs 3–4 marks** even if the equations are correct.
> 4. **Missing Intermediate Signal:** In Direct Form II, students forget to define $v(n)$ clearly and directly try to compute $y(n)$, losing the "canonical" structure.
> 5. **Block Diagram Arrows:** Every delay element must have its input on the left and output on the right. Feedback arrows must be clearly distinguished with proper arrowheads.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **IIR system** is recursive: $y(n)$ depends on past outputs. Its impulse response is **infinite**.
- ✅ An **FIR system** is non-recursive: $y(n)$ depends only on inputs. Its impulse response is **finite** and the system is **always stable**.
- ✅ The general IIR difference equation is $y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k)$.
- ✅ The IIR transfer function is rational: $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum a_k z^{-k}}$.
- ✅ **Direct Form I** = cascade of all-zero ($H_1$) and all-pole ($H_2$) sections → uses $M+N$ delays.
- ✅ **Direct Form II** = swapped cascade with a **shared delay line** → uses $\max(M,N)$ delays (canonical).
- ✅ The number of multipliers is the same in both forms: $M+N+1$. Only the **memory** differs.
- ✅ LTI systems commute: $H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z) = H_2(z) \cdot H_1(z)$ — this algebraic fact is the *foundation* of Direct Form II.
- ✅ Stability of IIR filters requires **all poles inside the unit circle**: $\vert p_k \vert < 1$.
- ✅ **KTU exam tip:** Always draw both structures side-by-side and label all nodes ($x(n)$, $w(n)$, $v(n)$, $y(n)$) and all coefficients.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascade-Form Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cascade-Form Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascade-Form Structure (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A cascade-form realization is a method of implementing a digital filter transfer function $H(z)$ by factoring it into a **product (series connection)** of lower-order subsections — typically **second-order sections (biquads)** and/or **first-order sections** — and connecting these sections in tandem (cascaded), where the output of one section becomes the input of the next.

For an $N$-th order rational IIR filter, the transfer function is expressed as:

$$H(z) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

In cascade form, this is rewritten as:

$$H(z) = \prod_{i=1}^{L} H_i(z)$$

where each $H_i(z)$ is a **biquadratic (biquad) section** of the form:

$$H_i(z) = \frac{b_{0i} + b_{1i} z^{-1} + b_{2i} z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1i} z^{-1} + a_{2i} z^{-2}}$$

If the overall order is odd, one section is first-order: $H_i(z) = \dfrac{b_{0i} + b_{1i} z^{-1}}{1 + a_{1i} z^{-1}}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Plumbing Analogy 💧**
> Imagine a water purification system consisting of **multiple filter stages** connected in a pipeline. The dirty water enters Stage 1 (say, a sand filter), its output flows into Stage 2 (a carbon filter), then Stage 3 (a UV purifier), and finally clean water comes out.
>
> - Each **stage** is a simple, low-order filter (easy to design and build).
> - The **overall quality** of purification is the cumulative effect of all stages.
> - The **order of stages matters**: putting UV before sand is wasteful.
> - **Pairing** is like choosing which impurities each stage removes.
>
> This is exactly how the **Cascade-Form** works in DSP — we split a complex high-order filter into a chain of simple **biquad stages**.

## 1.3 Key Physical/Design Constants & Metrics

The following numerical parameters govern the design of cascade structures:

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range | Significance |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Section Order** | **2** (biquad) | Standard, minimizes coefficient sensitivity |
| **Overflow Headroom** | **≥ 3 dB** (factor ≈ 1.414) | Prevents internal saturation between stages |
| **L₁-norm bound** | $\sum_k \vert h_i[k]\vert \leq 1$ | Used in $L_1$ scaling for overflow protection |
| **L₂-norm bound** | $\left[\sum_k h_i^2[k]\right]^{1/2} \leq 1$ | Used in $L_2$ (energy) scaling |
| **L∞-norm bound** | $\max_k \vert h_i[k]\vert \leq 1$ | Used in $L_\infty$ (peak) scaling |
