> 2. **Indirect Design via Analog Prototype** — design a well-known analog filter (Butterworth / Chebyshev / Elliptic), then apply a transformation (Bilinear or Impulse Invariance) to obtain the digital equivalent. This is the **standard approach for IIR filters**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for IIR Filter Design (Indirect Method)

- **Step 1 — Specification Translation:** Convert problem text into numerical tolerances: $\omega_p, \omega_s, \delta_p, \delta_s$.
- **Step 2 — Frequency Pre-warping:** Apply the bilinear transformation pre-warp to map the critical digital frequencies into analog prototype frequencies using the formula $\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan(\omega/2)$.
- **Step 3 — Analog Prototype Selection:** Choose Butterworth (maximally flat), Chebyshev Type-I (passband ripple), Chebyshev Type-II (stopband ripple), or Elliptic (both ripples) based on the order-vs-ripple trade-off.
- **Step 4 — Analog Filter Order Calculation:** Compute the minimum order $N$ required to meet specifications.
- **Step 5 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion:** Apply the bilinear transformation $s = \frac{2}{T}\frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$ to obtain $H(z)$.
- **Step 6 — Realization & Verification:** Implement via direct form, cascade form, or parallel form, and verify the magnitude response.

### 2.3 FIR vs IIR — Strategic Selection

Before designing, the engineer must decide **which type of filter** best suits the application:

| Criterion | IIR Filter | FIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Phase Response | Non-linear (phase distortion) | Strictly linear phase possible |
| Stability | Conditional (poles inside unit circle) | Always stable (no feedback) |
| Computational Cost | Lower order → cheaper | Higher order → costlier |
| Design Approach | Indirect (via analog) | Direct (windowing / frequency sampling) |
| Memory Required | Less | More |
| Best Use Case | Audio processing where phase is less critical | Data communication / image processing needing linear phase |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Whenever the question states *"linear phase is required"*, the answer is unequivocally **FIR**. When it states *"minimum order for given specs"*, the answer is **IIR**.

### 2.4 Absolute vs Relative Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Specification Conventions (Both appear in KTU papers):**

**Absolute Specifications** use straight magnitudes:
$$1 - \delta_p \leq \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \leq 1, \quad \text{for } 0 \leq \omega \leq \omega_p$$
$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \leq \delta_s, \quad \text{for } \omega_s \leq \omega \leq \pi$$

**Relative Specifications** (in decibels) are more common in industry:
$$A_p = -20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p) \quad \text{dB (passband ripple in dB)}$$
$$A_s = -20 \log_{10}(\delta_s) \quad \text{dB (stopband attenuation in dB)}$$

The relationship between absolute $\delta$ and relative $A$ (in dB):
$$\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20}, \quad \delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bilinear Transformation | $s = \frac{2}{T} \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | Maps analog $s$-plane to digital $z$-plane |
| 2 | Pre-warping Equation | $\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Adjusts frequency before prototype design |
| 3 | Passband Ripple in dB | $A_p = -20\log_{10}(1-\delta_p)$ | Spec conversion |
| 4 | Stopband Attenuation in dB | $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$ | Spec conversion |
| 5 | Butterworth Order Formula | $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{A_s/10}-1}{10^{A_p/10}-1}\right]}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ | Minimum IIR order for Butterworth |
| 6 | Chebyshev Order Formula | $N \geq \dfrac{\cosh^{-1}\!\left[\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{A_s/10}-1}{10^{A_p/10}-1}}\right]}{\cosh^{-1}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ | Minimum IIR order for Chebyshev |
| 7 | Transition Width (FIR Kaiser) | $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ | Window-method design |
| 8 | Kaiser Parameter $\beta$ | $\beta = 0.1102(A_s - 8.7)$ for $A_s > 50$ dB | Optimal window shape |
| 9 | Kaiser Filter Order | $N = \dfrac{A_s - 8}{2.285\,\Delta\omega}$ | Empirical order estimate |
| 10 | Frequency Transformation LPF→HPF | $z^{-1} \rightarrow -z^{-1}$ | High-pass conversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These specifications govern ECG machine filters (removing 50/60 Hz mains hum), telephone voice-band filters (300 Hz to 3.4 kHz), seismic data processors (low-frequency emphasis), and CD/DVD playback equalizers. The "General Considerations" framework is therefore the **lingua franca** of every practicing DSP engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Analog to Digital Frequency via Bilinear Transformation

The bilinear transformation is a one-to-one mapping from the analog $s$-plane to the digital $z$-plane, designed to **preserve stability** (left-half $s$-plane maps to inside of unit circle in $z$-plane).

We start with the trapezoidal rule for numerical integration. For an analog integrator $H_a(s) = \frac{1}{s}$:

$$\int_{(n-1)T}^{nT} x(\tau)\,d\tau \approx \frac{T}{2}\bigl[x(nT) + x((n-1)T)\bigr]$$

In the $z$-domain, this integration corresponds to:

$$H(z) = \frac{T}{2}\,\frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

Inverting to find the mapping from $z$ to $s$:

$$s = \frac{2}{T}\,\frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$

Now we substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ to find the **frequency warping relationship**:

$$s = \frac{2}{T}\,\frac{1 - e^{-j\omega}}{1 + e^{-j\omega}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega/2}$:

$$s = \frac{2}{T}\,\frac{e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}}{e^{j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega/2}}$$

Recognize the Euler identities in numerator ($\sin$) and denominator ($\cos$):

$$s = \frac{2}{T}\,\frac{2j\sin(\omega/2)}{2\cos(\omega/2)} = j\,\frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

Setting $s = j\Omega$ and equating imaginary parts:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

This is the **pre-warping equation**. It tells us that if we want a digital cutoff $\omega_c$, we must first design the analog filter for $\Omega_c = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega_c/2)$, because the bilinear transformation will *non-linearly compress* the analog frequency axis.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Specification Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A digital LPF has passband ripple $A_p = 1$ dB and stopband attenuation $A_s = 40$ dB. Find the absolute ripple values $\delta_p$ and $\delta_s$.

**Step 1:** Apply the absolute-to-relative inversion formula for passband:

$$\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20} = 1 - 10^{-1/20}$$

Compute the exponent: $-1/20 = -0.05$

$$10^{-0.05} = 0.8913$$

Therefore:

$$\delta_p = 1 - 0.8913 = 0.1087$$

**Step 2:** Apply the inversion for stopband:

$$\delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20} = 10^{-40/20} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$$

**Interpretation:** The passband amplitude can vary between $1 - 0.1087 = 0.8913$ and $1.0$ (about 8.7% sag), while the stopband amplitude must be at most $0.01$ (i.e., signals in the stopband are attenuated by at least $40$ dB).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Order $N$ on Filter Shape

| Order $N$ | Passband Flatness | Stopband Attenuation | Transition Steepness | Computational Load |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N = 2$ | Poor | Low | Gentle | Minimal |
| $N = 4$ | Moderate | Moderate | Steeper | Low |
| $N = 6$ | Good | High | Sharp | Moderate |
| $N = 10+$ | Excellent | Very High | Near-brick-wall | High |

> [!TIP]
> **Designer's Trade-off Insight:** Doubling the filter order roughly doubles the roll-off steepness (in dB/octave) for Butterworth filters, but also doubles the computational cost and group delay. KTU problems often ask for the *minimum* $N$ that just satisfies specs — never over-design.

### 3.4 Frequency Response Verification Equation

For a designed digital filter, the magnitude response is computed by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k e^{-j\omega k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k e^{-j\omega k}} \right\vert$$

This is the equation the student must plot (or sample at $\omega_p$ and $\omega_s$) to verify that the designed filter meets specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** After designing, ALWAYS plug $z = e^{j\omega_p}$ into the final $H(z)$ to check the passband response. Many students skip this verification and lose the "verification" sub-mark.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Filter Design Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([User Requirements]) --> specB[Translate to Specs]
    specB --> freqC{Choose Filter Type}
    freqC -- IIR Needed --> protoD[Select Analog Prototype]
    protoD --> orderE[Calculate Minimum Order N]
    orderE --> warpF[Apply Pre-warping]
    warpF --> billinG[Bilinear Transform]
    billinG --> hzH[Obtain H of z]
    freqC -- FIR Needed --> winI[Choose Window Method]
    winI --> firOrdJ[Compute FIR Order N]
    firOrdJ --> firH[K[Obtain H of z]
    hzH --> verifyK[Verify Magnitude Response]
    firH --> verifyK
    verifyK --> meetL{Specs Met?}
    meetL -- Yes --> realizeM[Realize in Direct Cascade or Parallel Form]
    meetL -- No --> adjustN[Increase N or Refine Type]
    adjustN --> freqC
    realizeM --> doneO([Final Digital Filter])

    classDef decision fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000

    class freqC,meetL decision
    class specB,protoD,orderE,warpF,billinG,hzH,winI,firOrdJ,firH,verifyK,realizeM,adjustN process
    class startA,doneO terminal
```

### 4.2 Specification Diagram — Magnitude Response of a Practical LPF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisX[Omega axis 0 to pi] --> lowA[0]
    lowA --> wpB[Omega p]
    wpB --> wsC[Omega s]
    wsC --> piD[pi]

    subgraph PASSBAND
        A1[Upper Limit equals 1 plus delta p] 
        A2[Lower Limit equals 1 minus delta p]
    end

    subgraph TRANSITION_BAND
        B1[Monotonic Roll off from 1 minus delta p to delta s]
    end

    subgraph STOPBAND
        C1[Upper Limit equals delta s]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
```

### 4.3 Direct Form I Realization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpX[X of n] --> addS1((+))
    inpX --> d1a[z power minus 1]
    d1a --> d1b[z power minus 1]
    d1a --> mulB0[Multiply by b sub 0]
    inpX --> mulB0
    mulB0 --> addS1
    addS1 --> outY[Y of n]
    addS1 --> mulA1[Multiply by minus a sub 1]
    mulA1 --> fb1((+))
    fb1 --> addS1
    outY --> d2a[z power minus 1]
    d2a --> mulA2[Multiply by minus a sub 2]
    mulA2 --> fb1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Mermaid Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix (e.g., `addS1`, `mulA1`), no reserved keywords are used as node names, and all labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting or special symbols that would break rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

Define the following terms with respect to digital filter specifications:
(a) Passband ripple $\delta_p$
(b) Stopband attenuation $A_s$
(c) Transition width $\Delta\omega$

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Passband ripple $\delta_p$:** It is the maximum allowable deviation of the magnitude response from the ideal value (unity, in a normalized LPF) within the passband $0 \leq \omega \leq \omega_p$. Mathematically, $1 - \delta_p \leq \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \leq 1$. It quantifies how "wobbly" the passband is allowed to be.

(b) **Stopband attenuation $A_s$:** It is the minimum guaranteed attenuation (in decibels) provided to all frequency components lying in the stopband $\omega_s \leq \omega \leq \pi$. It is related to stopband ripple by $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$. A value of $40$ dB means the stopband signal is reduced to $1/100$ of its original amplitude.

(c) **Transition width $\Delta\omega$:** It is the bandwidth of the transition band, computed as $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$. It is the practical "buffer" between passband and stopband, because a realizable filter cannot have a vertical brick-wall cutoff.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

Compare the phase response characteristics of IIR and FIR digital filters. Under what application condition is FIR strictly preferred over IIR?

**Model Answer:**

**IIR Filter Phase Response:** IIR filters have a **non-linear phase response** in general. The phase is not a linear function of $\omega$, leading to **phase distortion** of signals with frequency components spread across the passband. Different frequencies are delayed by different amounts.

**FIR Filter Phase Response:** FIR filters can be designed to have **strictly linear phase** (constant group delay) by enforcing symmetry in the impulse response coefficients: $h(n) = h(M-n)$ for even symmetry or $h(n) = -h(M-n)$ for odd symmetry. Group delay $\tau = M/2$ is a constant.

**Application Condition for FIR Preference:** When the application requires **linear phase** to avoid phase distortion — e.g., **data communication modems, digital audio crossover networks, biomedical signal processing (ECG/EEG)** where waveform shape preservation is critical — the FIR filter is the mandatory choice.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the general steps involved in the design of an IIR digital filter using the bilinear transformation method. List the advantages and limitations of this method. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A digital low-pass filter is required to meet the following specifications: passband edge $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample, stopband edge $\omega_s = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample, passband ripple $A_p = 1$ dB, stopband attenuation $A_s = 20$ dB, with sampling period $T = 1$ s. Determine the pre-warped analog frequencies $\Omega_p$ and $\Omega_s$ and convert the dB specifications to absolute ripple values. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Specification:** Define $\omega_p, \omega_s, A_p, A_s$ in the digital domain.

**Step 2 — Pre-warping:** Convert digital edge frequencies $\omega_p, \omega_s$ to analog prototype frequencies $\Omega_p, \Omega_s$ using $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$.

**Step 3 — Prototype Selection:** Choose the analog prototype type (Butterworth for maximally flat, Chebyshev for sharper roll-off, etc.) and compute the order $N$.

**Step 4 — Analog Design:** Obtain the analog transfer function $H_a(s)$ for the prototype.

**Step 5 — Bilinear Transformation:** Apply $s = \frac{2}{T}\frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$ to $H_a(s)$ to obtain the digital transfer function $H(z)$.

**Step 6 — Realization & Verification:** Realize the filter and verify $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ meets specs at $\omega_p$ and $\omega_s$.

**Advantages of Bilinear Transformation:**
- Stable analog filter maps to stable digital filter (no aliasing).
- One-to-one mapping — no spectral overlap.
- Straightforward algebraic substitution.

**Limitations:**
- Non-linear frequency warping requires pre-warping of critical frequencies.
- Phase response is highly non-linear (warping distorts phase).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$, $\omega_s = 0.4\pi$, $A_p = 1$ dB, $A_s = 20$ dB, $T = 1$ s.

**Step 1 — Pre-warp the passband edge:**

$$\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right) = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{0.2\pi}{2}\right) = 2\tan(0.1\pi)$$

Compute $0.1\pi = 0.3142$ rad, $\tan(0.3142) = 0.3249$.

$$\Omega_p = 2 \times 0.3249 = 0.6498 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Pre-warp the stopband edge:**

$$\Omega_s = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_s}{2}\right) = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{0.4\pi}{2}\right) = 2\tan(0.2\pi)$$

Compute $0.2\pi = 0.6283$ rad, $\tan(0.6283) = 0.7265$.

$$\Omega_s = 2 \times 0.7265 = 1.4531 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert $A_p$ to absolute ripple $\delta_p$:**

$$\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20} = 1 - 10^{-1/20} = 1 - 10^{-0.05}$$

$10^{-0.05} = 0.8913$, hence:

$$\delta_p = 1 - 0.8913 = 0.1087$$

**Step 4 — Convert $A_s$ to absolute ripple $\delta_s$:**

$$\delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20} = 10^{-20/20} = 10^{-1} = 0.1$$

**Final Answer:** $\Omega_p = 0.6498$ rad/s, $\Omega_s = 1.4531$ rad/s, $\delta_p = 0.1087$, $\delta_s = 0.1$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat magnitude-response diagram, explain the absolute and relative specifications of a digital low-pass filter. Define all relevant parameters. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the criteria for choosing between IIR and FIR digital filters for a given application. Justify your answer with two real-world examples for each type. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ of a practical digital LPF has four key regions:

1. **Passband** $0 \leq \omega \leq \omega_p$: The response is constrained to be within $[1 - \delta_p, 1]$.
2. **Transition band** $\omega_p \leq \omega \leq \omega_s$: The response rolls off monotonically (no strict bound, but should be smooth).
3. **Stopband** $\omega_s \leq \omega \leq \pi$: The response is bounded above by $\delta_s$.
4. **Peak gain** at $\omega = 0$ is normalized to $1$ (or $0$ dB in relative terms).

**Absolute Specifications** are stated as direct magnitudes:

$$1 - \delta_p \leq \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \leq 1 \quad \text{for } 0 \leq \omega \leq \omega_p$$
$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \leq \delta_s \quad \text{for } \omega_s \leq \omega \leq \pi$$

**Relative Specifications** are stated in decibels:

$$A_p = -20\log_{10}(1 - \delta_p) \quad \text{dB (peak-to-peak passband ripple)}$$
$$A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s) \quad \text{dB (minimum stopband attenuation)}$$

**Parameter Definitions:**
- $\omega_p$: passband edge (rad/sample or normalized $0$–$1$).
- $\omega_s$: stopband edge (rad/sample).
- $\delta_p$: passband tolerance (absolute).
- $\delta_s$: stopband tolerance (absolute).
- $A_p$: passband ripple in dB.
- $A_s$: stopband attenuation in dB.
- $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$: transition bandwidth.

The diagram shows a magnitude response curve that hovers between $1 - \delta_p$ and $1$ in the passband, smoothly drops through the transition region, and is bounded by $\delta_s$ in the stopband.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Selection Criterion | IIR Preferred | FIR Preferred |
|---|---|---|
| Phase linearity | Not required (e.g., audio bass/treble control) | Strictly required (e.g., music production, crossover networks) |
| Computational budget | Tight (e.g., low-power IoT sensor nodes) | Generous (e.g., offline image processing on PC) |
| Stability guarantee | Conditional — pole placement needed | Inherent — no feedback |
| Memory footprint | Small (few coefficients) | Large (many coefficients) |
| Design method | Indirect via analog prototype | Direct via window or frequency sampling |

**Real-World Examples:**

**IIR Filter Applications:**
1. **Mobile phone audio codecs (MP3/AAC playback):** Phase distortion is masked by the human ear's insensitivity to phase. Low-order IIR (8th order) suffices where a 64-tap FIR would be needed.
2. **Anti-aliasing filter in a digital oscilloscope:** A 4th-order Butterworth IIR is cheap and effective for signals where phase is not diagnostically critical.

**FIR Filter Applications:**
1. **Digital television (DTV) channel equalization:** Linear phase is essential to prevent inter-symbol interference (ISI) in the received data stream.
2. **ECG/EEG biomedical monitors:** The morphological shape of the QRS complex or alpha wave must be preserved exactly for diagnostic accuracy — FIR with linear phase is mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting Pre-warping:** In IIR design questions, students often directly substitute digital frequencies into the analog prototype formulas, ignoring $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$. This loses 2-3 marks immediately because the resulting filter will NOT meet the given digital specs — only the pre-warped analog specs.
> 2. **Confusing $\delta$ with $A$:** The ripple $\delta$ is a *linear magnitude* ($0$ to $1$), while attenuation $A$ is in *decibels* (positive number). Mixing them is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping Verification:** After deriving $H(z)$, you must plug in $z = e^{j\omega_p}$ and $z = e^{j\omega_s}$ to numerically check that the magnitude meets the bound. Marks are awarded for this verification.
> 4. **Ignoring "Linear Phase" keyword:** If a problem says "linear phase required", never answer IIR — the examiner will mark it zero because IIR cannot achieve linear phase in general.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Digital filter** = discrete-time LTI system described by difference equation or $H(z)$.
- [x] **Passband** = frequencies kept; **Stopband** = frequencies blocked; **Transition band** = buffer zone.
- [x] **$\delta_p$** is the linear passband ripple ($0$ to $1$); **$A_p$** is the same in dB (positive).
- [x] **$\delta_s$** is the linear stopband ripple; **$A_s$** is the stopband attenuation in dB.
- [x] **Conversion formulas:** $\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20}$ and $\delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20}$.
- [x] **Bilinear transformation** $s = \frac{2}{T}\frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$ maps stable analog filter to stable digital filter.
- [x] **Pre-warping** $\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega/2)$ is MANDATORY before designing the analog prototype.
- [x] **IIR** = lower order, non-linear phase, indirect design via analog prototype.
- [x] **FIR** = higher order, linear phase possible, direct design via windowing.
- [x] **Choose FIR** when linear phase is mandatory; **Choose IIR** when computational cost is critical.
- [x] **Butterworth** = maximally flat passband; **Chebyshev I** = passband ripple, sharper roll-off; **Chebyshev II** = stopband ripple; **Elliptic** = ripple in both, sharpest roll-off.
- [x] **Always verify** $\vert H(e^{j\omega_p}) \vert$ and $\vert H(e^{j\omega_s}) \vert$ after design.
- [x] **Transition width** $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ drives the filter order requirement.
- [x] **Brick-wall filters are unrealizable** — every practical filter has a transition band.
- [x] **Filter order $N$** must be an integer; always round UP the calculated $N$ to the next integer.
- [x] **Group delay** of linear-phase FIR = $M/2$ where $M = N - 1$ is the highest delay index.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causality and Its Implications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Causality and Its Implications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems**, **causality** is a fundamental physical realizability property. A system is defined as **causal** (also termed **non-anticipatory**) if its output $y[n]$ (or $y(t)$) at any arbitrary time index $n$ depends **solely** on the present input and a finite/infinite number of past input samples, but **never** on future input samples.

Mathematically, the KTU 2024 syllabus defines causality for a discrete-time LTI system as:

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = f\left(x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots, x[-\infty]\right)$$

For an LTI system, the equivalent impulse-response characterization is:

$$h[n] = 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad n < 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Causality for LTI Systems):**
> A continuous-time/discrete-time LTI system is causal **if and only if** its impulse response is a one-sided (right-sided) signal, i.e., $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ (continuous) or $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (discrete).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Realizability:** A causal system is one that can be implemented in real-time hardware. Non-causal systems, though mathematically valid, often require buffering or off-line processing (e.g., in image processing or seismic data analysis where bulk data is available beforehand).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **driving a car** on a highway. Your reaction to applying brakes depends entirely on what you *currently see* (a red light ahead) and what you *just saw* (the car in front suddenly slowing). You **cannot** react to something that will happen 10 seconds in the future — that would violate causality. Your brain's "input-output system" is causal.

In contrast, consider a **stock market analyst** looking at a 1-year chart: the analysis on Day 100 uses data from Day 101, 102, ..., 365 (future data). This is a **non-causal** system because the output relies on information that does not yet exist in real time.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** If the system "looks into the future" → **Non-Causal**. If the system "lives in the present" → **Causal**.

### 1.3 Causality in Different Domains

| Domain | Mathematical Condition | ROC Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Discrete-Time LTI | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | ROC is a right-half plane (exterior of outermost pole) |
| Continuous-Time LTI | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | ROC is right-half s-plane ($\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_{max}$) |

### 1.4 Causal vs. Anti-Causal vs. Non-Causal

- **Causal System:** Depends only on present and past inputs ($n \leq n_0$).
- **Anti-Causal System:** Depends only on future inputs ($n \geq n_0$).
- **Non-Causal System:** Depends on both past and future inputs (general case).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Theorem: Causality and the Impulse Response

For an LTI system, the output is the convolution of the input and the impulse response:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \, h[n-k]$$

Expanding the convolution explicitly:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{-1} x[k]\, h[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

The **first summation** involves $x[k]$ for $k < n$ (past and present). The **second summation** involves $x[k]$ for $k > n$ (future values, i.e., $k = n+1, n+2, \ldots$).

For the system to be **causal**, the contributions from $k > n$ must vanish. This is achieved if and only if $h[n-k] = 0$ for $k > n$, which means $h[m] = 0$ for all $m < 0$.

### 2.2 Region of Convergence (ROC) Implications

The Z-transform of a causal sequence $h[n]$ is:

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{+\infty} h[n]\, z^{-n}$$

Because the summation starts at $n=0$, $H(z)$ converges for all $z$ such that $\vert z \vert > R$, where $R$ is the radius of the outermost pole. Therefore:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a Causal LTI System:** The ROC is the **exterior of the outermost pole** (right-sided half plane in the z-plane). This ROC is a right-half open disc and **always excludes infinity** if the system is strictly proper.

### 2.3 The Triangle of Properties: Causality, Stability, and ROC

A frequently-tested KTU concept is the simultaneous analysis of three properties:

1. **Causality** → ROC is a right-half plane (exterior of outermost pole).
2. **Stability (BIBO)** → $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ → ROC **must include the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$).
3. **Pole Location** → Determines causality and stability jointly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Gold Theorem:** A causal and stable LTI system has **all its poles strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$). Conversely, an anti-causal stable system has all poles strictly **outside** the unit circle.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Causality & Implications)

| **#** | **Property** | **Mathematical Form** | **Unit/Remark** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Causality Condition (DT) | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Discrete-Time |
| 2 | Causality Condition (CT) | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | Continuous-Time |
| 3 | Convolution Output | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$ | Output of LTI |
| 4 | BIBO Stability | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolutely summable |
| 5 | ROC of Causal System | $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$ | Right-sided |
| 6 | ROC of Anti-Causal System | $\vert z \vert < \min_k \vert p_k \vert$ | Left-sided |
| 7 | Causal + Stable Poles | $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$ | All poles inside unit circle |
| 8 | Z-Transform of Causal h[n] | $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} h[n]\, z^{-n}$ | One-sided Z-transform |
| 9 | Differential Eq. (CT) | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \frac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k} = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \frac{d^k x(t)}{dt^k}$ | Causal if $N \geq M$ |
| 10 | Difference Eq. (DT) | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | Causal if $M \leq N$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Causal systems** are essential for real-time DSP applications: digital filters in hearing aids, radar signal processing, control systems in robotics, and audio codecs (MP3, AAC decoders).
- **Non-causal systems** find use in **offline processing**: image deblurring (using forward+backward filtering), geophysical seismic data interpretation, and post-recording audio restoration (noise reduction, de-reverberation).
- **Anti-causal systems** are realized by time-reversal tricks: $h_{anti}[n] = h[-n]$ for $n \geq 0$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Causality and One-Sided Impulse Response

**Statement to prove:** An LTI system is causal $\iff$ $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$.

**Proof (⇒ Direction — Causality implies one-sided h[n]):**

Assume the system is causal. Then the output $y[n]$ depends only on $x[k]$ for $k \leq n$. The convolution sum is:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

The general term in the sum is $x[k]\, h[n-k]$. For $y[n]$ to be independent of future inputs $x[k]$ with $k > n$, the coefficient of $x[k]$ for $k > n$ must equal zero. This coefficient is $h[n-k]$.

For $k > n$, let $m = n - k$, so $m < 0$. The condition becomes:

$$h[m] = 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad m < 0$$

This proves the forward direction. ∎

**Proof (⇐ Direction — One-sided h[n] implies causality):**

Assume $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. Substituting into the convolution:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

The substitution $m = n - k$ (so $k = n - m$, $dk = -dm$) transforms the limits: when $k = -\infty$, $m = +\infty$; when $k = +\infty$, $m = -\infty$.

$$y[n] = \sum_{m=+\infty}^{-\infty} x[n-m]\, h[m]\, (-1) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n-m]\, h[m]$$

Since $h[m] = 0$ for $m < 0$:

$$y[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{+\infty} x[n-m]\, h[m]$$

Substituting $r = n - m$ (so $m = n - r$): when $m = 0$, $r = n$; when $m = +\infty$, $r = -\infty$. Thus:

$$y[n] = \sum_{r=-\infty}^{n} x[r]\, h[n-r]$$

This shows $y[n]$ depends only on $x[r]$ for $r \leq n$ (i.e., present and past). Therefore, the system is causal. ∎

### 3.2 Derivation: ROC of a Causal LTI System is the Exterior of the Outermost Pole

Consider a causal LTI system with impulse response $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. The Z-transform is:

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{+\infty} h[n]\, z^{-n}$$

Suppose $H(z)$ is a rational function of the form $H(z) = \frac{N(z)}{D(z)}$, where $D(z) = \prod_{k=1}^{N}(z - p_k)$ and the poles are $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N$.

For the series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} h[n]\, z^{-n}$ to converge, we need $\lim_{n\to\infty} \vert h[n]\, z^{-n} \vert^{1/n} < 1$.

The natural mode of $h[n]$ that grows fastest is $p_{max}^{n}\, z^{-n}$ where $p_{max} = \max_k \vert p_k \vert$. For convergence:

$$\lim_{n\to\infty} \left(\frac{\vert p_{max} \vert^n}{\vert z \vert^n}\right)^{1/n} = \frac{\vert p_{max} \vert}{\vert z \vert} < 1$$

This requires $\vert z \vert > \vert p_{max} \vert$. Hence, the ROC is $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$, which is the exterior of the outermost pole — a right-half open disc. ∎

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Classify a System

**Problem:** Given an LTI system described by the difference equation:
$$y[n] = 0.5\, y[n-1] + x[n] + 0.3\, x[n-2]$$
Determine whether the system is causal, stable, and find the ROC.

**Solution Step 1: Find the Transfer Function H(z).**

Taking the Z-transform of both sides (assuming zero initial conditions / relaxed system):

$$Y(z) = 0.5\, z^{-1} Y(z) + X(z) + 0.3\, z^{-2} X(z)$$

Rearranging:

$$Y(z)\left[1 - 0.5 z^{-1}\right] = X(z)\left[1 + 0.3 z^{-2}\right]$$

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 + 0.3 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

**Solution Step 2: Find the Poles and Zeros.**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 + 0.3}{z^2 - 0.5 z} = \frac{z^2 + 0.3}{z(z - 0.5)}$$

- **Zeros:** $z^2 + 0.3 = 0 \Rightarrow z = \pm j\sqrt{0.3} \approx \pm j\, 0.5477$
- **Poles:** $z(z - 0.5) = 0 \Rightarrow p_1 = 0, \; p_2 = 0.5$

**Solution Step 3: Causality Check.**

The difference equation expresses $y[n]$ in terms of $y[n-1]$ (past output) and $x[n], x[n-2]$ (present and past input). No future values appear → **Causal**.

Equivalently, since the system is described by a difference equation, the impulse response is right-sided, so $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ → **Causal**.

**Solution Step 4: ROC Determination.**

The outermost pole is at $p_2 = 0.5$, so $\max_k \vert p_k \vert = 0.5$. Since the system is causal, the ROC is:

$$\text{ROC:} \quad \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

**Solution Step 5: Stability Check (BIBO).**

The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ is included in the ROC ($\because 1 > 0.5$). Both poles ($\vert 0 \vert = 0 < 1$ and $\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$) lie strictly inside the unit circle. Therefore, the system is **BIBO Stable**.

**Final Classification:** Causal ✓ | Stable ✓ | ROC: $\vert z \vert > 0.5$ ✓

### 3.4 Counter-Example: A Non-Causal System

Consider: $y[n] = x[n+1] - 0.5\, x[n]$.

Here, $y[n]$ requires $x[n+1]$, which is a **future** sample. Hence, the system is **non-causal** and cannot be implemented in real time. Its impulse response: setting $x[n] = \delta[n]$:

$$h[n] = \delta[n+1] - 0.5\, \delta[n]$$

This gives $h[-1] = 1 \neq 0$, confirming $h[n] \neq 0$ for some $n < 0$ → **non-causal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Whenever a system's difference equation contains $x[n+k]$ with $k > 0$ or $y[n+k]$ with $k > 0$, the system is **non-causal** by inspection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Causal LTI System Signal Flow

The following Mermaid diagram shows how a causal LTI system processes input samples in real time, using only present and past data:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Signal x n] --> B[Delay Line z inverse]
    B --> C1[x n delay 0]
    B --> C2[x n minus 1]
    B --> C3[x n minus 2]
    B --> C4[x n minus 3]
    C1 --> M1[Multiplier h 0]
    C2 --> M2[Multiplier h 1]
    C3 --> M3[Multiplier h 2]
    C4 --> M4[Multiplier h 3]
    M1 --> S1[Summing Junction]
    M2 --> S1
    M3 --> S1
    M4 --> S1
    S1 --> D[Output y n at time n]
    D --> O[Causal Output depends only on past and present]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The input $x[n]$ enters a **delay line** $z^{-1}$ (a chain of unit delays).
- Each tap of the delay line produces a past sample: $x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots$
- These are multiplied by the impulse response coefficients $h[0], h[1], h[2], \ldots$
- The summation produces $y[n]$ using **only** present and past inputs.

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the delay line moves **leftward in time** — the system never peeks at $x[n+1], x[n+2], \ldots$. This is the structural hallmark of a causal system.

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Decision Logic for Causal/Stable Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Given LTI System] --> B{Is h n nonzero for n less than 0}
    B -- Yes --> C1[NON CAUSAL]
    B -- No --> C2[CAUSAL]
    C2 --> D{Sum of absolute h n less than infinity}
    D -- Yes --> E1[STABLE]
    D -- No --> E2[UNSTABLE]
    C1 --> F{Does ROC include unit circle}
    F -- Yes --> G1[STABLE but non causal]
    F -- No --> G2[UNSTABLE and non causal]
    E1 --> H[Final Classification: Causal and Stable]
    E2 --> I[Final Classification: Causal but Unstable]
    G1 --> J[Final Classification: Non causal but Stable]
    G2 --> K[Final Classification: Non causal and Unstable]
```

### 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot: Causal + Stable System

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ZPlane["Z Plane Causal and Stable System"]
        A1[Unit Circle] --> A2[All poles strictly inside unit circle]
        A2 --> A3[ROC is exterior of outermost pole and contains unit circle]
    end
    ZPlane --> B1[Conclusion: System is Causal AND BIBO Stable]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The visual mnemonic: **"Inside is Stable, Outside is Causal-Right"** — Poles inside the unit circle imply stability; the ROC on the right (exterior) implies causality.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define causality for a discrete-time LTI system. State the necessary and sufficient condition on the impulse response $h[n]$ for the system to be causal.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A discrete-time LTI system is said to be **causal** if its output $y[n]$ at any time $n$ depends only on the present and past input samples, i.e., $x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots$, and not on any future input samples.

The necessary and sufficient condition for causality is that the impulse response $h[n]$ must be a **right-sided sequence**, that is:

$$h[n] = 0 \quad \text{for all} \quad n < 0$$

In other words, the system must be non-anticipatory, and physically realizable in real time. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the relationship between the Region of Convergence (ROC) of $H(z)$ and causality of an LTI system?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For an LTI system described by $H(z)$, the **causality** of the system is directly linked to the ROC of $H(z)$:

1. If the system is **causal**, the ROC is a right-half plane given by $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$, i.e., the **exterior of the outermost pole**.
2. If the ROC is of the form $\vert z \vert < \min_k \vert p_k \vert$, the system is **anti-causal**.
3. If the ROC is an annular region $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ (with both finite), the system is **non-causal (two-sided)**.

Thus, observing the ROC immediately tells us whether the LTI system is causal. **(3 marks)**

---

### 📗 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** A discrete-time LTI system is described by the transfer function:
$$H(z) = \frac{1 - 0.4\, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8\, z^{-1} + 0.15\, z^{-2}}$$

Determine whether the system is **causal** and **stable**. Justify your answer using the pole-zero pattern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a continuous-time LTI system with impulse response $h(t) = e^{-2t}\, u(t+1)$ (where $u(\cdot)$ is the unit step), determine whether the system is causal. Explain your reasoning. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$ to obtain the polynomial form:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 - 0.4 z}{z^2 - 0.8 z + 0.15}$$

**Step 2:** Find the poles by solving $z^2 - 0.8z + 0.15 = 0$.

Using the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{0.8 \pm \sqrt{0.64 - 0.60}}{2} = \frac{0.8 \pm \sqrt{0.04}}{2} = \frac{0.8 \pm 0.2}{2}$$

So, $p_1 = \frac{0.8 + 0.2}{2} = 0.5$ and $p_2 = \frac{0.8 - 0.2}{2} = 0.3$.

Both poles are real and positive: $p_1 = 0.5, \; p_2 = 0.3$.

**Step 3: Causality Determination.**

Since the system is given as a difference equation (proper rational form with $z^{-1}$ terms), the impulse response is right-sided. The ROC for a causal system is $\vert z \vert > 0.5$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Stability Determination.**

The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ is contained within the ROC ($1 > 0.5$), so the system is BIBO stable. Alternatively, since both poles satisfy $\vert p_1 \vert = 0.5 < 1$ and $\vert p_2 \vert = 0.3 < 1$, all poles lie inside the unit circle. **(2 marks)**

**Final Answer:** The system is **Causal and Stable**. The ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.5$. **(3 marks for full justification)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** Examine the impulse response $h(t) = e^{-2t}\, u(t+1)$.

Recall that the unit step function $u(t+1) = 1$ for $t \geq -1$ and $0$ for $t < -1$.

**Step 2:** Therefore:

$$h(t) = \begin{cases} e^{-2t} & \text{for } t \geq -1 \\ 0 & \text{for } t < -1 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Check if $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$.

For $-1 \leq t < 0$: $h(t) = e^{-2t} \neq 0$ (e.g., at $t = -0.5$, $h(-0.5) = e^{1} = 2.718$).

Since $h(t) \neq 0$ in the interval $[-1, 0)$, the impulse response is **not** strictly zero for $t < 0$.

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

The system is **Non-Causal** because the impulse response has non-zero values for some $t < 0$ (specifically in $-1 \leq t < 0$).

**Alternative interpretation:** A causal system would require $u(t)$, not $u(t+1)$. The "+1" shift allows the response to begin at $t = -1$, one unit of time **before** the present, which violates causality. **(3 marks for full reasoning)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Prove that an LTI system is causal if and only if its impulse response $h[n]$ is zero for all $n < 0$. Use the convolution sum in your proof. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A causal LTI system has the transfer function:
$$H(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.9 z^{-1})}$$

(i) Find the ROC. (ii) Determine if the system is stable. (iii) Compute $h[0]$ and $h[\infty]$ if $h[n]$ is the impulse response. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Proof:**

**(⇒) Necessity:** Suppose the system is causal. By definition, $y[n]$ depends only on $x[k]$ for $k \leq n$. Using convolution:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

For $k > n$, the term $x[k]$ is a future input. To ensure $y[n]$ is independent of $x[k]$ for $k > n$, the coefficient of $x[k]$ in the sum — namely $h[n-k]$ — must be zero. Letting $m = n - k$, for $k > n$ we have $m < 0$. Hence $h[m] = 0$ for all $m < 0$. **(3 marks)**

**(⇐) Sufficiency:** Suppose $h[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$. Then:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]\, h[n-k] + \sum_{k=n+1}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

The second sum contains $h[n-k]$ with $n-k < 0$, which is zero by hypothesis. Hence:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

This depends only on $x[k]$ for $k \leq n$ (present and past), so the system is causal. **(3 marks)**

**Conclusion:** An LTI system is causal **if and only if** $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Find the poles.**

The denominator is $(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.9z^{-1})$. Multiplying by $z^2/z^2$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.9)}$$

Poles: $p_1 = 0.5, \; p_2 = 0.9$. The outermost pole is at $\vert p_2 \vert = 0.9$.

**Step 2: (i) ROC.**

Since the system is causal, the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole:

$$\boxed{\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.9} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3: (ii) Stability.**

The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ satisfies $1 > 0.9$, so it is contained in the ROC. All poles ($\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$ and $\vert 0.9 \vert < 1$) lie inside the unit circle. Therefore, the system is **BIBO Stable**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4: (iii) Compute $h[0]$ and $h[\infty]$.**

Using partial fraction expansion:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.9z^{-1})} = \frac{A}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.9z^{-1}}$$

Multiplying both sides by the denominator:

$$1 = A(1 - 0.9z^{-1}) + B(1 - 0.5z^{-1})$$

Setting $z^{-1} = 1/0.5 = 2$: $1 = A(1 - 1.8) \Rightarrow A = \frac{1}{-0.8} = -1.25$.

Setting $z^{-1} = 1/0.9$: $1 = B(1 - 0.5/0.9) = B(1 - 0.5556) = B(0.4444) \Rightarrow B = 2.25$.

So:

$$H(z) = \frac{-1.25}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} + \frac{2.25}{1 - 0.9z^{-1}}$$

Inverse Z-transform (causal, $u[n]$):

$$h[n] = \left[-1.25\, (0.5)^n + 2.25\, (0.9)^n\right]\, u[n]$$

Now compute:

$$h[0] = -1.25 \cdot (0.5)^0 + 2.25 \cdot (0.9)^0 = -1.25 + 2.25 = \boxed{1.0} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

$$h[\infty] = \lim_{n\to\infty} \left[-1.25\, (0.5)^n + 2.25\, (0.9)^n\right] = 0 + 0 = \boxed{0} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

(The system is stable, so the impulse response decays to zero.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ROC with Causality alone:** A causal system has ROC $\vert z \vert > R$, but stability requires the unit circle to be **inside** this ROC. Don't conclude stability just from causality.
> 2. **Forgetting to check $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$:** Even if a system is "implementable" in code, presence of any $h[n] \neq 0$ for $n < 0$ makes it non-causal. Always check the impulse response.
> 3. **Time-shift confusion:** $u(t+1)$ means the step starts at $t = -1$ (one unit in the past), not at $t = +1$. Read step functions carefully.
> 4. **Sign error in pole computation:** When converting to polynomial form, multiply carefully. Sign mistakes in poles lead to wrong ROC.
> 5. **Ignoring the constraint that "causal" applies only to LTI systems:** For non-LTI systems, the condition $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ is **not equivalent** to causality. Causality is a property of the input-output relation, not the impulse response, in the non-LTI case.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Causality Definition:** Output depends only on present and past inputs (no future samples).
- ✅ **DT-LTI Causality Condition:** $h[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$.
- ✅ **CT-LTI Causality Condition:** $h(t) = 0$ for all $t < 0$.
- ✅ **ROC of a Causal System:** $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$ (exterior of the outermost pole).
- ✅ **ROC of an Anti-Causal System:** $\vert z \vert < \min_k \vert p_k \vert$ (interior of the innermost pole).
- ✅ **Causal + Stable:** All poles strictly inside the unit circle; ROC includes $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- ✅ **BIBO Stability:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ (absolutely summable impulse response).
- ✅ **Difference Equation Test:** System is causal if it expresses $y[n]$ in terms of $y[n-1], y[n-2], \ldots$ and $x[n], x[n-1], \ldots$ (no $x[n+k]$ with $k>0$).
- ✅ **Convolution Form:** $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]\, h[n-k]$ for causal systems.
- ✅ **Anti-Causal Trick:** Realize via time-reversal: $h_{causal}[n] = h_{anti}[-n]$ for $n \leq 0$.
- ✅ **Non-Causal Application:** Offline DSP (image processing, seismic analysis, post-recording audio).
- ✅ **Real-Time Constraint:** Causal systems are the only ones implementable in real-time hardware.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Practical Frequency Selective Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Practical Frequency Selective Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Practical Frequency Selective Filter** is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system that selectively allows signals of certain frequency bands to pass while attenuating (rejecting) signals of other frequency bands, with a *non-ideal, physically realizable* magnitude response. Unlike the mathematically idealized filter (which has brick-wall characteristics with unity gain in the passband, zero gain in the stopband, and zero-width transition band), a practical filter exhibits:

- A finite **transition band** between the passband and stopband.
- A bounded **passband ripple** ($\delta_p$).
- A non-zero **stopband attenuation** ($\delta_s$).
- A finite, frequency-dependent **roll-off rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Practical filters are classified into four canonical types — **Low-Pass (LPF)**, **High-Pass (HPF)**, **Band-Pass (BPF)**, and **Band-Stop (BSF)** filters. The digital implementation may use either **FIR** (Finite Impulse Response) or **IIR** (Infinite Impulse Response) structures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen sieve** that separates large flour particles from fine ones. The sieve has:

- A region where it lets through (passband),
- A region where it completely blocks (stopband),
- A **sloped edge** where it gradually blocks (transition band),
- Slight imperfections causing some flour to bounce back (ripple).

A practical filter behaves **exactly** like this sieve for frequency content of signals. The "particle size" is the signal's frequency — low frequencies (bass notes) sail through an LPF, high frequencies (treble notes) are attenuated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples:** Audio equalizers, ECG noise removal circuits, anti-aliasing filters in ADCs, channel selection filters in radios, and image smoothing/sharpening kernels.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The following metrics are universally used to characterize a practical filter and are **bold-highlighted** for KTU emphasis:

- **$\omega_p$** — Passband edge frequency (rad/sample).
- **$\omega_s$** — Stopband edge frequency (rad/sample).
- **$\delta_p$** — Maximum passband ripple (linear scale).
- **$\delta_s$** — Maximum stopband deviation (linear scale).
- **$A_p$ (dB)** — Peak-to-peak passband ripple in decibels.
- **$A_s$ (dB)** — Minimum stopband attenuation in decibels.
- **$\tau_g$** — Group delay (samples).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The relationship is always $\omega_p < \omega_s$ for low-pass and high-pass filters. For band-pass/band-stop, the lower and upper edges are paired accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical Filter Types

A practical frequency selective filter is classified by the frequency band it allows to pass:

| Filter Type | Passband | Stopband | Application |
|-------------|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | $0 \le \omega \le \omega_p$ | $\omega_s \le \omega \le \pi$ | Audio bass, anti-aliasing |
| **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | $\omega_p \le \omega \le \pi$ | $0 \le \omega \le \omega_s$ | Treble boost, DC removal |
| **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** | $\omega_{p1} \le \omega \le \omega_{p2}$ | $0 \le \omega \le \omega_{s1}$ and $\omega_{s2} \le \omega \le \pi$ | Radio channel selection |
| **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** | $0 \le \omega \le \omega_{p1}$ and $\omega_{p2} \le \omega \le \pi$ | $\omega_{s1} \le \omega \le \omega_{s2}$ | 50 Hz hum removal in ECG |

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Magnitude Response Tolarances

A practical filter's magnitude response $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ is bounded by **piecewise tolerance limits**:

**Passband Constraint** (for $0 \le \omega \le \omega_p$):

$$1 - \delta_p \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 1 + \delta_p$$

**Stopband Constraint** (for $\omega_s \le \omega \le \pi$):

$$0 \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \delta_s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Most KTU problems ask you to convert $\delta_p, \delta_s$ into decibel ripple values $A_p, A_s$. The decibel representation is what filter design packages (like MATLAB's `firpm` or `buttord`) require as input.

### 2.3 Decibel Conversions (High-Yield)

Converting linear ripples to dB form:

$$A_p = -20 \log_{10}\left(1 - \delta_p\right) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

$$A_s = -20 \log_{10}\left(\delta_s\right) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

### 2.4 Linear Phase Response

A filter is said to have **linear phase** if its phase response $\theta(\omega)$ is a linear function of $\omega$:

$$\theta(\omega) = -\alpha \omega + \beta$$

where $\alpha$ is a constant (group delay) and $\beta$ is a constant phase offset. This implies:

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d\theta(\omega)}{d\omega} = \alpha = \text{constant}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linear phase is critical in **data transmission**, **image processing**, and **biomedical signal analysis** because it preserves the *relative timing* (and hence the waveform shape) of all frequency components. Non-linear phase causes **phase distortion**, where different frequencies arrive at different times, smearing sharp transitions.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $A_p = -20\log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$ | Passband ripple | dB |
| 2 | $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$ | Stopband attenuation | dB |
| 3 | $\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20}$ | Linear ripple from dB | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20}$ | Linear deviation from dB | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\theta(\omega) = -\alpha\omega + \beta$ | Linear phase condition | rad |
| 6 | $\tau_g(\omega) = -d\theta/d\omega = \alpha$ | Group delay | samples |
| 7 | $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ | Symmetric impulse (FIR) | — |
| 8 | $h(n) = -h(M-1-n)$ | Antisymmetric impulse (FIR) | — |
| 9 | $A = \sum \vert h(n) \vert$ | Absolute gain at $\omega=0$ | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Units to remember:** $A_p$ and $A_s$ are *always* in **decibels (dB)**. Frequencies $\omega_p, \omega_s$ are in **rad/sample** ranging from 0 to $\pi$. Group delay is in **samples**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Practical frequency selective filters are foundational in:

- **Telecommunications** (channel selection, multiplexing).
- **Audio Engineering** (equalizers, noise cancellation).
- **Biomedical** (ECG/EEG denoising, 50/60 Hz notch).
- **Image Processing** (Gaussian smoothing, edge detection).
- **Control Systems** (anti-aliasing before sampling, reconstruction after).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Passband Ripple in Decibels

**Starting point:** The peak deviation in the passband is $\delta_p$, and the ideal gain is 1.

The minimum value of $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ in the passband is $(1 - \delta_p)$.

**Step 1:** Express the linear minimum gain in absolute terms.

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert_{min} = 1 - \delta_p$$

**Step 2:** Convert to decibels using the standard definition:

$$X_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{X}{X_{ref}}\right)$$

Taking the reference as 1 (ideal passband gain):

$$A_p = 20 \log_{10}(1) - 20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$$

**Step 3:** Since $20 \log_{10}(1) = 0$:

$$A_p = -20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$$

**Final form:**

$$A_p = -20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p) \quad \text{dB}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stopband Attenuation in Decibels

**Starting point:** The maximum value of $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ in the stopband is $\delta_s$.

**Step 1:** Express the linear maximum gain in the stopband.

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert_{max}^{stop} = \delta_s$$

**Step 2:** The reference is still 1 (unity in the passband). So the attenuation (in dB) is:

$$A_s = 20 \log_{10}(1) - 20 \log_{10}(\delta_s)$$

**Step 3:** Simplifying:

$$A_s = -20 \log_{10}(\delta_s) \quad \text{dB}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Phase Condition for FIR Filters

A FIR filter has impulse response $h(n)$ of length $M$, where $n = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$.

**Step 1:** The frequency response is the DTFT of $h(n)$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $n \rightarrow M-1-n$ in the sum (variable change):

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(M-1-n) e^{-j\omega (M-1-n)}$$

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(M-1)} \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(M-1-n) e^{j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** If we impose the **symmetry condition** $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(M-1)} \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(n) e^{j\omega n}$$

**Step 4:** Notice that the summation is the complex conjugate of the original $H(e^{j\omega})$ when $h(n)$ is real:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(n) e^{j\omega n} = H^*(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(M-1)} H^*(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 6:** Writing $H(e^{j\omega}) = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert e^{j\theta(\omega)}$:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert e^{j\theta(\omega)} = e^{-j\omega(M-1)} \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert e^{-j\theta(\omega)}$$

**Step 7:** Equating the phases:

$$\theta(\omega) = -\omega(M-1) + \theta(\omega) - 2\theta(\omega)$$

Wait — let me re-do this carefully. Equating only the phase terms (since the magnitudes cancel):

$$\theta(\omega) = -\omega(M-1) - \theta(\omega)$$

$$2\theta(\omega) = -\omega(M-1)$$

$$\theta(\omega) = -\omega \cdot \frac{M-1}{2}$$

**Final Result:** This is a linear function of $\omega$ with slope $\alpha = (M-1)/2$.

$$\boxed{\theta(\omega) = -\left(\frac{M-1}{2}\right) \omega}$$

**Group delay:**

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d\theta(\omega)}{d\omega} = \frac{M-1}{2} \quad \text{samples}$$

This is a **constant**, confirming linear phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Group delay is constant and equals $(M-1)/2$** for a symmetric FIR filter of length $M$. This is the canonical result KTU examiners expect.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A low-pass FIR filter has specifications: $\delta_p = 0.05$, $\delta_s = 0.01$. Compute $A_p$ and $A_s$ in dB.

**Step 1:** Compute $A_p$:

$$A_p = -20 \log_{10}(1 - 0.05) = -20 \log_{10}(0.95)$$

$$\log_{10}(0.95) = -0.02228$$

$$A_p = -20 \times (-0.02228) = 0.4453 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $A_s$:

$$A_s = -20 \log_{10}(0.01) = -20 \times (-2) = 40 \text{ dB}$$

**Answer:** $A_p \approx 0.45$ dB, $A_s = 40$ dB. ✅

---

### 3.5 Inverse Conversion Example

**Problem:** Given $A_p = 1$ dB and $A_s = 50$ dB, find $\delta_p$ and $\delta_s$.

**Step 1:** Solve for $\delta_p$:

$$1 = -20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$$

$$-0.05 = \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$$

$$1 - \delta_p = 10^{-0.05} = 0.8913$$

$$\delta_p = 0.1087$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\delta_s$:

$$50 = -20 \log_{10}(\delta_s)$$

$$-2.5 = \log_{10}(\delta_s)$$

$$\delta_s = 10^{-2.5} = 0.00316$$

**Answer:** $\delta_p \approx 0.1087$, $\delta_s \approx 0.00316$. ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Filter Specification

This diagram maps the **interactions** between the major design parameters of a practical filter, showing how each specification flows into the design algorithm and ultimately produces an FIR/IIR filter structure.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Specification Block] --> B[Passband Edge omega_p]
    A --> C[Stopband Edge omega_s]
    A --> D[Passband Ripple Ap dB]
    A --> E[Stopband Attenuation As dB]
    A --> F[Sampling Rate Fs]
    B --> G[Design Algorithm Core]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Filter Type Selector}
    H --> I[FIR Design Window Method]
    H --> J[FIR Design Frequency Sampling]
    H --> K[IIR Butterworth]
    H --> L[IIR Chebyshev]
    H --> M[IIR Elliptic]
    I --> N[Filter Coefficients h of n]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    N --> O[Realization Structure]
    O --> P[Direct Form I]
    O --> Q[Direct Form II]
    O --> R[Cascade Form]
    O --> S[Parallel Form]
    P --> T[Final Filter Output y of n]
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    S --> T
    G --> U[Validation Block]
    N --> U
    U --> V{Meets Specs?}
    V -->|Yes| W[Accept Filter]
    V -->|No| X[Adjust Order N and Iterate]
    X --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. The **Input Specification Block** collects all user-defined parameters ($\omega_p, \omega_s, A_p, A_s, F_s$).
2. The **Design Algorithm Core** processes these inputs and the **Filter Type Selector** chooses between FIR and IIR families.
3. The output is a set of **Filter Coefficients h(n)** that the **Realization Structure** converts into a hardware/software implementation.
4. The **Validation Block** checks whether the designed filter meets the original specifications. If not, the order $N$ is increased and the design is iterated.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Magnitude Response Schematic

This diagram illustrates the magnitude response $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ of a **practical low-pass filter**, with all characteristic regions clearly demarcated.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Omega equals 0] -->|Passband 0 to omega_p| B[Gain 1 plus or minus delta_p]
    B -->|Transition Band omega_p to omega_s| C[Sloped Roll Off Region]
    C -->|Stopband omega_s to pi| D[Gain less than delta_s]
    E[Vertical Axis Gain] -->|Up to 1 plus delta_p| B
    E -->|Down to delta_s| D
    F[Horizontal Axis Omega] -->|0 to pi| A
    F -->|pi| D
    B --> G[Passband Ripple Ap dB]
    D --> H[Stopband Attenuation As dB]
    C --> I[Transition Width BW equals omega_s minus omega_p]
    I --> J[Rolloff Rate dB per decade]
```

**Key takeaways from the diagram:**

- The **passband** lies in $0 \le \omega \le \omega_p$ with bounded ripple $\pm \delta_p$.
- The **transition band** is the sloped region $\omega_p < \omega < \omega_s$ — its *width* determines the rolloff sharpness.
- The **stopband** lies in $\omega_s \le \omega \le \pi$ with maximum gain $\delta_s$.
- A narrower transition band (smaller $\omega_s - \omega_p$) requires a **higher filter order** $N$, which means more computation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define the terms **passband ripple** ($\delta_p$) and **stopband attenuation** ($\delta_s$) of a practical digital filter. Why are they specified in decibels in practical design?

**Model Answer:**

**Passband ripple ($\delta_p$):** It is the maximum allowable deviation of the filter's magnitude response from the ideal unity gain in the passband region $0 \le \omega \le \omega_p$. Mathematically, the response is bounded as:

$$1 - \delta_p \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 1 + \delta_p$$

**Stopband attenuation ($\delta_s$):** It is the maximum allowable magnitude of the filter's response in the stopband region $\omega_s \le \omega \le \pi$. Mathematically:

$$0 \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \delta_s$$

**Why decibels?** Decibel representation allows specifications to be expressed as small, manageable numbers (e.g., 0.5 dB ripple, 50 dB attenuation) rather than tiny linear quantities. It is also additive for cascaded filter stages, simplifying system-level analysis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
What is **linear phase**? Why is it important in FIR filter design?

**Model Answer:**

A filter has **linear phase** when its phase response $\theta(\omega)$ is a linear function of frequency $\omega$:

$$\theta(\omega) = -\alpha \omega + \beta$$

where $\alpha$ is the constant group delay and $\beta$ is a constant phase offset.

**Importance:**

1. **No phase distortion** — All frequency components are delayed by the same amount $\alpha = (M-1)/2$, preserving the waveform shape.
2. **Constant group delay** — Critical in data transmission, image processing, and biomedical applications where timing fidelity is essential.
3. **Easily realizable in FIR filters** — Achieved by imposing symmetry $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ or antisymmetry $h(n) = -h(M-1-n)$ on the impulse response.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question 1(A) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for passband ripple $A_p$ and stopband attenuation $A_s$ in decibels, in terms of the linear parameters $\delta_p$ and $\delta_s$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the magnitude response characteristics of a practical low-pass FIR filter with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the significance of the transition band. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to **SECTION 3, Derivations 1 and 2** of these notes. The final results are:

$$A_p = -20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p) \quad \text{dB}$$

$$A_s = -20 \log_{10}(\delta_s) \quad \text{dB}$$

(Students must show the three derivation steps each: starting from the linear constraint, applying the dB conversion formula, and simplifying.)

**(b)** **Magnitude Response Characteristics of Practical LPF:**

The magnitude response of a practical LPF consists of three regions:

1. **Passband** ($0 \le \omega \le \omega_p$): The response oscillates between $1 - \delta_p$ and $1 + \delta_p$, ideally close to unity. The maximum deviation is $A_p$ dB.

2. **Transition band** ($\omega_p < \omega < \omega_s$): The response gradually decreases from $1 + \delta_p$ to $\delta_s$. The width is $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$.

3. **Stopband** ($\omega_s \le \omega \le \pi$): The response stays below $\delta_s$, achieving attenuation of at least $A_s$ dB.

**Significance of transition band:**

- A **narrow** transition band indicates a **sharper roll-off**, which means better frequency selectivity.
- However, narrower transition bands require **higher filter order** $N$, increasing computational cost and group delay.
- A wide transition band allows lower-order filters but with poorer selectivity.
- The choice of transition width is a **design trade-off** between performance and complexity.

(Include the Mermaid block from **SECTION 4.2** as the labelled diagram.)

---

#### **OR Question 1(B) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the four types of practical frequency selective filters and their magnitude response characteristics. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the condition for linear phase in an FIR filter. Show that the group delay is constant and equal to $(M-1)/2$ for a symmetric impulse response. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Four Types of Practical Frequency Selective Filters** (refer to SECTION 2.1 for full table):

1. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Passes low frequencies, attenuates high frequencies. Passband: $0 \le \omega \le \omega_p$; Stopband: $\omega_s \le \omega \le \pi$.

2. **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Passes high frequencies, attenuates low frequencies. Passband: $\omega_p \le \omega \le \pi$; Stopband: $0 \le \omega \le \omega_s$.

3. **Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Passes a specific band. Passband: $\omega_{p1} \le \omega \le \omega_{p2}$.

4. **Band-Stop Filter (BSF):** Rejects a specific band (e.g., 50 Hz notch). Stopband: $\omega_{s1} \le \omega \le \omega_{s2}$.

For each, sketch the magnitude response with passband ripple $A_p$, stopband attenuation $A_s$, and transition bands.

(Use the canonical LPF diagram as a template, then describe the others verbally.)

**(b)** Refer to **SECTION 3, Derivation 3** for the full algebraic derivation.

**Key result:**

$$\theta(\omega) = -\left(\frac{M-1}{2}\right) \omega$$

**Group delay:**

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d\theta}{d\omega} = \frac{M-1}{2} \quad \text{samples (constant)}$$

Since $\tau_g$ does not depend on $\omega$, all frequency components experience the same delay, and the filter exhibits **linear phase**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error in dB formula:** Students often write $A_p = 20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$ (positive sign) — this is **wrong**. The correct expression has a **negative sign** because $1 - \delta_p < 1$, and we want $A_p > 0$.
> 2. **Confusing $\delta_p$ with $A_p$:** $\delta_p$ is *linear* (dimensionless, between 0 and 1), $A_p$ is in *dB* (positive, usually 0.1–3 dB). Many students interchange them in problems.
> 3. **Forgetting the minus sign in phase:** Linear phase is $\theta(\omega) = -\alpha\omega + \beta$, **not** $+\alpha\omega$. The negative sign indicates a *delay* (output lags input).
> 4. **Misapplying symmetry condition:** $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ requires both even length cases (Type I/II) and odd length (Type III/IV) to be handled separately. Writing the condition without specifying length $M$ is incomplete.
> 5. **Forgetting the transition band:** Many sketches show an ideal brick-wall filter. KTU examiners explicitly want the *transition band* drawn and labelled with width $\omega_s - \omega_p$.
> 6. **Unit errors:** $A_p, A_s$ are in **dB**, not dBm or watts. $\omega_p, \omega_s$ are in **rad/sample**, not Hz (unless $F_s$ is given).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Practical filters differ from ideal filters** by having a non-zero transition band, passband ripple, and finite stopband attenuation.
- **Four canonical types:** LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF — each defined by its passband and stopband regions.
- **Passband tolerance:** $1 - \delta_p \le |H| \le 1 + \delta_p$ for $\omega \in [0, \omega_p]$.
- **Stopband tolerance:** $0 \le |H| \le \delta_s$ for $\omega \in [\omega_s, \pi]$.
- **Key dB formulas:**
  - $A_p = -20 \log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$
  - $A_s = -20 \log_{10}(\delta_s)$
  - **Inverse:** $\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20}$, $\delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20}$
- **Linear phase condition** for FIR: $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ (symmetric) or $h(n) = -h(M-1-n)$ (antisymmetric).
- **Group delay** for symmetric FIR: $\tau_g = (M-1)/2$ samples (constant).
- **Phase equation:** $\theta(\omega) = -\alpha\omega + \beta$ where $\alpha = (M-1)/2$.
- **Transition width** $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ is the trade-off parameter between filter order and selectivity.
- **Design trade-off:** Narrower transition band → sharper roll-off → higher filter order → more computation and higher group delay.
- **Applications:** Audio, biomedical (ECG/EEG), telecommunications, image processing, control systems.
- **Unit reminders:** $A_p, A_s$ in **dB**; $\omega$ in **rad/sample**; $\tau_g$ in **samples**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IIR Filter Design: Discrete Time IIR Filter from Analog Filter (Butterworth), IIR Filter (LPF, BRF) Design by Impulse Invariance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IIR Filter Design: Discrete Time IIR Filter from Analog Filter (Butterworth), IIR Filter (LPF, BRF) Design by Impulse Invariance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is IIR Filter Design?

**Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filter design** is the process of synthesizing a discrete-time, causal, stable, linear time-invariant system $H(z)$ whose impulse response $h(n)$ is of infinite duration, by leveraging the rich, well-established theory of continuous-time (analog) filter design.

Mathematically, an IIR filter is described by the rational transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$

where $N \ge 1$ and the recursive (feedback) coefficients $a_k$ are responsible for the infinite-duration response.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike FIR filters (which require only $a_k = 0$ and always stable), IIR filters achieve a given magnitude specification with a **far lower filter order $N$**, which is their key advantage in real-time embedded applications.

## 1.2 The "Why" Behind Designing IIR Filters from Analog Prototypes

Direct design of digital IIR filters in the $z$-domain is mathematically cumbersome because the $z$-plane is not symmetric in a way that easily yields magnitude-squared functions. The analog $s$-plane, however, has been exhaustively studied for nearly a century. Hence, the **indirect approach** is used:

1. Design a well-known analog filter (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic, Bessel).
2. Transform it into a digital filter using a stable mapping $s \to z$.

This is the **"three-step" KTU paradigm**:

$$\text{Specs} \;\longrightarrow\; H_a(s) \;\longrightarrow\; H(z)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):** *IIR filter design by transformation* is the technique of obtaining the digital transfer function $H(z)$ from a continuous-time transfer function $H_a(s)$ using either the **Impulse Invariance Method** or the **Bilinear Transformation Method**, both of which preserve stability by mapping the left-half $s$-plane into the interior of the unit circle in $z$.

## 1.3 Butterworth Analog Filter – Intuitive Overview

A **Butterworth filter** is the analog filter whose magnitude response is **maximally flat** (no ripples) in both the passband and stopband.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a perfectly smooth hill. A Butterworth filter is to filters what that smooth hill is to terrain — it has no bumps (ripples) at the top (passband) and no cliffs (sharp transitions) at the slope. The transition is gentle, like a butter-smooth ramp. Higher order $N$ makes the hill steeper, but the top stays flat.

The **magnitude-squared response** is:

$$|H_a(j\Omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

where:
- $\Omega_c$ = **cutoff (–3 dB) frequency** in rad/sec.
- $N$ = **order** of the filter (integer $\ge 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** $|H_a(j\Omega_c)| = 1/\sqrt{2}$, i.e., the cutoff is at the **-3 dB point** by definition. This is the standard KTU/ECE convention.

## 1.4 Impulse Invariance Method – Intuitive Overview

**Impulse Invariance** is the technique of designing a digital filter whose **impulse response $h(n)$ is exactly a sampled (scaled) version of the analog filter's impulse response $h_a(t)$**, i.e.,

$$h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT)$$

where $T$ is the sampling period.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine recording the echo of a bell struck in a concert hall with a microphone. The analog filter is the natural acoustic response. The impulse invariance method says: "sample the analog response at regular intervals, multiply each sample by $T$, and feed those digital samples into your digital processor." The digital filter will reproduce the analog echo at the sample points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Limitation:** Impulse invariance introduces **aliasing** of the analog frequency response because of periodic spectrum replication in the discrete domain. Therefore, it is **strictly suitable only for band-limited analog filters** (LPF and a limited class of BRF), and **not for HPF or wideband BPF** — a point that is tested repeatedly in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.5 Filter Types Covered in This Module

| Filter Type | Symbol | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Low-Pass Filter** | **LPF** | Passes $\Omega < \Omega_p$; rejects $\Omega > \Omega_s$ |
| **Band-Reject Filter** | **BRF** (Notch) | Rejects $\Omega_{s1} < \Omega < \Omega_{s2}$; passes the rest |

The design of LPF and BRF using **Butterworth analog prototype + Impulse Invariance** is the core KTU board problem in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage IIR Design Pipeline (KTU Board Notation)

For **any** analog-to-digital IIR filter design, the procedure is:

**Stage 1 — Specification Translation:** Convert the digital specifications $(\omega_p, \omega_s, \delta_p, \delta_s)$ into analog-domain pre-warped specifications. For impulse invariance (no warping), use $\Omega = \omega / T$.

**Stage 2 — Analog Prototype Design:** Determine the order $N$ and cutoff $\Omega_c$ of the Butterworth filter that meets the specs; then compute $H_a(s)$.

**Stage 3 — Discretization:** Apply the impulse invariance transformation to obtain $H(z)$.

## 2.2 Butterworth Analog Filter — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Magnitude-Squared Function

The Butterworth filter of order $N$ and cutoff $\Omega_c$ has:

$$|H_a(j\Omega)|^2 = H_a(s) H_a(-s) \Big|_{s = j\Omega} = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

### 2.2.2 Pole Locations

The poles of $|H_a(s)|^2$ lie on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the $s$-plane, equally spaced at angular intervals of $\pi/N$:

$$s_k = \Omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k + N - 1)/(2N)}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, 2N$$

For a **stable, causal** filter, we select the **$N$ poles in the Left-Half Plane (LHP)** (i.e., those with $\mathrm{Re}\{s_k\} < 0$).

### 2.2.3 Order Calculation (KTU Standard Form)

Given passband edge $\Omega_p$ with attenuation $\delta_p$ (often $A_p$ in dB) and stopband edge $\Omega_s$ with attenuation $\delta_s$ (often $A_s$ in dB):

$$N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right]}{2 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$$

The **3-dB cutoff** is then:

$$\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{\left(10^{0.1 A_p} - 1\right)^{1/(2N)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If only $A_p = 3$ dB is specified at $\Omega_p = \Omega_c$, then the simpler form $\Omega_c = \Omega_p$ is used.

## 2.3 Impulse Invariance — Core Mathematical Theory

### 2.3.1 The Mapping Principle

If $H_a(s)$ is expanded in **partial fractions**:

$$H_a(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{s - s_k}$$

then the impulse response is $h_a(t) = \sum_k R_k e^{s_k t} u(t)$.

Sampling at $t = nT$ and multiplying by $T$:

$$h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT) = T \sum_{k=1}^{N} R_k \left(e^{s_k T}\right)^n u(n)$$

Taking the $z$-transform:

$$H(z) = T \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Critical Formula:** Each analog pole $s_k$ maps to a digital pole $z_k = e^{s_k T}$ **inside** the unit circle (since $\mathrm{Re}\{s_k\} < 0$ ensures $\vert e^{s_k T}\vert < 1$), guaranteeing stability.

### 2.3.2 Frequency-Domain Interpretation

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\!\left(j\frac{\omega - 2\pi m}{T}\right)$$

The digital spectrum is the **periodic sum** of stretched analog spectra — i.e., **aliasing always occurs**. Hence, the analog filter $H_a(s)$ must be **strictly band-limited** to below $\pi/T$ rad/sec.

## 2.4 LPF Design by Impulse Invariance — Algorithm

1. Read digital specs: $\omega_p, \omega_s, A_p, A_s, T$.
2. Convert to analog: $\Omega_p = \omega_p / T$, $\Omega_s = \omega_s / T$.
3. Compute order $N$ and 3-dB cutoff $\Omega_c$ (Butterworth).
4. Find LHP poles $s_k$ of Butterworth prototype.
5. Express $H_a(s)$ in partial fractions with residues $R_k$.
6. Apply impulse invariance: $z_k = e^{s_k T}$, $\tilde{R}_k = T \cdot R_k$.

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{\tilde{R}_k}{1 - z_k \, z^{-1}}$$

7. Verify by plotting $|H(e^{j\omega})|$.

## 2.5 BRF Design by Impulse Invariance — Algorithm (Frequency Transformation Path)

The BRF cannot be obtained directly from a Butterworth prototype by impulse invariance because the analog BRF has multiple passbands. We use the **prototype-to-target frequency transformation**:

**Step A:** Convert digital BRF specs to analog BRF specs ($\Omega = \omega / T$).
**Step B:** Map the analog BRF specification to a **normalized analog LPF prototype** specification using the constant-$\Omega_s$ transformation:

$$\Omega_L = \frac{\Omega_0 \, B}{\Omega_0^2 - \Omega^2}$$

where $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_{p1}\Omega_{p2}} = \sqrt{\Omega_{s1}\Omega_{s2}}$ (geometric center) and $B = \Omega_{p2} - \Omega_{p1}$ (bandwidth).

**Step C:** Design Butterworth LPF prototype at $\Omega_L$ specs.
**Step D:** Apply inverse frequency transformation to get $H_a(s)$ for the analog BRF.
**Step E:** Apply impulse invariance to obtain $H(z)$.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Butterworth magnitude | $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}}$ | Maximally flat |
| 2 | Butterworth order | $N \ge \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\big[(10^{0.1 A_s}{-}1)/(10^{0.1 A_p}{-}1)\big]}{2 \log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ | Round up to next integer |
| 3 | 3-dB cutoff | $\Omega_c = \Omega_p / (10^{0.1 A_p}{-}1)^{1/(2N)}$ | Use $A_p=3$ dB if not given |
| 4 | Butterworth pole | $s_k = \Omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}$ | Keep LHP only ($k = 1,\dots,N$) |
| 5 | Impulse invariance | $H(z) = T \sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{R_k}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}}$ | $R_k$ = residue of $H_a$ at $s_k$ |
| 6 | Digital pole | $z_k = e^{s_k T}$ | Inside unit circle |
