| 7 | L₁ scaling factor | $A \le 1/\sum \vert h[k]\vert$ | Anti-overflow |
| 8 | L∞ scaling factor | $A \le 1/\max_k \vert h[k]\vert$ | Sinusoid worst case |
| 9 | Deadband width (1st-order IIR) | $\Delta \approx \dfrac{\vert a_2 \vert}{1-\vert a_2 \vert}\cdot q$ | Zero-input LCO |
| 10 | Overflow oscillation fix | Use **saturation** arithmetic | 2's-complement wrap-around → LCO |

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Round-Off Noise Variance (Uniform Distribution)

> **Goal:** Show $\sigma_e^2 = q^2/12$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the error distribution.** Assuming *rounding*, the quantization error $e$ lies in the symmetric interval:

$$-\frac{q}{2} \le e \le +\frac{q}{2}$$

with uniform probability density:

$$p(e) = \frac{1}{q} \quad \text{for } e \in [-q/2, +q/2]$$

**Step 2 — Mean of the error.**

$$\mu_e = \int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e \cdot p(e)\, de = \frac{1}{q}\!\int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e\, de$$

Since the integrand is odd over a symmetric interval:

$$\mu_e = 0$$

**Step 3 — Variance (= mean-square since mean is zero).**

$$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e^2 \cdot p(e)\, de = \frac{1}{q}\!\int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e^2\, de$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{q}\!\left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-q/2}^{+q/2}$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{q}\cdot\frac{1}{3}\!\left[\left(\frac{q}{2}\right)^3 - \left(-\frac{q}{2}\right)^3\right]$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{q}\cdot\frac{1}{3}\cdot 2\cdot\frac{q^3}{8}$$

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{q^2}{12} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation of FIR Output Noise Power

> **Goal:** Derive $\sigma_f^2 = N q^2/12$ for a length-$N$ FIR.

**Step 1 — Define the output equation.** Direct-form FIR:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k]\, x[n-k]$$

**Step 2 — Insert per-multiplier noise.** Each product $h[k]x[n-k]$ has its own independent quantizer with error $e_k[n]$:

$$\tilde{y}[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\bigl(h[k]x[n-k] + e_k[n]\bigr)$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the true output.**

$$\tilde{y}[n] - y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e_k[n] \;=\; f[n]$$

**Step 4 — Variance of the output noise.**

$$\sigma_f^2 = E\!\left\{\bigl(f[n]\bigr)^2\right\} = E\!\left\{\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} e_k[n]\,e_m[n]\right\}$$

Because the $e_k$ are **independent and zero-mean**:

$$E\{e_k e_m\} = \sigma_e^2\,\delta_{k-m}$$

$$\sigma_f^2 = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1}\sigma_e^2 = N\cdot\frac{q^2}{12} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation of IIR Output Noise Power (Direct Form II)

> **Goal:** Show $\sigma_f^2 = (q^2/12)\sum \vert h[k]\vert^2$.

**Step 1 — Error injection points.** In Direct Form II transposed, each multiplier injects an independent $e_k[n]$ immediately *at the recursive node*. The contribution of $e_k[n]$ to the output $\tilde{y}[n]$ is the impulse response $h[n]$ of the filter sampled appropriately.

**Step 2 — Output contribution of one noise source.** The contribution of $e_k[n]$ to $\tilde{y}[n]$ is:

$$f_k[n] = e_k[n] * h[n] = \sum_{m=0}^{n} h[m]\,e_k[n-m]$$

**Step 3 — Variance of one contribution.**

$$E\{f_k^2[n]\} = \sum_{m=0}^{n}\sum_{r=0}^{n} h[m]h[r]\,E\{e_k[n-m]e_k[n-r]\}$$

White-noise assumption $E\{e_k[i]e_k[j]\} = \sigma_e^2\delta_{i-j}$:

$$E\{f_k^2[n]\} = \sigma_e^2\sum_{m=0}^{n}\vert h[m]\vert^2$$

For a stable IIR, $n \to \infty$:

$$E\{f_k^2[\infty]\} = \sigma_e^2\sum_{m=0}^{\infty}\vert h[m]\vert^2 = \sigma_e^2\Vert h\Vert_2^2$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all $M$ multipliers** (independent):

$$\sigma_f^2 = M\cdot\sigma_e^2\Vert h\Vert_2^2 = M\cdot\frac{q^2}{12}\Vert h\Vert_2^2$$

For a *first-order section* ($M=1$), this reduces to the familiar $\sigma_f^2 = (q^2/12)\Vert h\Vert_2^2$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A first-order IIR filter $y[n] = 0.9\,y[n-1] + x[n]$ is implemented in 2's-complement fixed point with $b = 8$ fraction bits. Compute:
> (a) the round-off noise variance per multiplier,
> (b) the output noise power, and
> (c) the L₁ scaling factor required to prevent overflow for $|x[n]| \le 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Quantization step.**

$$q = 2^{-8} = \frac{1}{256} = 0.00390625$$

**Step 2 — Round-off variance per multiplier.**

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{q^2}{12} = \frac{(0.00390625)^2}{12} = \frac{1.5259\times 10^{-5}}{12} \approx 1.272\times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 3 — Output noise power.** Impulse response $h[n] = (0.9)^n u[n]$:

$$\Vert h\Vert_2^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(0.9)^{2n} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.81} = \frac{1}{0.19} \approx 5.263$$

$$\sigma_f^2 = 1.272\times 10^{-6} \times 5.263 \approx 6.69\times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — L₁ scaling factor.** $\Vert h\Vert_1 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(0.9)^n = 1/(1-0.9) = 10$:

$$A \le \frac{x_{\max}^{-1}}{\Vert h\Vert_1} = \frac{1/0.5}{10} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2$$

So the input must be attenuated by **0.2×** (i.e., gain $A=0.2$) before entering the filter to guarantee no overflow.

## 3.5 Worked Example — SQNR for Sinusoidal Input

> **Given:** $b=12$ bits, $N=10$ multipliers, $A_m = 0.5$ (peak amplitude).
> **Find:** SQNR in dB.

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02(12) + 1.76 + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3(0.5)^2}{10}\right)$$

$$= 72.24 + 1.76 + 10\log_{10}(0.075)$$

$$= 74.0 + 10(-1.1249)$$

$$= 74.0 - 11.249 \approx 62.75 \text{ dB}$$

## 3.6 Limit Cycle Calculation — Deadband Width

> **Filter:** $y[n] = a_1 y[n-1] + x[n]$ with $a_1 = 0.5$, $b=8$.
> **Find:** Deadband width.

**Step 1 — Quantization step.**

$$q = 2^{-8} = 0.00390625$$

**Step 2 — Deadband formula for 1st-order IIR.**

$$\Delta \approx \dfrac{\vert a_1 \vert}{1-\vert a_1 \vert}\cdot q = \frac{0.5}{0.5}\cdot 0.00390625 = 0.00390625$$

So any initial condition $\vert y[-1] \vert \le 0.0039$ will sustain a non-zero limit cycle instead of decaying to zero — a striking illustration of how round-off *freezes* an otherwise-stable pole.

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Quantization Error Injection in Direct Form II Transposed IIR

The following Mermaid block diagram shows *exactly* where each round-off error $e_k[n]$ enters the recursive loop, and how they all propagate to the output through the same impulse response $h[n]$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["x of n"] --> nodeA1["Multiplier 1"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeS1["Adder / Accumulator"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeD1["Delay z-1"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeA1
    nodeS1 --> nodeY["Output y of n"]
    nodeE1["e1 roundoff"] -.-> nodeA1
    nodeY --> nodeHbox["Filter to output\nh k impulse response"]
    nodeHbox --> nodeF["Total Output Noise\nsigma_f squared"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The outer loop (solid arrows) is the *ideal* IIR computation.
- The dashed arrow from $e_1$ (roundoff) to the multiplier is the *injected* quantization noise.
- All noise sources, regardless of injection point, are *filtered by the same $h[k]$* to reach the output — this is the geometric reason for the $\Vert h \Vert_2^2$ term in $\sigma_f^2$.

## 4.2 Architecture Flow — From FWL Effect to Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput["Infinite Precision\nInput x of n"] --> nodeMul["Multiplier"]
    nodeMul --> nodeQuant["Quantizer\nb fraction bits"]
    nodeQuant --> nodeAdd["Adder / Accumulator"]
    nodeAdd --> nodeFeed{"Overflow?"}
    nodeFeed -- Yes Saturation --> nodeSat["Clamp to plus or minus 1"]
    nodeFeed -- Yes Wrap-around --> nodeWrap["2s complement\nwrap"]
    nodeFeed -- No --> nodeOut["Output y of n"]
    nodeWrap --> nodeLCO["Overflow Limit Cycle\nlarge amplitude"]
    nodeSat --> nodeSafe["Stable output\nno LCO"]
    nodeQuant --> nodeRO["Roundoff Noise\nsigma_e squared = q squared over 12"]
    nodeRO --> nodeGranular["Granular Zero-Input LCO\namplitude less than q"]
    nodeOut --> nodeScale["Preventive: Signal Scaling\nA less than 1 over norm h"]
```

**Interpretation:**

- The *upper* branch (wrap-around) is the dangerous path that triggers large overflow oscillations.
- The *middle* branch (saturation) is the safe path — required for stable IIR design in 2's complement.
- The *lower* branch illustrates that even *without* overflow, product round-off creates granular limit cycles.
- Signal scaling (bottom) is the *preventive* measure applied **before** the input reaches the multiplier.

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Which Scaling Norm to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ["Input class?"] --> nodeSin{"Sinusoidal?"}
    nodeQ --> nodeBB{"Broadband\nrandom?"}
    nodeSin -- Yes --> nodeLinf["L infinity scaling\nA less than 1 over max h"]
    nodeBB -- Yes --> nodeLone["L one scaling\nA less than 1 over sum of absolute h"]
    nodeLinf --> nodeCheck{"Confirmed\nmax output less than 1?"}
    nodeLone --> nodeCheck
    nodeCheck -- No --> nodeReduce["Reduce A further\nthen amplify at output"]
    nodeCheck -- Yes --> nodeDone["Filter scaled and safe"]
```

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define round-off error in fixed-point DSP. With the standard white-noise assumptions, derive the variance of the round-off error for a $b$-bit fractional register.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Round-off error is the difference between the exact mathematical product $p[n] = a\cdot x[n]$ and its $(b+1)$-bit quantized representation $Q\{p[n]\}$, i.e., $e[n] = Q\{p[n]\} - p[n]$.

**Assumptions:** (i) $e[n]$ is uniformly distributed over $[-q/2, +q/2]$ where $q = 2^{-b}$, (ii) zero-mean, (iii) white, (iv) uncorrelated with $x[n]$.

**Variance derivation (1.5 marks):**

$$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-q/2}^{+q/2}\!e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{q}\,de = \frac{1}{q}\!\left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-q/2}^{+q/2} = \frac{q^2}{12}$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Hence $\boxed{\sigma_e^2 = \dfrac{q^2}{12} = \dfrac{2^{-2b}}{12}}$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**What is an overflow limit cycle? Why is 2's-complement wrap-around arithmetic particularly susceptible, and how can it be eliminated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **overflow limit cycle** is a large-amplitude, sustained parasitic oscillation (amplitude $\approx \pm 1$) appearing at the output of a recursive IIR filter in the absence of any input. (1 mark)

**Mechanism (1 mark):** In 2's-complement arithmetic, a sum exceeding $+1$ *wraps around* to a large negative value (and vice versa). This sign-flip acts as an *effective new input* that is amplified by the recursive gain, sustaining the oscillation indefinitely — i.e., the loop is regenerative rather than dissipative.

**Elimination (1 mark):** Replace wrap-around with **saturation arithmetic** — when $|s[n]| \ge 1$, clamp to $\pm 1$. The saturated value is *smaller* in magnitude than the overflowing one, breaking the regenerative feedback path.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Consider a 4-tap FIR filter $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{3} h[k]\,x[n-k]$ where $h = [0.25,\; 0.5,\; 0.25,\; 0.5]$. Implemented with $b = 10$ fraction bits, compute the per-multiplier round-off noise variance and the total output noise power. If the peak input amplitude is $A_m = 0.8$, compute the SQNR in dB. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of signal scaling. Derive the L₁-norm scaling rule for a filter with impulse response $h[n]$ and bounded input $|x[n]| \le x_{\max}$. Compute the scaling factor for a 1st-order IIR $y[n] = 0.95\,y[n-1] + x[n]$ with $x_{\max} = 0.6$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Quantization step (0.5 mark).**

$$q = 2^{-10} = \frac{1}{1024} \approx 9.766\times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Per-multiplier noise variance (1.5 marks).**

$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{q^2}{12} = \frac{(9.766\times 10^{-4})^2}{12} = \frac{9.5367\times 10^{-7}}{12} \approx 7.95\times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 3 — Total output noise power for FIR with $N=4$ (2 marks).**

$$\sigma_f^2 = N\cdot\sigma_e^2 = 4 \times 7.95\times 10^{-8} \approx 3.18\times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 4 — Signal power of input sinusoid (1 mark).**

$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{A_m^2}{2} = \frac{0.64}{2} = 0.32$$

**Step 5 — SQNR (2 marks).**

$$\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_f^2}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.32}{3.18\times 10^{-7}}\right)$$

$$= 10\log_{10}(1.006\times 10^{6}) = 10 \times 6.003 = 60.03 \text{ dB}$$

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks).** Signal scaling is the deliberate pre-attenuation of the input $x[n]$ by a gain $A < 1$ such that no internal filter node ever overflows for the worst-case input in the assumed class. The reciprocal $1/A$ is reapplied at the output (or at a safe stage) to restore nominal signal levels.

**Step 2 — Derivation of L₁ rule (3 marks).** The output is the convolution $y[n] = x[n]*h[n]$. Using the triangle inequality:

$$\vert y[n] \vert = \left\vert \sum_{k} x[n-k]\,h[k] \right\vert \le \sum_{k} \vert x[n-k]\vert\cdot\vert h[k]\vert$$

With $|x[n]| \le x_{\max}$:

$$\vert y[n]\vert \le x_{\max}\sum_{k}\vert h[k]\vert = x_{\max}\,\Vert h\Vert_1$$

To enforce $\vert y[n]\vert \le 1$ (the no-overflow bound), scale input by $A$:

$$A\,x_{\max}\,\Vert h\Vert_1 \le 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A \le \frac{1}{x_{\max}\,\Vert h\Vert_1}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical computation for the 1st-order IIR (2 marks).**

$$\Vert h\Vert_1 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\vert 0.95\vert^n = \frac{1}{1-0.95} = 20$$

$$A \le \frac{1}{0.6 \times 20} = \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.0833$$

So the input must be attenuated by at least **$1/12$** (about $-21.6$ dB), with a compensating gain of $12$ at the output.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For a 1st-order IIR $y[n] = a_1 y[n-1] + x[n]$ with $a_1 = -0.75$ and $b = 12$ bits, derive an expression for the output noise power $\sigma_f^2$ due to product round-off. If the pole were *exactly* at $z = -1$, describe what happens qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain *zero-input limit cycle* and *overflow limit cycle*. Show with a diagram how saturation arithmetic eliminates overflow limit cycles in 2's-complement. State the deadband-width formula for a 1st-order IIR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Impulse response (1 mark).**

$$h[n] = (a_1)^n u[n] = (-0.75)^n u[n]$$

**Step 2 — $\Vert h\Vert_2^2$ (2 marks).**

$$\Vert h\Vert_2^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(0.75)^{2n} = \frac{1}{1-(0.75)^2} = \frac{1}{1-0.5625} = \frac{1}{0.4375} \approx 2.286$$

**Step 3 — Output noise variance (2 marks).** With $q = 2^{-12} = 1/4096$ and one multiplier ($M=1$):

$$\sigma_f^2 = \frac{q^2}{12}\,\Vert h\Vert_2^2 = \frac{(1/4096)^2}{12}\times 2.286 = \frac{5.96\times 10^{-8}\times 2.286}{12} \approx 1.135\times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 4 — Qualitative behaviour at $z = -1$ (2 marks).** At $a_1 = -1$ exactly, the impulse response is $h[n] = (-1)^n$, which is *periodic* and never decays. In a finite-precision implementation, the actual quantized coefficient becomes $a_1 = -1 + q$ or $-1 - q$, placing the pole *just inside* the unit circle. The system cannot decay to zero because every round-off adds exactly enough energy to compensate the per-step attenuation, producing a **sustained $\pm q$ oscillation** — a textbook zero-input limit cycle at the maximum possible amplitude for granular noise.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Zero-input limit cycle (2 marks).** A zero-input limit cycle is a small-amplitude ($\le q$), non-decaying oscillation that appears at the output of a recursive filter when the input is identically zero. It is caused entirely by the round-off (or truncation) of products inside the feedback loop. The quantizer acts as a *nonlinear injection* that exactly replenishes the energy lost to the pole being slightly inside the unit circle.

**Step 2 — Overflow limit cycle (2 marks).** An overflow limit cycle is a *large* (near full-scale $\pm 1$) oscillation caused by 2's-complement wrap-around in the accumulator. Wrap-around creates a sign-flip; if the recursive gain is positive, the new sign-flip value is amplified further — a *regenerative* feedback loop that sustains indefinitely.

**Step 3 — Saturation arithmetic elimination (1.5 marks).**

| Step | 2's-Complement Wrap-Around | Saturation Arithmetic |
|---|---|---|
| True sum | $+1.3$ | $+1.3$ |
| Quantized | $-0.7$ (wrap) | $+1.0$ (clamp) |
| Effect on loop | Injects $-0.7$ into positive-gain recursion → amplified | Injects $+1.0$ (smaller magnitude than $+1.3$) → decays |

Saturation makes the *clamped* value smaller in magnitude than the *true* overflow, breaking the regenerative feedback.

**Step 4 — Deadband-width formula (1.5 marks).** For a 1st-order IIR $y[n] = a_1 y[n-1] + x[n]$:

$$\Delta \approx \frac{\vert a_1 \vert}{1-\vert a_1 \vert}\cdot q$$

Any initial state $\vert y[-1]\vert \le \Delta$ is trapped in the limit cycle and cannot decay to zero.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this Module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing rounding vs. truncation variance.** Many students write $\sigma_e^2 = q^2/12$ for *truncation*. Truncation variance is $q^2/3$. Examiner deducts **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting that FIR noise grows linearly with $N$.** Writing $\sigma_f^2 = q^2/12$ alone (without the factor $N$) costs a full mark.
> 3. **Confusing L₁, L₂, L∞ scaling.** Most students apply L₁ universally. For sinusoidal inputs, **L∞** is the correct bound. Mis-application costs **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Stating "saturation" without explaining *why* it works.** Just naming the technique is worth 0.5; explaining that the clamped value is *smaller* in magnitude than the overflowing value — breaking the regenerative feedback — earns the full mark.
> 5. **Mixing up round-off error and quantization error of the input.** Round-off = *product* quantization (at multiplier). Input quantization is a *separate* phenomenon covered in Module 3. Examiner expects you to disambiguate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Pin this on your wall before the exam:**

- [ ] Overflow = adder result exceeds $\pm 1$ dynamic range; causes huge errors in IIR.
- [ ] Round-off noise variance for rounding = $q^2/12$; for truncation = $q^2/3$.
- [ ] FIR output noise: $\sigma_f^2 = N q^2/12$ (linear in $N$).
- [ ] IIR output noise: $\sigma_f^2 = M (q^2/12)\Vert h\Vert_2^2$ — each multiplier's noise filtered by *full* impulse response.
- [ ] SQNR (sinusoid, dB) = $6.02 b + 1.76 + 10\log_{10}(3A_m^2/N)$.
- [ ] Zero-input limit cycle = small ($\le q$), product-round-off-induced, granular noise.
- [ ] Overflow limit cycle = large ($\sim 1$), 2's-complement-wrap-induced, regenerative.
- [ ] Deadband width (1st-order IIR) = $\dfrac{\vert a_1\vert}{1-\vert a_1\vert}\cdot q$.
- [ ] **Fix for overflow LCO** = saturation arithmetic (clamp to $\pm 1$).
- [ ] **L₁ scaling**: $A \le 1/(x_{\max}\,\Vert h\Vert_1)$ — for bounded / broadband inputs.
- [ ] **L∞ scaling**: $A \le 1/(x_{\max}\,\max_k\vert h[k]\vert)$ — for sinusoidal inputs.
- [ ] **L₂ scaling**: minimizes *output round-off noise* power — preferred for SQNR optimization.
- [ ] IIR filters are *more sensitive* to FWL effects than FIR because the recursive loop filters the noise.
- [ ] Higher-order sections should be decomposed into 2nd-order (biquad) cascades to control $\|h\|_2^2$.
- [ ] 2's-complement *adds* are inherently overflow-safe in the sense that they don't wrap *internally* — the *accumulator* is where the issue lives.
- [ ] **Real-world analogy:** Round-off = discrete spoon sizes; Overflow = bucket overflow; Limit cycle = sloshing in a leaky bucket; Scaling = pour 70 % at a time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to TMS320 Family: Architecture, C24x CPU and Other Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to TMS320 Family: Architecture, C24x CPU and Other Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **TMS320 family** is a series of **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** developed by **Texas Instruments (TI)**, introduced in 1982 with the TMS320C10. It is one of the most commercially successful and academically significant DSP processor families in the world. The family is broadly classified into three platform series:

- **C2000 Series** — Real-time control applications (Motor control, Power conversion, Digital Power)
- **C5000 Series** — Low-power, portable applications (Mobile phones, MP3 players)
- **C6000 Series** — High-performance applications (Multimedia, Telecom infrastructure, Image processing)

The **TMS320C24x** is a member of the **C2000 platform**, specifically designed for **embedded control applications**. It is a **16-bit fixed-point DSP** featuring a modified **Harvard architecture**, a high-performance CPU, on-chip memory, and a rich set of **on-chip peripherals** such as Event Managers, ADCs, and communication interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The C24x CPU is the **canonical example** used across Indian engineering curricula (KTU, VTU, Anna University, GTU) to teach the practical architecture of a real-world DSP. Board examination questions (12-14 marks) are frequently drawn from its **CPU block diagram, bus structure, register set, and peripheral mapping.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **super-fast specialized accountant** (the CPU) working inside a large **accounting firm** (the DSP chip). A general-purpose accountant (regular microcontroller like 8051) handles all tasks sequentially with one desk. But our DSP accountant has a special trick:

- **Three separate desks** (one for reading instructions, one for fetching data, one for storing results) — this is the **Harvard architecture**. The accountant can pull an instruction, a number, and write a result **all in the same clock cycle**.
- A **dedicated multiplication clerk** (the 16×16 hardware multiplier) — normal microcontrollers take dozens of cycles for a single multiply; this clerk does it in **one single cycle**.
- A **fast calculator (32-bit ALU)** that crunches numbers instantly.
- Several **specialized helpers** (Event Manager for timers/PWM, ADC for sensing real-world signals, SCI/SPI for talking to other chips).

In simple terms: **TMS320C24x = a fast accountant with multiple parallel desks, a multiplication specialist, and assistants for talking to the real world.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** A **Microcontroller (μC)** is optimized for *control tasks* with moderate math, while a **DSP** is optimized for *repetitive high-speed mathematical operations* (MAC — Multiply and Accumulate) on streaming data. The C24x is a **DSP-Controller hybrid**, ideal for closed-loop digital control of motors and power systems.

### 1.3 Standard Architectural Metrics (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Parameter | Value (C24x Standard) |
|---|---|
| Data Word Length | **16 bits** (Fixed-point) |
| ALU Width | **32 bits** |
| Hardware Multiplier | **16 × 16 bit → 32-bit result** |
| Cycle Time | **50 ns (at 20 MHz)** |
| MIPS | **20 MIPS** |
| Architecture Class | **Modified Harvard** |
| Address Space | **64K × 16 bits** (per memory space) |
| Internal Buses | **6 buses** (3 Address + 3 Data) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Philosophy of TMS320C24x

The C24x architecture is engineered around **three non-negotiable design principles** for real-time DSP:

**Principle 1 — Maximize Parallelism (Harvard Architecture)**

Unlike the von Neumann architecture (used in 8051) where program and data share one bus, the C24x uses **separate physical buses** for program memory, data read, and data write. This permits a **single-cycle MAC operation**.

**Principle 2 — Minimize Arithmetic Overhead**

The hardware multiplier and dedicated barrel shifter reduce the multiply-and-shift overhead to **zero extra cycles**. Convolution and filtering (e.g., FIR filter) become single-instruction inner loops.

**Principle 3 — Integration for Embedded Control**

On-chip integration of PWM modules, ADCs, and event managers means the C24x is a **System-on-Chip (SoC)** for digital control — eliminating the need for external ICs.

### 2.2 The C24x CPU — Block-Level Decomposition

The C24x CPU consists of the following functional sub-blocks:

#### (A) Central Arithmetic Logic Unit (CALU)
- A **32-bit ALU** that performs all standard arithmetic and logical operations.
- Two primary 32-bit registers feed it:
  - **ACC (Accumulator)** — Stores results of ALU and MAC operations.
  - **PREG (Product Register)** — Stores the 32-bit output of the hardware multiplier.

#### (B) Hardware Multiplier
- Performs **16 × 16 → 32-bit** multiplication in a **single instruction cycle**.
- Operands are sourced from data memory or registers.
- Result is always placed in **PREG**; the **MAC (Multiply and Accumulate)** instruction moves it to ACC and adds it to the previous ACC value.

#### (C) Auxiliary Register Arithmetic Unit (ARAU)
- Performs arithmetic on the **eight Auxiliary Registers (AR0–AR7)** in a **single cycle**, in parallel with the CALU.
- Used for efficient data addressing (e.g., circular buffers, FIFO indexing) — critical for FIR/IIR filters.

#### (D) Barrel Shifter
- Shifts the 16-bit input data by **0 to 16 bits** in a **single cycle**.
- Used for scaling, normalization, and bit-extraction.

#### (E) Status and Control Registers
- **ST0 (Status Register 0)** — Contains flags: **ARP (Auxiliary Register Pointer), C (Carry), OVM (Overflow Mode), TC (Test/Control), SXM (Sign-Extension Mode)**.
- **ST1 (Status Register 1)** — Contains flags: **ARB, CNF, INTM, DP, PM bits**, etc.

### 2.3 Bus Structure — The Heart of the Harvard Architecture

The C24x employs **six internal buses** (this is a **KTU 14-mark favourite topic**):

| Bus | Full Name | Width | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PAB** | Program Address Bus | 16-bit | Carries instruction addresses from PC to program memory |
| **DRAB** | Data-Read Address Bus | 16-bit | Carries addresses for reading data |
| **DWAB** | Data-Write Address Bus | 16-bit | Carries addresses for writing data |
| **PRDB** | Program-Read Data Bus | 16-bit | Carries instructions from program memory to CPU |
| **DRDB** | Data-Read Data Bus | 16-bit | Carries data from data memory to CPU |
| **DWDB** | Data-Write Data Bus | 16-bit | Carries data from CPU to data memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 6 buses?** Because in a single machine cycle, the C24x must be able to **(1) fetch an instruction, (2) read a data operand, AND (3) write a result simultaneously.** This is the essence of "single-cycle MAC."

### 2.4 Memory Organization (Modified Harvard)

The C24x uses a **Modified Harvard architecture** — physically separate program and data memory spaces, but with some shared configuration:

| Memory Type | Function | Access Speed |
|---|---|---|
| **DARAM (Dual-Access RAM)** | Single-cycle access for both read & write in one cycle | Fastest |
| **SARAM (Single-Access RAM)** | Single-cycle access for one op per cycle | Fast |
| **ROM / Flash** | Program memory storage (non-volatile) | Used for boot code |
| **External Memory Interface** | Expands address space off-chip | Slower |

**Configuration via CNF bit (in ST1 register):**
- **CNF = 0** → DARAM mapped to data space
- **CNF = 1** → DARAM0 mapped to program space (allows instructions from data memory)

### 2.5 On-Chip Peripherals (C24x-Specific — KTU 14-Mark Hot Topic)

| Peripheral | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **EV** | Event Manager | Generates PWM, captures timing events, QEP for motor control |
| **ADC** | Analog-to-Digital Converter | 10-bit, ~500 ns conversion; reads sensor signals |
| **SCI** | Serial Communication Interface | UART-type async serial communication |
| **SPI** | Serial Peripheral Interface | High-speed sync serial to peripherals (EEPROM, displays) |
| **CAN** | Controller Area Network | Automotive/industrial networked communication |
| **WDT** | Watchdog Timer | Resets CPU on software hang |
| **GPT** | General Purpose Timers | Standard timer/counter functions |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Performance Metrics

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second |
| $\text{Cycle Time} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}$ | Time per instruction cycle (in seconds) |
| $T_{\text{MAC}} = 1 \text{ cycle}$ | Time for one Multiply-Accumulate |
| $T_{\text{MAC}} = \text{Cycle Time}$ | Numerical MAC time |
| $N_{\text{MAC per sample}} = N$ | MACs per sample for N-tap FIR |
| $f_{\text{sample, max}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{N}$ | Max sampling frequency for real-time FIR |
| $N_{\text{states}} = 2^{n}$ | Number of addressable locations for n-bit bus |
| $M_{\text{size}} = N_{\text{states}} \times \text{word size}$ | Total memory size |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write cycle time as "1/clock" but forget that the **clock period** is the inverse. For a 20 MHz clock, **Cycle Time = 50 ns = 0.05 μs**.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The C24x is the workhorse in:
- **AC Induction Motor Drives** (variable frequency drives — VFDs)
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor Control** — EV powertrains, drones, robotics
- **Solar Micro-Inverters** and **MPPT charge controllers**
- **Digital Power Supplies** (PFC, LLC resonant converters)
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)**
- **Servo Drives** in CNC machines

The F28xx series (modern C2000) is the direct descendant used in Tesla's first-generation powertrain and SpaceX early designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Sampling Frequency for N-tap FIR Filter

**Problem Statement:** A TMS320C24x DSP running at clock frequency $f_{\text{clk}}$ is implementing an N-tap FIR filter. Each tap requires **one MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) instruction**. Derive the maximum real-time sampling frequency $f_{s,\text{max}}$.

**Step 1: Identify the computational load per output sample.**

An N-tap FIR filter is defined mathematically as:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]
$$

This summation has **N multiplications and N-1 additions**. On a C24x with hardware MAC, the dominant operation is the **N MAC instructions** required per output sample. (We absorb the additions into the same instruction.)

**Step 2: Determine the number of instruction cycles per output sample.**

The hardware MAC executes in **exactly 1 instruction cycle** per tap. Therefore:

$$
N_{\text{cycles/sample}} = N \times 1 = N
$$

**Step 3: Express the available time per sample in terms of clock cycles.**

The instruction cycle time is:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}
$$

For real-time operation, the time to process one sample **must be less than or equal to** the sampling period $T_s = 1/f_s$:

$$
N \times T_{\text{cycle}} \leq T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for the maximum sampling frequency.**

Rearranging the inequality by taking the equality (worst case) and inverting:

$$
N \times \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} \leq \frac{1}{f_s}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $f_s \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$:

$$
N \cdot f_s \leq f_{\text{clk}}
$$

Solving for $f_s$:

$$
f_s \leq \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{N}
$$

Therefore, the maximum sampling frequency is:

$$
\boxed{f_{s,\text{max}} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{N}}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical verification for a typical KTU-style problem.**

For $f_{\text{clk}} = 20 \text{ MHz}$ and $N = 100$:

$$
f_{s,\text{max}} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{100} = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz} = 200 \text{ kHz}
$$

The maximum time available per sample is:

$$
T_{s,\text{min}} = \frac{1}{200{,}000} = 5 \text{ μs}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** A 100-tap FIR filter on a C24x can process audio up to 200 kS/s — far above the 44.1 kHz CD audio rate, proving the C24x is well-suited for real-time DSP.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Memory Space Size from Address Bus Width

**Problem Statement:** The C24x has a 16-bit program address bus (PAB). Derive the maximum addressable program memory size.

**Step 1: Number of unique addresses.**

With $n$ address lines, the number of unique addressable locations is $2^n$:

$$
N_{\text{addresses}} = 2^n = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Total memory size.**

Each location stores a 16-bit instruction word, so:

$$
M_{\text{size}} = 64 \text{ K} \times 16 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ K} \times 2 \text{ bytes} = 128 \text{ KB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some texts report "64K words" while others say "64 KB" — clarify whether the unit is **words** or **bytes**. Always state the word size explicitly in your KTU answer.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — C24x vs. Conventional Microcontroller

| Feature | 8051 (μC) | TMS320C24x (DSP) |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Von Neumann | Modified Harvard |
| Multiplier | Software (multi-cycle) | Hardware 16×16 (1 cycle) |
| MAC Instruction | Absent (multi-step) | Native, single-cycle |
| Buses | 1 shared | 6 dedicated |
| Registers | Few (A, B, DPTR) | Many (AR0–AR7, ACC, PREG, ST0/ST1) |
| Data Memory | 128 bytes internal | Up to 64K words |
| Real-Time DSP Capability | Limited | Excellent |
| Power Consumption | Low | Moderate |
| Typical Use | Appliances, toys | Motor control, digital power |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Space Configuration via CNF Bit

| CNF Bit | DARAM0 Mapping | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | Data space only | Pure data storage |
| **1** | Program space | Bootloader, runtime code from RAM |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level Block Diagram of TMS320C24x Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["C24X CPU CORE"]
        CALU["CENTRAL ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT 32-BIT ALU"]
        ACC["ACCUMULATOR 32-BIT ACC"]
        PREG["PRODUCT REGISTER 32-BIT PREG"]
        MULT["HARDWARE MULTIPLIER 16x16 BIT"]
        ARAU["AUXILIARY REGISTER ARITHMETIC UNIT"]
        AR["AUXILIARY REGISTERS AR0 TO AR7"]
        SHIFT["BARREL SHIFTER 0 TO 16 BIT SHIFT"]
        ST["STATUS REGISTERS ST0 ST1"]
    end

    subgraph BUSES["INTERNAL BUS STRUCTURE 6 BUSES"]
        PAB["PAB PROGRAM ADDRESS BUS 16-BIT"]
        DRAB["DRAB DATA READ ADDRESS BUS 16-BIT"]
        DWAB["DWAB DATA WRITE ADDRESS BUS 16-BIT"]
        PRDB["PRDB PROGRAM READ DATA BUS 16-BIT"]
        DRDB["DRDB DATA READ DATA BUS 16-BIT"]
        DWDB["DWDB DATA WRITE DATA BUS 16-BIT"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["MEMORY SUBSYSTEM"]
        PROG["PROGRAM MEMORY ROM FLASH"]
        DARAM["DARAM DUAL ACCESS RAM"]
        SARAM["SARAM SINGLE ACCESS RAM"]
    end

    subgraph PERIPH["ON-CHIP PERIPHERALS"]
        EV["EVENT MANAGER EV PWM QEP TIMERS"]
        ADC["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER 10-BIT"]
        SCI["SERIAL COMMUNICATION INTERFACE SCI UART"]
        SPI["SERIAL PERIPHERAL INTERFACE SPI"]
        CAN["CONTROLLER AREA NETWORK CAN"]
        WDT["WATCHDOG TIMER WDT"]
    end

    CPU <--> BUSES
    BUSES <--> MEM
    BUSES <--> PERIPH
```

### 4.2 C24x CPU Internal Data Path — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRDB_IN["DRDB DATA READ 16-BIT"] --> SHIFT_BLOCK["BARREL SHIFTER"]
    SHIFT_BLOCK --> MUL_IN1["OPERAND 1 TO MULTIPLIER"]
    PREG_BLOCK["PREG 32-BIT"] --> MUL_IN2["OPERAND 2 TO MULTIPLIER"]
    MUL_IN1 --> MULT_BLOCK["HARDWARE MULTIPLIER 16x16 TO 32"]
    MUL_IN2 --> MULT_BLOCK
    MULT_BLOCK --> PREG_BLOCK
    PREG_BLOCK --> MAC["MAC INSTRUCTION PATH"]
    SHIFT_BLOCK --> ALU_IN1["ALU INPUT 1"]
    ACC_BLOCK["ACC 32-BIT"] --> ALU_IN2["ALU INPUT 2"]
    ALU_IN1 --> CALU_BLOCK["32-BIT ALU"]
    ALU_IN2 --> CALU_BLOCK
    MAC --> CALU_BLOCK
    CALU_BLOCK --> ACC_BLOCK
    ACC_BLOCK --> DWDB_OUT["DWDB DATA WRITE 16-BIT"]
```

### 4.3 Memory Organization (Modified Harvard)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ADDR16["16-BIT ADDRESS SPACE"]
        PROG_SPACE["PROGRAM MEMORY SPACE 64K x 16"]
        DATA_SPACE["DATA MEMORY SPACE 64K x 16"]
    end

    subgraph PROG_DETAIL["PROGRAM MEMORY DETAIL"]
        ROM_BLK["ROM BOOT CODE"]
        FLASH_BLK["FLASH USER CODE"]
        EXT_PROG["EXTERNAL PROGRAM MEMORY OPTIONAL"]
    end

    subgraph DATA_DETAIL["DATA MEMORY DETAIL"]
        REGS["MEMORY MAPPED REGISTERS 0x0000 TO 0x005F"]
        DARAM_BLK["DARAM DUAL ACCESS BLOCK 0x0060 TO 0x07FF"]
        SARAM_BLK["SARAM SINGLE ACCESS BLOCK 0x0800 TO 0x0FFF"]
        EXT_DATA["EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY OPTIONAL"]
    end

    PROG_SPACE --> ROM_BLK
    PROG_SPACE --> FLASH_BLK
    PROG_SPACE --> EXT_PROG
    DATA_SPACE --> REGS
    DATA_SPACE --> DARAM_BLK
    DATA_SPACE --> SARAM_BLK
    DATA_SPACE --> EXT_DATA
```

### 4.4 Single-Cycle MAC Operation Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as CLOCK CYCLE
    participant PAB as PAB FETCH INSTRUCTION
    participant PRDB as PRDB LOAD INSTRUCTION
    participant DRAB as DRAB FETCH OPERAND
    participant DRDB as DRDB LOAD OPERAND
    participant MUL as MULTIPLIER
    participant ACC as ACCUMULATOR

    CLK->>PAB: CYCLE N PHASE 1
    PAB->>PRDB: SEND INSTRUCTION ADDRESS
    PRDB->>MUL: INSTRUCTION DECODED MAC
    CLK->>DRAB: CYCLE N PHASE 2
    DRAB->>DRDB: SEND DATA ADDRESS
    DRDB->>MUL: LOAD OPERAND X N
    MUL->>MUL: COMPUTE H K TIMES X N K
    MUL->>ACC: ACC = ACC PLUS RESULT
    Note over CLK,ACC: ALL OPERATIONS COMPLETE IN ONE MACHINE CYCLE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation for KTU:** This timing visualization is exactly what examiners expect when the question asks: *"With a block diagram, explain how the C24x executes a single-cycle MAC."* Always annotate the **parallelism of bus operations** explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Explain the significance of the Harvard architecture used in the TMS320C24x processor. How is it different from the Von Neumann architecture?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Harvard architecture used in the TMS320C24x features **physically separate program and data memory spaces** with **dedicated buses** for instructions and data. This allows the CPU to fetch an instruction, read a data operand, and write a result **simultaneously in a single machine cycle**, enabling the single-cycle MAC operation essential for real-time DSP. In contrast, the Von Neumann architecture (used in 8051) uses a **single shared bus** for both instructions and data, causing a "Von Neumann bottleneck" where instruction fetches and data accesses must be **sequential**, making high-speed DSP inefficient.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *List any three on-chip peripherals of the TMS320C24x processor and state their functions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Event Manager (EV)** — Generates PWM waveforms, captures timing events, and performs QEP (Quadrature Encoder Pulse) decoding for motor speed/position sensing.
2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — 10-bit, multi-channel ADC with ~500 ns conversion time for digitizing analog sensor signals (current, voltage, temperature).
3. **Serial Communication Interface (SCI)** — Provides asynchronous (UART) serial communication for talking to PCs, other MCUs, or GPS modules.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 4]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the TMS320C24x CPU. List all major functional units. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **six-bus structure** of the C24x and show how it enables single-cycle MAC operation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The TMS320C24x CPU consists of the following major functional units (each **1 mark**, with block diagram carrying 2 marks):

**1. Central Arithmetic Logic Unit (CALU):** Contains a **32-bit ALU** that performs all arithmetic (add, subtract, multiply-add) and logical (AND, OR, XOR) operations. The two primary input registers are the **32-bit ACC (Accumulator)** and the **32-bit PREG (Product Register)**.

**2. Hardware Multiplier:** A dedicated **16 × 16 → 32-bit** multiplier that completes a multiplication in **one single instruction cycle**. Without this unit, a software multiply would consume 10–20 cycles.

**3. Auxiliary Register Arithmetic Unit (ARAU):** Performs arithmetic operations on the **eight Auxiliary Registers (AR0–AR7)** in parallel with the CALU, supporting **modular addressing** (circular buffers) used in FIR/IIR filters.

**4. Barrel Shifter:** A dedicated shifter that can shift the 16-bit input data left by **0 to 16 bits in one cycle**, used for scaling, alignment, and bit extraction.

**5. Status and Control Registers (ST0, ST1):** Hold processor flags such as **C (Carry), Z (Zero), OVM (Overflow Mode), SXM (Sign Extension Mode), TC (Test/Control), CNF, DP, INTM**, and the **ARP (Auxiliary Register Pointer)**.

**6. Program Counter and Stack:** Manages instruction flow and subroutine context saving.

**Block Diagram:** [See Section 4.1 — Mermaid Block Diagram — Reproduce on paper in the answer script].

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The C24x employs **six dedicated internal buses** organized as **three address buses** and **three data buses**:

| Bus | Function |
|---|---|
| **PAB (Program Address Bus)** | Carries instruction addresses from PC to program memory |
| **DRAB (Data Read Address Bus)** | Carries addresses for reading data operands |
| **DWAB (Data Write Address Bus)** | Carries addresses for writing results |
| **PRDB (Program Read Data Bus)** | Carries fetched instructions back to CPU |
| **DRDB (Data Read Data Bus)** | Carries data operands from memory to CPU |
| **DWDB (Data Write Data Bus)** | Carries results from CPU to memory |

**Single-Cycle MAC Explanation (4 marks):**

Consider a MAC instruction `MAC p16, *AR2++`:

- **Phase 1:** PAB places the instruction's address on the bus → instruction fetched via PRDB into the CPU instruction register.
- **Phase 2:** DRAB places the address pointed to by AR2 on the bus → data operand $x[n]$ fetched via DRDB.
- **Phase 2 (parallel):** The PREG output (previous product $h[k] \cdot x[n-k-1]$) is fed to the ALU, and the multiplier computes the **new** product $h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ → results combined with ACC and stored back.

**All three bus activities happen in the same machine cycle, achieving the single-cycle MAC.** This is the **key advantage** of the C24x's bus architecture for real-time DSP.

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 4] — Alternative Choice**
> **(a)** Explain the memory organization of the TMS320C24x. Differentiate between **DARAM** and **SARAM**. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** With an example, derive the **maximum sampling frequency** for an N-tap FIR filter running on a C24x at clock frequency $f_{\text{clk}} = 20$ MHz with $N = 200$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The TMS320C24x uses a **Modified Harvard architecture** with **separate program and data memory spaces**, each addressable up to **64K × 16 bits**.

**Memory Types:**

**1. DARAM (Dual-Access RAM):** Allows **two simultaneous accesses in a single cycle** — one read and one write — to two different locations. This is critical for the single-cycle MAC, where the multiplier reads $x[n]$ while the ALU writes to ACC. (3 marks for DARAM explanation)

**2. SARAM (Single-Access RAM):** Allows only **one access per cycle** (either read OR write). It is used for storing coefficients, large buffers, or data that doesn't need concurrent access. (2 marks for SARAM explanation)

**3. Program Memory:** Holds instructions in ROM/Flash; accessed via PAB/PRDB.

**4. Memory-Mapped Registers:** Special function registers (peripheral control, status) mapped into the data address space (typically the first few hundred locations).

**Differentiation Table:**

| Feature | DARAM | SARAM |
|---|---|---|
| Accesses per cycle | 2 (concurrent) | 1 |
| Cost per bit | Higher | Lower |
| Use in MAC | Mandatory operand fetch + write | Coefficient storage |

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $f_{\text{clk}} = 20 \text{ MHz}$, $N = 200$ taps.

**Step 1: FIR filter equation.**

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]
$$

**Step 2: Cycles per output sample.**

One MAC per tap, 1 cycle per MAC:

$$
N_{\text{cycles}} = N = 200 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3: Time per output sample.**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^6} = 50 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{sample}} = 200 \times 50 \text{ ns} = 10{,}000 \text{ ns} = 10 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum sampling frequency.**

$$
f_{s,\text{max}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{1}{10 \text{ μs}} = 100 \text{ kHz}
$$

Equivalently, using the formula:

$$
f_{s,\text{max}} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{N} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{200} = 100 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Conclusion:** The C24x can implement a 200-tap real-time FIR filter at a maximum sampling rate of **100 kHz**, suitable for many control and audio applications.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in C24x Architecture Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "16-bit DSP" with "16-bit address bus"** — the C24x is a 16-bit *data* DSP with a *16-bit address* bus (giving 64K words). The ALU is 32-bit, the multiplier result is 32-bit, and the ACC is 32-bit. Mentioning all widths in your answer earns full marks.
> 2. **Listing only 2 or 3 buses** — you MUST list all **6 buses** (PAB, DRAB, DWAB, PRDB, DRDB, DWDB) with their functions. Part (b) of 14-mark questions is built around this.
> 3. **Forgetting the three memory types** (DARAM, SARAM, ROM/Flash) and the **CNF bit** configuration for Modified Harvard.
> 4. **Confusing MIPS with MHz** — MIPS = MHz × (instructions per cycle). C24x is 20 MIPS at 20 MHz (1 instruction/cycle).
> 5. **Not relating architecture to real-time DSP** — always conclude with "this enables single-cycle MAC for real-time filtering."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: TMS320 Family**

- ⭐ **TMS320C24x is a 16-bit fixed-point DSP** with a 32-bit ALU and 32-bit ACC.
- ⭐ **Architecture:** Modified Harvard — separate program and data memory spaces.
- ⭐ **Six internal buses:** PAB, DRAB, DWAB (address) + PRDB, DRDB, DWDB (data).
- ⭐ **Hardware multiplier:** 16 × 16 → 32-bit, single-cycle execution.
- ⭐ **ACC (Accumulator)** is the primary 32-bit result register.
- ⭐ **PREG (Product Register)** holds the multiplier output.
- ⭐ **8 Auxiliary Registers (AR0–AR7)** with **ARAU** for single-cycle address updates.
- ⭐ **Barrel shifter** performs 0–16 bit shifts in one cycle.
- ⭐ **DARAM** allows 2 accesses/cycle; **SARAM** allows 1 access/cycle.
- ⭐ **CNF bit** (in ST1) configures DARAM0 between program and data space.
- ⭐ **Status Registers ST0 and ST1** contain flags (C, OVM, SXM, TC, CNF, DP, INTM, ARP).
- ⭐ **On-chip peripherals:** EV (PWM/QEP), ADC, SCI (UART), SPI, CAN, WDT.
- ⭐ **Key formula:** $f_{s,\text{max}} = f_{\text{clk}} / N$ for N-tap FIR.
- ⭐ **Cycle time** at 20 MHz = 50 ns; **20 MIPS** throughput.
- ⭐ **Memory size:** 64K × 16-bit words addressable per space.
- ⭐ **Primary application domain:** Real-time motor control and digital power conversion.
- ⭐ **KTU focus areas:** Block diagram (Section 4.1), bus structure (Section 4.1), MAC timing (Section 4.4), memory organization (Section 4.3), and the FIR sampling frequency derivation (Section 3.1).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Language Instructions, Instruction Set Summary, Simple Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assembly Language Instructions, Instruction Set Summary, Simple Programs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Assembly Language Instructions** are low-level symbolic representations of machine-level operations that a Digital Signal Processor (DSP) can execute directly. Each mnemonic corresponds to a fixed binary opcode stored in the program memory of the DSP. In the KTU 2024 scheme, the reference processor is typically the **Texas Instruments TMS320C5x / TMS320C54x** fixed-point DSP family.

An **instruction** in DSP assembly language consists of three primary fields:
1. **Label** (optional): A symbolic address for branch/call targets.
2. **Mnemonic** (opcode): The operation to be performed (e.g., `ADD`, `MPY`, `LACC`).
3. **Operands**: Source/destination registers, memory addresses, or immediate data.

The general syntax is:

```
[label[:]]   mnemonic   [operand1 [, operand2 [, operand3]]]   [; comment]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes *instruction syntax, addressing modes, instruction set categories, and writing simple programs* such as FIR filter implementation, convolution, and waveform generation. Students must memorize the **mnemonic-operand relationships** for the TMS320C5x family.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are instructing a **highly specialized factory robot (the DSP)** that has a single purpose: to multiply and add numbers extremely fast.

- A **high-level language** like C is like giving the robot a *project brief* in plain English ("compute the average of 1000 samples").
- **Assembly language** is like giving the robot a *numbered, step-by-step checklist* where each line is one precise physical action — "Lift Box A", "Place in Slot 3", "Multiply contents by 5".

The robot does not understand English; it only obeys the checklist. Each line of assembly language is exactly **one instruction word** that the DSP hardware can decode and execute in a single cycle (often in a single clock tick of **10 ns to 25 ns**).

> [!TIP]
> Think of assembly instructions as the **atoms** of DSP programming — combine them in the right order, and you can build any signal-processing molecule: filter, FFT, or modulator.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Instruction Cycle Time (Cycle Period):** **10 ns to 25 ns** depending on the clock frequency (40 MHz to 100 MHz).
- **Instruction Word Length:** **16-bit** opcode for TMS320C5x.
- **Program Memory:** 64K × 16-bit words addressable via the 16-bit Program Counter (PC).
- **Data Memory:** 64K × 16-bit words.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Instruction Format (Three-Word Format)

Most TMS320C5x instructions fit into one of three formats:

| Format Type | Word 1 (Opcode) | Word 2 (Operand 1) | Word 3 (Operand 2) | Example Instruction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single-Word** | Opcode + Embedded Data | — | — | `NOP`, `RET` |
| **One-Operand** | Opcode | Operand | — | `LACC #5` |
| **Two-Operand** | Opcode | Operand 1 | Operand 2 | `MACD 1000h, 2000h` |

> [!NOTE]
> The instruction word is fetched from program memory. The opcode specifies the operation, while the operand words hold the data or address needed to execute it.

## 2.2 Categories of Instructions

The instruction set of TMS320C5x is classified into the following functional groups:

### A. Accumulator, Arithmetic, and Logic Instructions
- **ADD** – Add memory to accumulator.
- **SUB** – Subtract memory from accumulator.
- **MPY** – Multiply TREG0 by memory word, store in PREG.
- **MAC / MACD** – Multiply-accumulate with optional data move (used in FIR filtering).
- **AND, OR, XOR** – Bitwise logic on accumulator.
- **LACC** – Load accumulator with memory or immediate value.
- **SACL** – Store accumulator low byte/word into data memory.
- **SACH** – Store accumulator high byte/word into data memory.

### B. Load and Store Instructions
- **LDPK** – Load data page pointer (DP).
- **LARP** – Load auxiliary register pointer (ARP).
- **LAR** – Load auxiliary register (AR0–AR7).
- **SAR** – Store auxiliary register.
- **LARK** – Load AR with immediate 8-bit constant.

### C. Branch and Control Instructions
- **B** – Unconditional branch.
- **BACC** – Branch to address in accumulator.
- **BANZ** – Branch if auxiliary register not zero (loop control).
- **BCND** – Conditional branch (TC, C, OV, EQ, LT, GT, etc.).
- **CALL** – Subroutine call.
- **RET** – Return from subroutine.
- **RPT** – Repeat next instruction.
- **RPTZ** – Repeat and clear accumulator.

### D. I/O and Memory-Mapped Instructions
- **IN** – Read from I/O port.
- **OUT** – Write to I/O port.
- **TBLR / TBLW** – Table read/write.

## 2.3 Addressing Modes (KTU High-Yield Topic)

| Mode | Syntax Example | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direct Addressing** | `ADD 0100h` | Uses DP (Data Page Pointer) + 7-bit offset; DP × 128 + offset = address. |
| **Indirect Addressing** | `ADD *` or `ADD *+` | Uses AR (Auxiliary Register) pointed to by ARP. |
| **Immediate Addressing** | `LACC #05h` | Constant encoded directly into the instruction word. |
| **Memory-Mapped** | `IN 0200h, PA0` | Accesses I/O ports via memory-mapped addresses. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the TMS320C5x, **direct addressing** forms a 16-bit address = (DP × 128) + 7-bit offset. **Indirect addressing** supports post-increment (`*+`), post-decrement (`*-`), and indexed modes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Item | Formula / Relation | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Address formation (Direct) | $A_{16} = (DP \times 128) + \text{offset}_{7}$ | decimal/hex word |
| MAC execution time | $T_{MAC} = N \times T_{cycle}$ | seconds |
| FIR filter MAC per tap | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ | samples |
| Convolution sum | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$ | samples |
| Cycle period | $T_c = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | seconds |
| Instruction rate | $\text{MIPS} = f_{CLK} \times 10^{-6}$ | million instr/sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These instructions form the bedrock of every real-time DSP system — audio codecs (MP3/AAC), digital radios (DAB), echo cancellers in VoIP, motor-control loops in industrial drives, and biomedical signal analyzers (ECG/EEG).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Address Formation in Direct Addressing Mode

In TMS320C5x, the data memory is logically divided into **512 pages**, each containing **128 words**. The 16-bit data memory address is formed by concatenating the 9-bit **Data Page Pointer (DP)** with the 7-bit **offset** embedded in the instruction word.

$$
A_{16} = (DP \times 128) + \text{offset}_{7}
$$

**Step-by-step conversion logic:**

1. The DP register holds the upper 9 bits of the address (page number).
2. The instruction word contains a 7-bit offset within that page.
3. Shift the DP left by 7 bits to align it with the page boundary.

$$
\text{Shifted DP} = DP \ll 7 \quad (\text{multiplication by } 2^7 = 128)
$$

4. Add the 7-bit offset:

$$
A_{16} = (DP \ll 7) + \text{offset}_{7}
$$

5. **Numerical Example:** Suppose DP = `001000000` (binary) and the instruction is `ADD 0100 0000`.

- Shifted DP: `001000000 0000000`
- Offset:     `0000000 1000000`
- Result:     `001000000 1000000` = `0x4080`

> [!TIP]
> The programmer **must** update DP before accessing a new page. Forgetting this is a classic KTU exam pitfall!

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: FIR Filter Implementation (MAC-Based)

The **Finite Impulse Response (FIR)** filter is implemented using the Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) instruction. For an $N$-tap filter:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]
$$

| Iteration $k$ | AR0 (input pointer) | AR1 (coefficient pointer) | Operation | Result in ACC |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | $x[n]$ | $h[0]$ | `MAC *AR0+, *AR1+` | $h[0] \cdot x[n]$ |
| 1 | $x[n-1]$ | $h[1]$ | (next cycle) | $h[0]x[n] + h[1]x[n-1]$ |
| 2 | $x[n-2]$ | $h[2]$ | (next cycle) | $+ h[2] \cdot x[n-2]$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $N-1$ | $x[n-N+1]$ | $h[N-1]$ | (final cycle) | $y[n]$ |

**Execution time derivation:**

$$
T_{exec} = (N + K_{overhead}) \times T_c
$$

where $K_{overhead}$ accounts for setup (clearing ACC, initializing AR, loop entry). On TMS320C5x, with `RPT` repeat mode and dedicated MAC hardware, the inner loop runs at **one MAC per cycle** ($K_{overhead} \approx 4$ cycles).

## 3.3 Worked Example: Convolution of Two Sequences

Given $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $h[n] = \{4, 5\}$ (lengths $M = 3$ and $N = 2$), find $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$.

The output length is $L = M + N - 1 = 4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[0] &= x[0] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 4 = 4 \\
y[1] &= x[0] \cdot h[1] + x[1] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 5 + 2 \cdot 4 = 13 \\
y[2] &= x[1] \cdot h[1] + x[2] \cdot h[0] = 2 \cdot 5 + 3 \cdot 4 = 22 \\
y[3] &= x[2] \cdot h[1] = 3 \cdot 5 = 15
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $y[n] = \{4, 13, 22, 15\}$.

**Assembly Program Skeleton (TMS320C5x):**

```assembly
        .mmregs
        .bss    y, 4            ; Output array (4 words)
        .data
x:      .word   1, 2, 3         ; Input sequence
h:      .word   4, 5            ; Impulse response

        .text
START:  LDPK    #6              ; Set DP for data page
        LAR     AR0, #x         ; AR0 -> x[0]
        LAR     AR1, #h         ; AR1 -> h[0]
        LAR     AR2, #y         ; AR2 -> y[0]
        LACC    #0
        MPY     *AR0+, *AR1+    ; y[0] = x[0]*h[0]
        SACL    *AR2+
        ; ... (continue for overlapping products)
        END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `RPT` instruction makes the next MAC instruction execute $(N+1)$ times in hardware — a single-instruction inner loop — which is the **single most important optimization** in KTU DSP programming questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Instruction Word Decoding Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a 16-bit instruction word travels through the DSP pipeline to produce an executed operation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Memory PC] --> B[Fetch 16-bit Instruction Word]
    B --> C{Decode Opcode}
    C --> D1[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    C --> D2[Multiplier Block]
    C --> D3[Barrel Shifter]
    C --> D4[Address Generation Unit]
    D1 --> E1[Update Accumulator ACC]
    D2 --> E2[Update PREG and ACC via MAC]
    D3 --> E3[Shifted value to ACC]
    D4 --> E4[Load Address into ARn]
    E1 --> F[Write Back to Data Memory]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> G[Next PC]
    F --> G
    G --> A
```

## 4.2 FIR Filter Program Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[AR0 points to x n]
    end
    subgraph COEFF
        C1[AR1 points to h k]
    end
    subgraph RESULT
        R1[ACC holds y n]
    end
    INIT[Initialize AR0 AR1 DP] --> CLEAR[Clear ACC and PREG]
    CLEAR --> LOOP[MAC AR0+ AR1+]
    LOOP --> RPT[RPT N minus 1]
    RPT --> STORE[SACL to output buffer]
    STORE --> DONE[Loop complete]
```

## 4.3 Addressing Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Instruction Fetch] --> Q1{Operand field type}
    Q1 -->|Direct| DIR[DP shift left 7 plus offset]
    Q1 -->|Indirect| IND[Use ARn via ARP]
    Q1 -->|Immediate| IMM[Operand is constant]
    DIR --> EXEC[Execute operation]
    IND --> EXEC
    IMM --> EXEC
    EXEC --> NEXT[Advance PC]
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU valuation, drawing such block diagrams for instruction execution and addressing modes fetches **easy 2–3 marks** even if the rest of the answer is partially incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Explain the difference between direct addressing and indirect addressing modes in TMS320C5x DSP processor.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Direct Addressing Mode:** The 16-bit data memory address is formed by concatenating the 9-bit Data Page Pointer (DP) with the 7-bit offset from the instruction word.

$$
A_{16} = (DP \ll 7) + \text{offset}
$$

- **Indirect Addressing Mode:** The address is taken from one of the eight Auxiliary Registers (AR0–AR7), selected by the 3-bit Auxiliary Register Pointer (ARP). Supports auto-increment `*+` and auto-decrement `*-`.

**Distinction:** Direct mode is static and depends on DP; indirect mode is dynamic and is suited for loops and table traversal.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]

**List any six arithmetic instructions of TMS320C5x with their function.**

**Model Answer:**

| Instruction | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| ADD | Adds memory word to accumulator |
| SUB | Subtracts memory word from accumulator |
| MPY | Multiplies TREG0 by memory, result in PREG |
| MAC | Multiply-accumulate with data move |
| LACC | Loads constant or memory word into ACC |
| SACL | Stores low word of ACC into data memory |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Explain the instruction formats of TMS320C5x DSP with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an assembly language program to compute the sum of 10 data samples stored in memory. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Instruction Formats:**

The TMS320C5x supports three instruction formats:

1. **Single-word format** — opcode only. Example: `NOP`, `RET`, `IDLE`.
2. **One-operand format** — opcode + one 16-bit operand. Example: `LACC #2000h` (load immediate).
3. **Two-operand format** — opcode + two 16-bit operands. Example: `MACD 1000h, 2000h`.

The instruction word is 16 bits wide; multi-word instructions occupy consecutive program memory locations and are fetched in successive cycles.

**(b) Assembly Program to Sum 10 Data Samples:**

```assembly
        .mmregs
N       .set    10              ; Number of samples
        .bss    sum, 1          ; Result
        .data
x:      .word   5, 12, 8, 3, 9, 4, 11, 6, 2, 7
        .text
START:  LDPK    #4              ; Set DP for data page of x
        LAR     AR0, #x         ; AR0 -> x[0]
        LACC    #0              ; Clear ACC
        RPT     #(N-1)          ; Repeat next instruction N times
        ADD     *AR0+           ; Add sample, increment AR0
        SACL    sum             ; Store sum
END:    B       END             ; Halt
        .end
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Initialize DP and AR0.
2. Clear accumulator.
3. Use `RPT #(N-1)` to repeat the next instruction **N times** automatically.
4. Inside the repeat, `ADD *AR0+` adds the memory word and auto-increments AR0.
5. After N iterations, ACC holds the sum; `SACL` stores it in `sum`.

**Execution cycles:** $T_{exec} = (N+3) \times T_c = 13 \times T_c$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the addressing modes of TMS320C5x DSP. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an assembly language program to compute the dot product of two vectors each of length 8. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Addressing Modes:**

The TMS320C5x supports four addressing modes:

1. **Direct Addressing** – (DP × 128) + 7-bit offset.
2. **Indirect Addressing** – AR0–AR7 selected by ARP; supports `*`, `*+`, `*-`, `*0+`, `*0-`, `*BR0+`, etc.
3. **Immediate Addressing** – Constant embedded in instruction word (8-bit or 16-bit).
4. **Memory-Mapped Addressing** – For I/O ports via the `IN` and `OUT` instructions.

**Block Diagram:**

```
+--------------------+
|   Program Memory   |
|  [Fetch Opcode]    |
+---------+----------+
          |
          v
+--------------------+
|  Address Decoder   |
+--|----|----|------+
   |    |    |
   v    v    v
 Direct Indirect Immediate
   |    |    |
   v    v    v
+--------+--------+
|   Data Memory    |
+--------+---------+
```

**(b) Dot Product Program:**

The dot product is $D = \sum_{i=0}^{7} a_i \cdot b_i$, executed with `MPY` plus accumulation.

```assembly
        .mmregs
N       .set    8
        .bss    dot, 1
        .data
a:      .word   1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8
b:      .word   8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1
        .text
START:  LDPK    #4
        LAR     AR0, #a
        LAR     AR1, #b
        LACC    #0
        RPT     #(N-1)
        MAC     *AR0+, *AR1+
        SACL    dot
        .end
```

**Logic:** `MAC *AR0+, *AR1+` multiplies two operands, adds to ACC, and increments both pointers — all in one cycle. After N repeats, dot product is in ACC and stored via `SACL`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic (costing 2–4 marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to set DP (LDPK) before direct addressing** — leads to wrong page access and silent data corruption.
> 2. **Misusing RPT count** — `RPT #(N-1)` repeats the *next* instruction **N times**, not N-1. Off-by-one errors are the most common reason for incorrect sums.
> 3. **Confusing SACL vs SACH** — `SACL` stores the low 16 bits of ACC, `SACH` stores the upper 16 bits. Mixing them up yields wrong results in MAC operations.
> 4. **ARP left in wrong state** — after a loop using `*AR0+`, the ARP points to AR0; the next indirect instruction will use AR0 unless ARP is changed.
> 5. **Not initializing ACC** — accumulator is a guarded register. Always `LACC #0` before accumulation to avoid garbage sums.
> 6. **Mixing immediate and direct operands** — `ADD 1000h` is direct (memory), `ADDK #1000h` (or `LACC #1000h`) is immediate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Instruction Word Size:** 16 bits; multi-word instructions fetch in successive cycles.
- ✅ **Three Instruction Formats:** Single-word, One-operand, Two-operand.
- ✅ **Major Instruction Groups:** Arithmetic/Logic, Load/Store, Branch/Control, I/O.
- ✅ **Addressing Modes:** Direct (`DP + offset`), Indirect (`ARn via ARP`), Immediate (constant), Memory-Mapped (I/O).
- ✅ **Direct Addressing Equation:** $A_{16} = (DP \times 128) + \text{offset}_{7}$.
- ✅ **Key Loop Constructs:** `RPT N` (hardware repeat), `BANZ` (branch if AR $\neq$ 0), `BCND` (conditional branch on TC/C/OV/EQ/LT/GT).
- ✅ **MAC Family:** `MAC`, `MACD`, `MACA`, `MADS` — all perform multiply-accumulate in one cycle (key for FIR/IIR/convolution).
- ✅ **Storage Registers:** `SACL` (low), `SACH` (high) — must be chosen correctly to preserve precision.
- ✅ **Auxiliary Registers AR0–AR7:** Used in indirect addressing; ARP selects which one is active.
- ✅ **Typical Program Cycle Count:** A single `MAC` inside `RPT` loop = **1 cycle per iteration**; ideal for real-time FIR filters.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision trick:** Memorize the table in Section 2.3 (Addressing Modes) and Section 2.2 (Instruction Categories). Together they account for **~60%** of all marks asked in this Module 4 topic across KTU ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation of Filter Structures: MATLAB Functions and TMS320 Implementation (Demo/Assignment Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET636 - DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of Filter Structures: MATLAB Functions and TMS320 Implementation (Demo/Assignment Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Digital Signal Processing (PEEET636)**, the topic *"Design and Implementation of Filter Structures: MATLAB Functions and TMS320 Implementation"* represents the critical transition phase of any DSP system — moving from a **mathematical filter specification** on paper to a **realizable, executable filter** in either a high-level simulation environment (MATLAB) or on a dedicated **Digital Signal Processor (DSP) hardware platform** (such as the Texas Instruments TMS320C6x family).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** This topic is officially tagged as **"Demo / Assignment Only"**. This means it is **not directly assessed in the End Semester Examination (ESE)**, but it forms the basis for **laboratory assignments, internal evaluations, and viva-voce questions**. However, examiners frequently test the **conceptual awareness** of MATLAB design functions and TMS320 architecture in Part A (3-mark) questions.

**Design and Implementation** of a digital filter involves two distinct stages:

1. **Design Stage:** Computing the filter coefficients $\{b_k\}$ and $\{a_k\}$ (or in the case of FIR filters, $\{h[n]\}$) that satisfy a given frequency-domain specification (passband ripple, stopband attenuation, cutoff frequency, etc.).
2. **Implementation Stage:** Realizing the designed filter using a specific **filter structure** (Direct Form I, Direct Form II, Cascade, Parallel, Lattice, etc.) on the target platform — be it MATLAB for simulation or a TMS320 DSP processor for real-time execution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect** who has designed a beautiful house on paper (this is the **filter design** — you have the dimensions, materials list, and structural drawings). The house plan is useless to a family until someone **actually builds it** with bricks, cement, and labour (this is the **filter implementation** — translating the abstract mathematical design into a working physical/computational system).

- **MATLAB** acts like a **3D virtual simulation tool**. Before laying a single brick, you can "walk through" the house in software, check if the rooms are the right size, see if the windows let in the right amount of light, and find design flaws — all without spending any real money.
- **TMS320 DSP Processor** is the **actual construction site**. It is the real hardware where the house is built, where the mathematical filter is realized using **Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) operations**, memory buffers, and real-time I/O.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The same filter "design" (e.g., a low-pass Butterworth filter with cutoff 2 kHz) can be **implemented** in many different ways — Direct Form, Cascade, Parallel, Transposed — each with different numerical stability, memory requirements, and computational costs. Choosing the *right* structure for the *right* platform is the essence of DSP implementation.

### 1.3 The TMS320C6x Family — Industry Standard for Real-Time DSP

The **TMS320C6x** series (commonly C6713, C6748) is a family of **32-bit floating-point DSP processors** from Texas Instruments. The KTU laboratory curriculum typically uses the **TMS320C6713 DSK (DSP Starter Kit)** or its variants.

| Feature | Specification | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | VLIW (Very Long Instruction Word) | Executes multiple instructions per cycle |
| Clock Speed | Up to **225 MHz** (C6713) | Real-time processing capability |
| Arithmetic | **32-bit IEEE 754** floating point | High precision, avoids overflow issues |
| On-chip Memory | L1P, L1D, L2 (configurable cache/SRAM) | Fast data access |
| Key Peripheral | **McBSP** (Multichannel Buffered Serial Port) | Audio codec interfacing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU:** Always remember that the TMS320C6x uses a **modified Harvard architecture** with separate program and data buses, enabling simultaneous fetch of instruction and data — a critical advantage for the highly parallel MAC operations required in digital filtering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MATLAB Filter Design Functions — The Designer's Toolkit

MATLAB's **Signal Processing Toolbox** provides a rich set of functions for designing digital filters. The most commonly used functions in KTU coursework are organized below:

#### 2.1.1 IIR Filter Design Functions

The four classical analog filter prototypes are mapped to digital filters using the **Bilinear Transformation**. The general syntax in MATLAB follows the pattern:

$$[b, a] = \text{filter\_type}(n, W_n, \text{options})$$

where **$n$** is the filter order, and **$W_n$** is the normalized cutoff frequency in the range $[0, 1]$ (where $1$ corresponds to the Nyquist frequency $\pi$ radians/sample).

**The Four Classical IIR Filter Design Functions:**

- **`butter(n, Wn)`** — Designs a **Butterworth** filter characterized by a **maximally flat magnitude response** in the passband. No ripples in either passband or stopband. Roll-off is monotonic.

- **`cheby1(n, Rp, Wn)`** — Designs a **Chebyshev Type-I** filter with **Rp dB of ripples in the passband** and a **monotonic stopband**. Steeper roll-off than Butterworth for the same order.

- **`cheby2(n, Rs, Wn)`** — Designs a **Chebyshev Type-II** (Inverse Chebyshev) filter with a **monotonic passband** and **Rs dB of ripples in the stopband**. Useful when passband flatness is critical.

- **`ellip(n, Rp, Rs, Wn)`** — Designs a **Cauer (Elliptic)** filter with **Rp dB ripples in the passband AND Rs dB ripples in the stopband**. Provides the **steepest transition** for a given order but has the most nonlinear phase response.

- **`besself(n, Wn)`** — Designs a **Bessel** filter with maximally linear phase response (constant group delay). Less commonly used in KTU but conceptually important.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In audio engineering, **Butterworth** is used for crossovers (maximally flat response). In communication receivers, **Elliptic** filters are chosen when transition bandwidth is extremely tight. In data transmission, **Bessel** filters are used to preserve pulse shapes (linear phase).

#### 2.1.2 FIR Filter Design Functions

- **`fir1(n, Wn, type)`** — Uses the **windowed Fourier series method**. Simple and widely used in KTU labs. Common windows: `'hamming'`, `'hanning'`, `'blackman'`, `'kaiser'`.
- **`fir2(n, f, m)`** — Designs an FIR filter with **arbitrary frequency response** using frequency sampling method.
- **`firls(n, f, m)`** — Designs a **least-squares linear-phase FIR filter**.
- **`firpm(n, f, m)`** — Designs a **Parks-McClellan optimal equiripple FIR filter** (minimizes maximum deviation — same algorithm as in the famous Remez exchange).

#### 2.1.3 Filter Analysis and Implementation Functions

| MATLAB Function | Purpose | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `freqz(b, a, N, Fs)` | Computes the **frequency response** $H(e^{j\omega})$ | Most common verification tool |
| `grpdelay(b, a, N)` | Computes **group delay** (linear phase check) | Critical for FIR design validation |
| `filter(b, a, x)` | Implements the filter using **Direct Form II** transposed | Real-time filtering on vectors |
| `fftfilt(b, x)` | Implements an **FIR filter using FFT-based overlap-add** | Efficient for long FIR filters |
| `zplane(b, a)` | Plots **pole-zero diagram** | Visual stability check |
| `impz(b, a, N)` | Computes **impulse response** | Time-domain verification |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Filter Difference Equation (General IIR)** | $\displaystyle y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k]$ | Core computation performed every sample period |
| **FIR Filter Equation** (no feedback) | $\displaystyle y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} h[k]\, x[n-k]$ | Pure convolution — always stable |
| **Transfer Function** | $\displaystyle H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | The "DNA" of the filter in the z-domain |
| **Bilinear Transform Mapping** | $\displaystyle s = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | Maps analog $s$-plane poles to digital $z$-plane |
| **Number of Multiplications per sample (Direct Form I, order $N$)** | $\text{MULs} = N + N + 1 = 2N+1$ | Computational cost metric for TMS320 |
| **Number of Memory Locations (Direct Form II)** | $\text{MEM} = \max(M, N)$ | Storage requirement for delay line buffers |
| **Normalized Frequency to Hz conversion** | $F_{\text{Hz}} = \dfrac{W_n \cdot F_s}{2}$ | Convert MATLAB $W_n$ to physical Hz |
| **Group Delay (Linear Phase FIR)** | $\tau_g = \dfrac{N-1}{2} \text{ samples}$ | Symmetric FIR of length $N$ has constant delay |
| **Stability Condition (LCCDE / IIR)** | All poles $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for $\forall k$ | Inside the unit circle in the $z$-plane |
| **MAC Operations (FIR of order $N$)** | $\text{MAC per output} = N + 1$ | The fundamental DSP operation |

### 2.3 TMS320 Implementation — Why a DSP Processor and Not a General Microprocessor?

The fundamental question KTU examiners love to ask: **"Why do we need a specialized DSP processor like TMS320 when we have general-purpose CPUs?"**

**The Three Critical Reasons:**

1. **Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) in a Single Cycle:** The TMS320C6x contains dedicated **hardware MAC units**. A general-purpose microprocessor requires multiple cycles to fetch, multiply, add, and store. The MAC operation $y[n] = y[n] + b[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ is the **heart** of every FIR/IIR filter — DSPs do this in **one clock cycle**.

2. **Harvard Architecture with Multiple Memory Buses:** Separate program and data memory buses allow **simultaneous instruction fetch and data access**. The TMS320C6x has two data paths (A and B), each with its own register file and 4 functional units (.L, .S, .M, .D), giving theoretical peak of **8 instructions per cycle** (VLIW).

3. **Zero-Overhead Looping and Circular Addressing:** DSPs have **hardware loop buffers** that allow tight inner loops (typical in filtering) to execute **without branch penalties**. **Circular addressing** automatically wraps pointer increments around buffer boundaries — essential for delay-line implementations of filters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When you implement an FIR filter on TMS320, the code essentially becomes a tight loop that performs MAC on circular buffers. This is why the order of operations and memory layout directly affects **real-time performance** — a poorly structured implementation may not meet the sampling rate deadline.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the Memory and Computation Budget for FIR Filter on TMS320

Consider a real-world KTU assignment scenario: **Design and implement a 64th-order low-pass FIR filter on the TMS320C6713 DSK to process audio sampled at 8 kHz.**

#### Step 1: Formulate the FIR Filter Equation

The FIR filter is a pure convolution of the input $x[n]$ with the impulse response $h[k]$:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$

For a **64th-order** filter, $M = 64$, meaning we have **65 coefficients** (indices $0$ through $64$).

#### Step 2: Count Multiplications and Additions per Output Sample

The computational load is measured in **MACs (Multiply-Accumulates) per output sample**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Multiplications per output} &= M + 1 = 64 + 1 = 65 \text{ MULs} \\
\text{Additions per output} &= M = 64 \text{ ADDs} \\
\text{Total MACs per output} &= 65
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Estimate the Real-Time Computational Load

Given the sampling rate $F_s = 8\,\text{kHz}$, the time budget available for processing **one sample** is:

$$
T_{\text{sample}} = \frac{1}{F_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125\,\mu\text{s}
$$

The total number of MACs per second required:

$$
\text{MACs/sec} = 65 \times 8000 = 520{,}000\,\text{MACs/sec} = 0.52\,\text{MMACS}
$$

#### Step 4: Check Against TMS320C6713 Capability

The TMS320C6713 runs at a clock speed of $f_{\text{clk}} = 225\,\text{MHz}$. With its dual datapath architecture, the peak MAC throughput is:

$$
